text_: I work in the fashion industry and having multiple screens when I am working from home is a must.  I got the smaller version but quickly upgraded for work purposes.  Easy set up and comes with everything you need, including a nice case with the larger one.  I definitely recommend getting the support though, because the ports are a little funky at times.
You will be surprised for the amount of brightness it has and good viewing angles for a TN panel.

The screen itself is crisp and colorful, if a bit dim, and I cannot detect a single dead pixel. The monitor is incredibly light as well, a welcome change from the brick I was using before.

The controls are as others have described, difficult to use because of the awkward placement. In particular the lack of readily available volume control is a ridiculous oversight.

With the controls in the base, I dont see how this monitor can be mounted, something that I did not read anywhere in the description or in other reviews. With my current desk setup it is useable, but ony because I am short. Anyone over 5'9" will be looking over the top of the monitor without placing it on something else to heighten it.

I am not planning on sending it back, but only because I got crap in the office for ordering a new monitor already and it would be embarrassing to admit this one was a mistake. Just being honest.

For less than $160 this screen is OUTSTANDING. UI is a little underwhelming but at $160 I can't complain. Out of the box it will only display 60hz, you will need to make sure your PC is sending 144hz signal to this screen and that you have your menu settings set correctly. HIGHLY recommend using a Display Port cable (included) for this reason, I was able to successfully use an HDMI cable as well but only up to 120hz, this was limited by the PC not the screen though so milage may vary.

I have one at work (I'm a graphic and web designer for an Ad agency) and now one at home and love them both. Both were purchased here at Amazon in August '08. The one at work was purchased to replace a 2004 model 23" Cinema HD (same model, just older) one that was stolen. That one had an obvious pink cast to it that was hard to correct even with calibration, but both of the new ones are beautiful and quite color "neutral." The 1920x1200 feels like a lot of room to work but not "too much" ...if that's even possible. If you can afford it, go for it.

====================================== Update ======================================

It took awhile, but WIMAXIT finally contacted me about the issue that I had encountered with their Portable Monitor 15.6 inch USB C HDMI Monitor. (See my original review below.) It took several emails and a couple of back and forth emails, but WIMAXIT agreed to send me a replacement monitor.

It turned out that that monitor that I had been sent initially had defective ports. I didn't notice this at first, but in comparing the two monitors it was clear that the monitor was defective. The HDMI port was damaged and the USB-C port simply didn't work.

I don't know if the monitor was damaged in manufacturing or whether I was sent a return. (It happens.) It really doesn't matter because when I connected the replacement monitor via HDMI to my PC, it immediately worked. I was able to connect my two monitors to the dual HDMI ports on my PC. This is what I had needed all along. Therefore I am raising my rating to four stars and rescinding my reluctance to recommend the WIMAXIT Portable Monitor 15.6inch USB C HDMI Monitor.

**************************************** Original Review *******************************

I am reluctantly giving the WIMAXIT Portable Monitor 15.6inch USB C HDMI Monitor a one-star review. The monitor that I received had a serious issue and WIMAXIT support let me down. Therefore, although this looks like it could have been a very nice monitor, I am unable to recommend it.

The WIMAXIT Portable Monitor has the ability to use either an HDMI connection or a USB 3 connection via Type C cable. The USB 3 connection was of particular interest to me as I need to have dual monitors connected to my PC and the HDMI port on the PC is taken by the first monitor. Therefore, via the PC’s USB 3 port, the WIMAXIT Portable Monitor was to be used as a second monitor. However, despite everything that I tried, I could not get the WIMAXIT Portable Monitor go display anything including the Windows desktop. It would start up, however, and display the WIMAXIT logo. The controls on the side worked and I could select either AUTO or the Type C input. Past that, the monitor would go black.

I contacted WIMAXIT Support who responded saying that the PC needed to support USB 3.1. That made sense so I tried a second PC (an Asus) that I know supports USB 3.1. When I plugged the monitor in to the Asus, Windows 10 Pro issued a "The Last USB Device Malfunctioned" error. I tried different USB 3.1 ports on the PC, swapped in another USB Type C cable. I even made sure that Windows 10 Pro was updated. But nothing helped.

Then I borrowed another USB 3 monitor and plugged the monitor in via the same USB Type C cable. The borrowed monitor worked perfectly on both computers. From this I assume that the WIMAXIT Portable Monitor 15.6inch USB C HDMI Monitor is defective. I contacted WIMAXIT Support again. Nothing. I tried a third time. Again nothing. So I am stuck with a non-working monitor and no support.

Because I have a MSDN Subscription I was able to download the Windows 8 RTM that will ship on Oct 26, 2012 now in August. I wanted to have a touch screen for my desktop PC, so I can use all the Windows 8 features the way they are supposed to be. I got this monitor from another local retailer, and even though I was a bit worried first, as soon as I hooked it to my PC (Acer), it started working flawlessly. It is very responsive like any good tablet or smartphone. You need to learn the gestures for Windows 8 but they work easily. Only minor thing is the monitor has a bevel, so your finger must touch either the right or left of the screen inside of the bevel and swipe quickly from right to left to open the Charms bar. And from left to right to switch apps, or left to right to left quickly to open the application bar. After two or three minutes you get it, and then it is very easy. Without the bevel as a tablet it would be even simpler, but anyway this monitor is very responsive. It looks like the RTM version took in consideration that some monitors have a bevel (I mean frame) that is a bit higher than the actual screen surface, so there is an automatic offset to compensate. Good job Microsoft.

All the Windows 8 gestures work (charms, app bar, app snap, app close, pinch zoom-in, zoom-out and others). This is a 2 point multi-touch monitor, like most available monitors at the moment. Windows 8 supports 4, 5 or 10 multi-touch points, but with 2 I've been able to do everything so far.

It works flawlessly, no driver needs to be installed, as soon as it is connected it starts working. Creates a fresh, great experience for the desktop user. So if you need a good but not so expensive touch screen monitor, this is a great choice. Highly recommended (5 stars), if you need one now, this is it

The replacement crapped out on me seven months later with minimal wear and tear. Literally three months out of those seven, it was powered off or in sleep mode. The screen flickered and was heavily lined. I was required to pay to ship the defective unit back to their California headquarters so that I could get a replacement monitor, so I essentially had to pay for my monitor a second time.

Given the track record of unreliability and issues that others have reported, I do not recommend this monitor. It's broken twice within the warranty period, which suggests to me that it will break a third time within the warranty period.

I bought this monitor for my Mac Mini after reading one reviewer, a Mac user, who gave a good rating with positive experience. For me, the experience was mixed with major disappointments:

Pros:
1. Decent price. As compared to a $899 24" LED Apple Display, this monitor is a steal, at least it looks like so.
2. Easy set up. The base is flimsy, but easy to be connected to the display. The monitor itself is basically a plug-n-play without extra effort if you are satisfied with the default setting.
3. Good delivery time. The monitor arrived less than a week from the day I ordered.

Cons:
1. With its highest resolution (and the only resolution that makes sense without having to distort the proper height/width ratio), i.e. 1900x1024, the display was abysmal, especially the texts were pixelated (when sharpened), or blurry (when softened) to a point that my eyes got tired after 10 minutes looking at it. I have tried different settings with the monitor, with or without the "Magic Color", or "Magic Brightness", with no satisfaction. This is my major complaint.

2. The CD that came with the monitor has no Mac version for driver, etc. So it is essentially useless, except that you could read the user's guide.

3. The power button is rather inconveniently located at the bottom of right side of the monitor. And it needs some effort to push to take effect. The blue light beneath the monitor would keep flashing when there is no incoming signal. In the dark, it could be rather annoying.

4. I am not sure if it is because mine is connecting to a Mac, but the "Auto Ajudgement" button doesn't not function.

In all, if you are planning to get a monitor for your Mac laptop or desktop, look elsewhere.

Bought a Samsung 28" S271HL U28E590D 4K UHD Monitor for $435 on cyber Monday in 2015.  It now goes for around $250.  It's an amazingly clear 1ms monitor that I still love.  Only downside is the 60hz refresh rate.  It bottlenecks my rtx 2080ti i9-9900k rig on fps.  Finally broke down and bought the Dell S2719DGF.  It's a 1440p 1ms 144hz butter smooth monitor.  It can be over clocked to 155hz with a dvi cable, but not worth doing unless you like tracers/ghosting for only 7% faster framerate.  The monitor is not quite as crystal clear or quite as bright as the Samsung, but had I never owned the Samsung, I'd be gushing over the picture quality.  It really is an excellent gaming monitor, and I couldn't imagine why anyone not getting paid to game could want to spend more than $300 for a different one.  I mounted mine on the wall, but debated using the stand instead, because it is a very nice looking stand.  I just don't have a very large desk for my gaming pc like I do for the office one which is where the Samsung is now at.  Bottom line, if your looking for the best bang for the buck 1440p monitor, this is it.

A Solid WIN but with only the Right Specs.
This Monitor is very high spec
Resolution is 3440X1440 with G Sync and 120 htz refresh.
It's a very bright display has to be around 450 nits Max brightness. It's so bright I use only Game 2 mode in RPG setting I can't use anything brighter trust me it's rediculously bright. You will have to turn down settings in the game your playing if too bright.
Ok so with that out of the way this monitor is fully Capable of running AAA titles at Ultra settings or HIGH if you have the right hardware to support it. What does that mean ?
Well for starters I am running for my SPEC: I9900k 8 Core Up to
(5.2 gtz OC) MSI Trident X Plus with 32GB RAM
RTX 2080ti Ventus DUO by MSI
PSU: Gold Standard 750
3 Pcie NVME SSD's
You get the point.
So I can see high frame rates on most titles either RPG or FPS in HIGH settings or ULTRA but that's not hitting full refresh of 120htz in ULTRA nope. HIGH settings or even medium on MOST titles I am seeing refresh rate being hit at that MARK. It really just depends on the game and type of game you play.

The OSD: is so easy to use the navigation menu was very thought out and I have no issues. Don't forget to change your FACTORY refresh from 60htz to 120htz in Nvidia control panel or this monitor would be useless in that regard.
I happen to like the Alien FX lighting and how simple it is to customize in the OSD menu. I typically leave it on breathing or Spectrum to cycle through the colors to match my Logitech G560 speakers.
I really reccomend sitting further away from this monitor as opposed to being close up. The colors are very bright and vibrant. Literally no washed up colors no back light bleed really at all and if it's there just a little bit on this IPS screen you won't notice it at least not my unit.

Assembly and set up: Ummm took like maybe 2 mins to set up and get running. It was easy as cake. No tools of any kind required just use the supplied Wing BOLT and your good to go.
The monitor really isn't that heavy either. It's very solid and doesn't wobble doesn't move around when slightly touching it.
It's also adjustable so thats a plus swivels and tilts. Is it necessary for swivel ? No not really this thing is huge I leave it set in 1 motion only.

Final thoughts: You guys I really have nothing negative to say about this monitor really at all. This monitor is truely epic in every way and when it was in sale for $1029 I grabbed it. Shipping took literally 2 days than there it was on my door step. Keep in mind the BOX is huge ok. The packaging is second to none and the directions are clear as day when removing the monitor from it's old home (The BOX)
I highly recommend this amazing piece of hardware to add to any high spec set up...
Additional thoughts: Please have a high spec machine at least RTX 2070 Super or above to run this beast at it's native resolution. If not than I wouldn't see a use for it and to look elsewhere.
Hope this review helps you in your purchase decision as I am extremely impressed and hope you will be to when it arrives on your doorstep.

I've owned this monitor for about six months. Before buying, I researched and examined almost every common monitor available and I choose the FDP2485W.

First, a reason why you should buy a 24" monitor. Any monitor (other than Apple's) that's smaller than 24" will have less pixels. The number of pixels increases from 19 inch to 20", 23" to 24", and 29" to 30". This is why a 19" monitor is so much less than a 20" monitor and a 23" monitor is a lot less than a 24" monitor. Buying a 24" monitor gives you 1920 x 1200 pixels. It means you can view two full pages side by side. Once you have, you will never go back to a smaller monitor.

OK, why this HP monitor. Instead of repeating mostly what others have said, I'll state a positive feature that others have likely overlooked. This monitor uses S-PVA LCD technology. That means the picture brightness does not fade/change from any angle. Most monitors (including other Gateway 24" monitors) use a cheaper technology. Every time you lean in you chair or someone standing looks at the monitor - it will likely be darker/lighter. Most notebook computers use the cheaper TFT technology. How many times do you adjust the angle of your notebook screen?

This monitor is perfectly bright, no matter what angle. Few monitors have this - except the top of line Dell and Apple monitors. At the price on Amazon, you're getting a great monitor.

So I wrote a 5 star review in May for my first monitor, I love it and was a great purchase. I loved it so much I bought a second one to match in July. However, in the photo attached you can see that the panels do not match. The new monitor on the right has a yellow tint to it. I did a factory reset on both and matched the settings but no solution. I called amazon support and they sent me a replacement. The replacement still has the tint and I think I figured out why.

In the color adjust settings, you have options to pick the “color temp” of the screen. The options are Custom, Warm, Medium, and Cool. However, if I try to switch from custom to Warm, the tint does not change. I’m thinking the new monitors being manufactured have this defect.

What is funny is that my first monitor has the same issue but the opposite: when I try to switch from custom to warm the color temp does not change. So it’s literally impossible for me to get these monitors to match.

UPDATE 12/26/21: Changed review from 3 stars to 1. Bought this in January last year and thought this monitor was pretty nice for the price it was. I decided to run a dead/stuck pixel test now since I've been seeing what I thought to be dead pixels on a specific part of my screen. It turns out that my monitor is littered with dead pixels that I never noticed until now. Since it's a 2K monitor, the pixels are relatively small and hard to notice when they're dead. It's not a huge issue, but now I'll be looking into a new monitor. I'm updating this because even though I can deal with it for the time being, I know someone out there will hate this with a passion and because it's an understatement to call what I see unacceptable. I won't be bothering with the 3 year warranty as was advertised since everyone else says their warranty is a different time and that they don't fulfill their promises. I'll leave my original review below, but I now would greatly advise that you avoid getting AOC products.

TL;DR - An insane amount of dead/stuck pixels; Don't support AOC or their products.

I've been wanting an LCD flat-screen monitor for some time, but I kept putting off the purchase since they're so expensive. But a few weeks ago I decided to get serious and purchase one for my Windows98 system. I looked at the reviews on CNET and Amazon, etc., and at first I decided to purchase a VP151. Then I learned about the new 150m. It was a difficult choice, but I eventually purchased the 150m; so far, I'm delighted with it!
Installation wasn't completely smooth, since I ran into the usual so-called "plug-and-play" problems. Despite years of experience with this sort of thing, I always find that reality never quite matches either the printed instructions (in this case, half a page from the large, multi-lingual manual) or the Windows98 screen instructions. This was slightly more annoying in the case of installing a monitor, since a monitor is REALLY important, to say the least!
Nevertheless, once I rebooted a few times and tinkered around with reported-to-be-missing files, eventually I got things working. From this point on, everything seems great.
The display is BRIGHT (perhaps even too bright -- I've taken to reducing the intensity using a setting in my video card driver) and, as advertised, viewable from quite a large range of angles. The unit is reasonably attractive, and seems more-or-less mechanically solid.
I installed the included "PerfectPortrait" software; this allows me to easily rotate the screen display 90 degrees to see full-page display. "Full screen" might not be quite the story if you take into account the screen real estate taken up by your application's menu bars, etc. With Microsoft Publisher, for example, I find that I need to zoom out to the 90% setting in order to actually see the entire page. By some stroke of luck, though, this 90% setting seems to render the page almost exactly to scale.
The display seems a bit less mechanically stable in the rotated configuration; sometimes it lands at something more like 88 degrees instead of 90. But it returns to the horizontal position quite accurately, so this is OK with me.
The built-in speakers are fine for general use. I compared them with somewhat larger stand-alone speakers that had come with my Sony Vaio 526, and I decided I could live with the built-in speakers.
Anyway, I've been up and running for about two days so far, and I'm quite happy with the VP150m.

I'm very impressed by this monitor. I'm a freelance editor who works mostly in Word, Acrobat Pro, Excel, and PowerPoint. I need the vertical height of a 5x4 monitor, so I ordered this one when my old Samsung 940BX died. The backlighting really makes everything pop, the display is crystal clear (1280x1024), and the color calibration is good right out of the box. All I needed to do was scale back the brightness. I hate ClearType and font smoothing so I get rid of them in all programs, but with my old monitor the type was often very pixelated. On this monitor, the type is crisp but the edges aren't so jagged. My eyes are happy, and so am I.

I paid $159.99 with free shipping elsewhere.

This portable monitor is definitely different than other monitors on the market. I have used 3 other monitors but I think I will continue to come back to this monitor. I use this monitor with a 14" HP Laptop so I ordered the 14" to match the screen sizes and helps a little more with portability. I work as an engineer, working on excel sheets, drafting, and reviewing multiple plan sets, so working while traveling, I feel like I need a portable monitor to have a second screen to look references while working.

I REALLY REALLY like the stand, it's built in and sturdy enough, but the major thing is that it doesn't take up too much space. I travel for work a lot and when setting my laptop/portable monitor on a small coffee table, the base on this one works perfect for a smaller table footprint. All the other monitors I have tested come with the Folio style stand which always bugs me because how unstable they are and how easily they can be knocked over. There have been so many times where the cable that's connected to these "Folio" style stand, knocks the monitor over. This is not the case here.

The stand also has USB-C on both sides of the monitor. This is something I didn't realize was super important to me, but with this small feature, I am able to carry a 1 foot USB-C to USB-C cable, because I don't need a long cable to wrap around the laptop or the monitor to connect. This just helps take up way less clutter on a small table, looks less messy when at a like a Starbucks.

The screen brightness is just fine. My HP Laptop maxes out to about 300 nits. The max for this monitor is around 300 nits as well. This is perfectly fine since I don't have mismatching monitor brightness. If you were to use this with a MacBook (they have higher brightness) this monitor would not look that good beside it. But for around $120, it doesn't really matter in my case.

The colors are washed out when comparing to a better screen, like a MacBook again. It doesn't really bother me for my type of work since I'm mainly working on excel sheets, or high contrast drawings that don't affect my quality of work. I wouldn't recommend this for someone looking for color accuracy.

One thing to note, you do have to contact the store to receive a travel case. At the moment, my bag has a dual compartment that can safely store the monitor, but do note that you will be traveling with the screen exposed and I would recommend something to protect it while traveling.

Overall, I would definitely recommend this monitor for someone who is traveling, and just needs a second screen for reference material. It feels super light and I really like the stand. I didn't weigh the monitor but I would say it's less than 2 pounds, it's probably just as light as an iPhone. This monitor would also work for someone who games at home and wants discord or some other monitoring software on a smaller screen. I plan on using it for this purpose as well.

This monitor measures 14 1/2 inches wide, 8 7/8 inches tall, and 3/8 inch thick. It weights 2 1/2 lbs. This monitor comes with a magnetic cover/case/stand that is similar to ones that I have seen with iPads. It utilizes the magnetics of the cover to set the monitor up as an angled stand. It works quite well, saving weight in having a built in or separate stand. It is dual functioning. The matte screen is quite clear when I used it with the USB-C male connector on both ends. This allows connectivity without having to use a wall plug or other power source. There is a trackwheel on the side that controls volume as well as selections on the menu. There are two USB-C female outlets on the other side, as well as mini HDMI. There is a USB-C cable with the males on both ends, a cable with standard HDMI on one end, and mini HDMI on the other. There is a wall adapter with regular USB outlet, and a cable with regular USB on on end and USB-C on the other. The kit comes pretty much complete with cables and adapters that are needed. This monitor also has a speaker on each side. I wouldn’t call it high performance sound by any stretch, but it had much more treble than my muffled speakers on my laptop, so it’s adequate. All things considered, this reasonably light, thin, sleek monitor .

First off, the product name is a lie, this monitor is either 1280x768 (according to Windows) or 1280x800 (according to the monitor itself), neither of which are 1080p. This isn't an issue for me because when I ordered such a small monitor I didn't expect it to be FHD, but some of you might be upset to find it lower res than advertised.

As for the image quality, it's solid. 1280x768 is nice and crisp for a monitor this size, and the color reproduction is comparable to my HDR monitor at a glance(though I highly doubt this little screen supports HDR). It's nice and bright, though as far as I've been able to tell there is no way to adjust the backlight's brightness, only the gamma.

Speaking of which, the monitor's settings menu is a pain to navigate, but if you've ever dealt with the settings on an older TV then you should be able to figure it out. The remote that comes with the product does help, but then you run into the biggest hurdle with the remote: The IR sensor/receiver on the monitor itself is located on the side, behind the screen's bezel. This means that if you want to use the remote, you either need to position the remote around the monitor's right side, or carefully position a mirror behind the screen in order for the sensor to pick up the remote.

I'm not going to talk about sound quality because honestly I never use the built-in speaker on computer monitors. From the little bit I heard when setting it up, it's perfectly serviceable for listening to speech or dialogue, but you probably don't want to be using it for music.

Unfortunately, my biggest issue with the monitor, and the reason I'm returning it, is the faulty electrics. The screen sporadically would turn off and back on again as I used it, sometimes even being accompanied by a loud popping sound that I can only imagine is internal sparking. I don't know if my unit is faulty or if it's a result of the strange electrical setup of my current home, but none of my other devices on the same surge protector have ever suffered from this and I'm not interested in going through the work of troubleshooting or trying out a replacement.

Spend 300$ on a monitor rated for 144hz, and they don't even include a 144hz capable cable. Only item included was a standard HDMI, not a 1.3a, so you'll have to buy a dual link dvi or a better HDMI when you buy this or you'll be stick at 60hz. Pretty frustrating.

Like many other people I cannot tell the difference between how fast a refresh rate is or certain minute color differences or the quality of pixels...  All I know is that it looks great and performs well.  I had to take of 1 star though for the following reason:  When we bought this monitor we also bought a new Dell Inspiron 3668 tower computer.  In the set up it has three cables to plug in the back of the monitor; 1) power, 2) DP cable and 3) miniDP cable, it worked just fine, turns out we had to return the computer due to other issue and bought another Dell 3668 computer.  This time when I tried to plug the cords from the monitor into the computer there were no DP ports tp plug into.  So I called Dell and asked why the Dell monitor cannot be used with the Dell computer and was told the first computer must have had a graphics card with the proper ports and the second computer had a lesser grade cheaper graphics card.  We were told to exchange it for another computer with a better graphics card.  REALLY????  Yeah, it turns out not all Dell monitors are compatible with all Dell computers!!!  No adapters provided?, NOPE.  So we sent back computer 2 for computer 3 with the upgraded graphics card and it works.  I also found that an HDMI cable will work bypassing the graphics card (not included).  But then what is the point of the graphics card?  Dell really should explain that this monitor requires graphic cards on your computer that supports DP ports.

If you're like me and just need a solid monitor to work on then you'll love this product for the price. My only complaint is that the speaker is low quality but I'm not trying to rock out or wake the neighbors.

After having issues with other portable monitors and connectivity with a macbook pro, I decided to give this monitor a try. I have only had it for a few days, but I can already tell that the monitor feels higher quality and the cables that come with the monitor seem "studier" with a better connection.

Perfect for work from home or traveling with your office set up.

A True, Step-Up

Investing in this monitor make perfect sense. Especially when I'm creating 140mp multi-row, nodal rail panoramas as well as editing 4k video, there does not exist a display that 1) accommodates the Adobe RGB color space and 2) has a pretty reasonable price point compared to any other offerings. For those that are running a Mac Pro (late 2013) and are having issues with getting your resolution right, check to make sure you are connecting your display(s) to the appropriate Thunderbolt busses.

http://support.apple.com/en-us/HT202801

Just don't attach your displays to the same bus and you'll be fine. I'm running 3x displays via thunderbolt- to- display adapter cables from Cable Matters. I couldn't use the included cable because it wasn't long enough. For $10 a cable and fantastic customer service, it's a great investment! Also, I am running the full, 4096x 2160 @ 50hz with no problems. If I need to use the display for purposes not relating to proofing 4k videos or high resolution images, I'm fine switching to a scaled resolution one notch down (3008 x 1586)- looks comparable to a 24" display running 1920x1200.

Why not 5 stars?
The instructions aren't the best. The driver installation disc is horrible, and LG's customer support is beyond bad. They don't even have anyone competent with Macs- I got grilled by their support rep accusing me of running emulated Windows (he didn't know what Bootcamp was) and put me on hold 2-3 times. I ended up figuring things out while on hold doing my own research. You can't even find the model number listing under their support site to download the latest drivers- the product on their support site doesn't exist. As for Windows, I can live with 2 displays (Windows 8 seems to barf when trying to run 3 displays after initiating rebooting via Bootcamp). I only use Windows for audio and slideshow programs that don't have a version for the Mac, so no big deal. As for all of the other Windows users- good luck- I switched to the Mac OS because it is simply a leaner, more efficient OS with way less overhead.

This display rocks for Mac users. It's my 1st LG Purchase, and I couldn't be happier! Again, just make sure you know your Thunderbolt bus mapping and consider investing in a higher grade cable for $10.

A few years ago I owned a BenQ "Full HD" monitor that I got for ~$450 (then low end for HD) and I figured that by now I'd get a similar image from this. When I got this display I was slightly disappointed, the image on this isn't as clear and the colors seemed off and kinda washed out, comparatively. After doing a couple Windows display calibrations and tweaking the displays settings I was a bit happier, but it's colors are still not as sharp as something more then twice the price. After about a day of using it I got used to its "look" and I can say it is totally capable of displaying amazing visuals in games, movies, etc. It's a great monitor for the price, and I doubt you'll have any issue with the image unless you're a hardcore gamer with a $3000 rig. I have to say though the speakers are crap; on par with laptop speakers or the speaker on your phone or iPod. I use headphones 90% of the time and have windows routing sound out my computer speakers, as many of you probably do, so this is a non issue for me, honestly I didn't even know it had them when I ordered it, I never use built in speakers; it's not a buying point for me, I'm just happy they are not visible. I have had one experience with them I can share. Last night I used it to watch a movie off my laptop and chose to use the built in speakers as they are somewhat louder then what my 11" laptop can output, however the extra volume gains you less clarity in dialog to the point where we had to sit ~3 ft away from it in order to understand what was being said. There was a distinct cutoff point around there where if we schootched our chairs back it would become incomprehensible, and the louder the volume is up the worse this effect got. It wouldn't be so bad with loud music, but with voice it is definitely an issue if you plan to use the built in speakers. Built in speakers are always crap, so this is to be expected. If you were at a desk in a quiet room watching youtube or listening to Windows make its alert sounds I doubt you'd have an issue.

Nit picks:
-No DisplayPort (if you don't know: it's like an HDMI alternative)
-No audio out/headphone jack
-Stand only tilts
-Menus are not intuitive to navigate
-Power button is orange and I think it clashes with the slick glossy black bezel, it is SUPER easy to find though.

TL;DR
-I would choose this monitor for a sub ~$1500, mid range gaming rig, as response times are fast and while I found the colors slightly "muddy" while viewing hi-rez landscape photos I use for wallpaper the colors are quite vibrant and well suited to gaming, producing fantastical visuals free from graphical anomalies (ie tearing).
-I would also choose this to do homework, word processing, email, spreadsheets, and web surfing on; it is cheap (although there are cheaper, if all you are doing is surfing and office stuff pick up a 1080p $110 acer if you can) simply because of the screen space and low price point. Having built in speakers also means you won't have to spend $18 on USB speakers to hear the funny cats on youtube or windows boot up.
-I would also choose this if I was planning to use my computer as my movie/TV watching device, as long as I got a $50-$100 desktop sound system to accompany it.
-I would not choose this monitor if my main use was to edit photos, video, etc or utilize the built in speakers expecting high quality audio you'd expect accompanying the "Full HD" viewing experience. You're going to have to buy something expensive for all of that.

People tend to only write reviews when they hate a product or love it. I felt that I should write this review, because some of the negative reviews are the result of a failure to understand the panel type.

Misconception:
This IS NOT an OLED monitor. If you are expecting that sort of image you will be disappointed. It is mini-LED. Therefore, it utilizes local dimming zones as opposed to per pixel illumination. This is why there is ghosting (the halo of light around the mouse when the screen is black for example). There is no avoiding this on a panel of this sort. Those who complain about it simply do not understand that on an HDR mini-LED display this will always occur.

Pros:

Great color accuracy. Very high HDR brightness and white point.

This is the best gaming HDR 4k gaming monitor available at the moment. OLED monitors with G-Sync Ultimate are simply not available yet, though it looks as though they will start appearing this year.

Cons:

Price: There is no reason to spend anywhere near this amount of money on a display that will be bested within the year (and for a much lower price point in all likelihood).

Instability: This may just be an issue with my unit, but there are times that the monitor simply goes black and then comes back on (particularly when using Photoshop). The monitor control panel does not offer enough in the way of options, and the features it does offer are finicky at best (i.e. the FPS display on the front of the display underneath the panel).

I have not noticed anything in the way of atypical fan noise, so perhaps this is an issue with only some units... or some users are more sensitive to such noise than I.

Conclusion:

Do not buy this monitor, but not for the reasons that most of the negative reviewers say. It is a great monitor, but it will be obsolete as 4k OLED displays with G-Sync (not G-Sync compatible!) release this year. It is also ludicrously priced, even if it were to be positioned to be the best monitor for years to come.

Update after playing with the color balance, the Philips white isn't as clean as the Dell especially with the brightness turned down. I keep both monitors on the minimal brightness to avoid eye fatigue. The Dell has a cleaner white color, but also dosent process the gray scales as well as the Philips. I've attached some pictures where you can see how the dell doesn't pick up the last gray scale where as the Philips gets them all. I like the dell better for the clean white, but appreciate the Philips abilities to process. Both monitors have +/-, and not fair to compare directly, however the Dell version of a 32" 2k curved monitor is almost double in price. Photos of a monitor never look great due to light interference, but you can see the Dell missing the last gray bar before full white, whereas the Philips gets them all. The Philips is superior in color, especially when brightness is turned up.

Update; Both monitors togeather are amazing. 4+ft end to end. Amazing color. Everything is better. So happy with the purchase.

Benefits:
-Freesync range of 48-144hz (when Freesync is set to Ultimate) means that you can crank up the graphics and not lose adaptive sync. For example, playing Assassin's Creed Odyssey with a Vega Frontier Edition Liquid which can churn out Very High 1440p frames at about 60-70fps but it's still smooth when it drops into the low 50s or even high 40s. With regular Freesync, this wasn't possible. Also note that you won't get this Freesync range using HDMI, but then why would you do that when DisplayPort is far superior?
-Manufaturer's lie about response times. However, this one really does appear to be a true 1ms response time. So you have TN-like refresh and response, with IPS-like color and viewing angle. VA is alright in my book!
-1800R curve seems just about right to me.

Cons:
-Mine has a stuck pixel in the bottom left, which shows blue when it should turn black. It's only noticeable on a solid black screen, so I'm really not concerned about it. At $600, I probably would be, but for $439 I'm happy.
-The stand is wobbly. This is only noticeable when you bump the screen, but still. The other problem with the stand is that the foot goes pretty far forward, which is necessitated by physics and the 21-pound weight. Perhap's I'm overly picky having come from a rock-solid monitor arm.

After having an AOC CRT for ~10 years, giving it to my grandmother to use for another ~5 years, during which time it always functioned perfectly and was only replaced because she bought a new computer that came with an LCD, I didn't hesitate in the least when I purchased this AOC portable monitor a few years ago. The first one lived up to my expectations and worked flawlessly. It would still be here if I hadn't regrettably trampled it and smashed the screen a couple months ago >.<

I didn't hesitate in purchasing this again, as the first one was exactly what I wanted and needed at the right price point for occasional travel and "work from bed" use. The new one still works well, BUT the slightest movement causes it to shut off, which is a huge drawback when working from my bed, or even bumping it the slightest bit on a hard surface (this is definitely a problem when flipping the screen orientation as well).

Now this may be as minor an issue as a fault in the USB cable that came with it, so I will update this review if the issue resolves when I try a new one. But given that in 20+ years of using them, I've never had a standard Type A USB cable malfunction out of the box, I'm not hopeful that this is the problem. It could also be an issue just with mine, as I haven't looked at other reviews yet to search for that problem.

So for now, I would say this is still a good product IF the sensitive connection is not a recurring issue with the monitor itself. Purchase with caution and test it out immediately so you can return if it has this problem.

I bought this screen because it's extremely portable and supports hdr. Wasn't sure what to expect, but most of the other reviews were positive so I gave it a chance. It is a true 1080p screen and does support hdr input. The manual gives a few specs such as it supports up to 16.7 million colors (8-bit panel so can't really reproduce HDR colors like a true 10-bit panel) and consumes a maximum of 10W as well as having a 8msTYP response time. The pixel density is insane at almost 190 ppi. I used the lagom test patterns on the web from my ps4 to calibrate the picture. The best settings I found were contrast 18, black level 57, dcr off, sharpness 2, color temp sRGB, and setting the hdr to auto. Backlight I found comfortable anywhere from 20 to 30. The only downside to the screen are the speakers. Very small and put behind the monitor instead of on the front which would have made more sense to me, but i guess that's what headphones are for. All in all its a great portable screen that holds firm to my pro and I am honestly very pleased with it initially. Also turn OD on. This is the overdrive function that reduces ghosting which is a world of difference when you play or watch anything with fast motion.

Update: Did some testing with my pc using an i1 Display Pro and display cal then went back to re-calibrate contrast and black levels after colors were properly set. Here are my findings:
Brightness: 44 (120 cd/m2)
Contrast: 11 (would be 17 but blues clip a bit)
Black level: 62 (60-65 is fine depending on how much detail you want to see vs how dark you like your blacks)
DCR: Off
Sharpness: 2
Color Temp: User R-53, G-50, B-54 this gave me a white point balance of 6561k (6500k target for "true white")
Hope this helped anyone wanting to make sure they get the best calibration for this display because the ppi is still absolutely insane and the display is still going strong for me!

Updated review: Well, I've gone through 3 of these things and they're all lemons. The reports of a wide range of QC issues all over the net are accurate.

The first and 3rd have visible horizontal scan lines across the screen with gsync enabled. This happens whether the panel is overclocked or not. The 2nd one I had experienced the same scan lines, but only in the leftmost 1/3rd of the screen...

All 3 panels experienced excessive IPS glow in the corners, and my 3rd one experienced excessive back light bleed towards the top. I don't know if it's because of how wide these are, but I've never seen IPS glow so dramatic in 16:9 screens.

If you somehow strike gold and get a perfectly functioning one of these, it's worth it. I came from 3x1080p 24" TN panels and am never going back.

For >$1000, these issues shouldn't exist in so many panels.

I'll probably look at replacing this with one of the HP or AOCs ultrawide VA panels that are supposed to come out this month instead.

My first monitor developed a problem after about 6 months. It reached the point where it would only recognize the HDMI signal after multiple resets. All of the other inputs seemed to work correctly. (Tested with multiple computers and multiple cables.)
I contacted Asus support and spent 15 minutes going through the usual scripted questions. At the end of that the HD technician said that I should send the monitor in and they'd send out a replacement. Since I didn't have the original box to ship it in, I pointed out that this monitor includes their "VIP" support which includes advance shipment. The tech stated that that only applies if they have one available (not what it says in the docs) and that he would check on it. He put me on "hold" - which is to say he terminated my call.
I called back and got a different tech. Went through the same process, got the same disclaimer. This time he put me on hold to check and see if there was a monitor available for advance replacement. 10 minutes later he came back and said that there was not a monitor available.
I asked to speak to a supervisor. About 15 minutes later he came back and said that there was still no supervisor available but that they had found a monitor to ship me. After I provided my info again, he arranged shipment (though they put a hold on my credit card equal to the retail price of the monitor).
The monitor showed up promptly with return shipping (prepaid) provided. (They did promptly release the hold on my CC when they received the faulty monitor.)
About 3 hours after I plugged in the replacement monitor I heard a fairly loud "pop" from the back left side of the monitor. The screen went blank and then returned a few seconds later. Since that time, I've used the monitor for 30-40 hours without issues. Yesterday it popped again, and once again the screen went blank for several seconds.
I'm going to have to contact their support again and presumably go through all of that hassle again.
As an IT manager, I know that support is just as important as product quality. Unfortunately Asus seems not to have learned this lesson yet.

One Star Off Reason: Two Monitors with Dead Pixels before receiving a Good One. This is not an Amazon Issue, nor is it a Shipping Issue, this issue resides purely with Samsung. Being as this is the first 4k monitor I have ever purchase, I was sure to check for bad or dead pixels once I received them.

Pros:
1) Image quality is superb, blacks are wonderful, pictures look great, viewing angle is awesome!
2) 1ms Latency is great!
3) One Display Port and Two HDMI will allow you to have multiple inputs, of which you can do PIP for two of them (with different placement options and sizes for the second input).
4) Pleasing to look at! The bezel on this is glossy, but is only ~1/2" wide.
5) Light monitors. I took an Asus 23" 1080p 16:9 3D Monitor off my desk, and it had to weight 2x more than this one.
6) OSD interface placement and style is weird, but intuitive. The interface will be accessible only though the bottom or right side of the screen though, so if you plan on doing a Over-Under mount setup, plan ahead with your OSD settings.
7) VESA 75 x 75 capable, but more on that bellow.

Cons:
1) Manufacturer not catching one monitor with one bad pixel and one dead pixel, and then another one with one bad pixel and a 2-3 pixel cluster that was dead. Great QC on the last one I received though, no bad/dead pixels there!
2) Due to the light weight design, they do wiggle when the desk is shaken. I have a very large desk, so they should not have this much wobble.
3) Only Up and Down Tilt available on the default mounts. Samsung has another mount available that allows height and swivel adjustment, maybe a little more expensive is a better choice for a few more bucks?
4) Bezel is Glossy. Fingerprints...

Mounting Support Options:
VESA Mounting is possible with the 75 x 75 mount type. However, the mount location is on the top part of the back panel. This means that if you decide to mount this monitor on an arm (or any other VESA 75 x 75 mounts), then you will have to account for the higher mount location.

Don't let anyone lie to you... bigger is better! Well, the first one came in last week and it had a gorgeous display. It is crisp and easy to read text on. I went ahead and purchased the second one which arrived today. I have one connected to my HDMI port and the second one connected to the VGA port. At first, Windows 7 simply mirrors them showing the same picture on both monitors, but if you go into your display settings in Windows, you can change it to "Extend Desktop" which takes you from the standard 1920x1080 resolution to a second desktop (for a total of 3840x1080) which you can drag applications over to so you can see something on monitor 1 and something else on monitor 2. The picture is awesome! I run an Internet software company and I typically have 20 or 30 applications open, so having two of these monitors should really increase productivity for me. I would recommend this to anyone looking for a great monitor. The 27" is a great size. And I do agree with what everyone else has said about the sound. I think someone at Viewsonic was not very intelligent for trying to add speakers and even less intelligent for trying to make it a selling point in their advertising. The price is right too!

We operate a medical office, and needed multiple privacy screens for employees. One employee already had the hanging type, and it is excellent aside from the dimensions (far too large for our 27" monitors). So after carefully measuring the precise dimensions, we bought 5 of these with confidence. After all, products such as this should be very similar to one another, aside from the size, right?

Not even close. With the hanging type, you can see perfectly through the privacy screen, assuming that you are sitting within the narrow field of the viewing angle. Whereas with this product, even while sitting perfectly still directly centered on the monitor, you constantly have to make small adjustments to see your screen. In other words, you must move your head around to see corners and edges of the screen. Also, the screen itself is quite a bit more blurry than with the other type even if you are perfectly centered. It does not seem to be clear, rather a bit of a haze covers the screen (and yes, we did remove the cling from each side).

Another issue, as seen in the photo, is the cutout. I don't know for whom these were originally manufactured, but for a modern monitor, a cutout is a distraction, not a benefit. Almost seems like they were spec designed for a system which needed the cutout.

Also, not crazy about the attachment system.

Lastly, and most importantly of all, was the poor protection afforded by these. After getting it perfectly situated (a bit of a challenge, but very doable), I moved about 4 feet away and to one side by about 3 feet to check the screen. It was fully visible. Dimmed, but not close to what we were expecting. We were willing to live with the annoyance of the poor viewing angle, but if people can see your screen then what is the point. I know that sounds contradictory, stating that it is difficult to see through and then stating that it is TOO easy to see the screen. It's simply a matter of being able to comfortably view the monitor while working vs an unauthorized person being able to sneak a quick peek. Yes, it's hazy from any angle, but it is viewable from practically any position/angle.

We rarely return any product to Amazon, because we research before we buy. But these are going back.

i read many of the reviews before i bought this monitor. most ppl dont understanding modern technology. just because you intend on touching a certain part of the screen doesnt mean you are actually touching it. sometimes the angle that you are sitting in can make it seem like the monitor isnt reacting or will make other things that you didnt intend on touching activate. this exact same thing happens on cell phones and tablets, thats just the nature of the product.

another thing is when you are using the computer that this monitor is connected to, the mouse is "first priority".
so if you are using the touch screen and you bump or unintentionally move the mouse, the touch screen will play "2nd fiddle" and deactivate. if this happens, or even if you were using the mouse and want to switch to touch screen, you have to touch the screen TWICE. once to "deactivate" the mouse (you'll see the cursor disappear) and once to activate the touch screen. this makes sense because the computer needs to "know" which function you are trying to use. and its not designed to use both at the same time. there may be some cases where you can do that, but that is dependent on the program you are multi tasking with

* Two red LED's on the front (great if your setup is red, but can be turned off otherwise)
* The stand is not very tall or adjustable (about 3-4" off desk), but it's extremely solid construction and the monitor can be wall-mounted instead if you prefer. I personally have always used a riser so this doesn't bother me.
* A few of the color mode presets (game, movie, FPS, RTS, etc) are pretty similar.

Cons:
* Menu navigation is hard to get used to. It's not intuitive at all, and it's easy to lose your place or select the wrong thing. You do get used to it over time, but changing from game mode to movie mode for example is not exactly quick.
* If you select the wrong language during first startup (very easy to do), then it's quite annoying to figure out how to change it back to English. If this happens to you, language is the top/first setting under the "OSD" sub-menu.
* There is a slight "second bezel" effect around the screen. By this I mean that the actual illuminated display area does not quite run from bezel-to-bezel. There is about a 4mm black border around the entire screen. Honestly I saw some reviews complain about this but I don't even notice it during use. Honestly in practice it just looks like the monitor has a slightly thicker bezel than it actually does.

Overall, really cannot recommend this monitor enough for a budget gaming setup.

[[VIDEOID:67b2652aac5e3f1991e80f10c09dee11]] This monitor came in an oversized box, but nicely and securely packaged. It comes with cables (DisplayPort, HDMI & USB-C), a huge power adapter with good length cords, a well written user manual and color calibration report.

Assembling the stand to the monitor took just a couple of minutes and did not require any tool. The stand is well made and sturdy. The stand has an opening through which you can pass the cables. The monitor is thick (~2.5”), almost twice thicker than my few years old 27” HP monitor. With the stand it weighs ~6.6 kg (~14.5 lbs).

After powering it on, I selected the language and was good to go. The menu system is easy to navigate and easy to update the settings. For now, I am happy with the factory calibration, I may tweak it in the future as needed. I love how bright it can get. My desk is next to a window, with the monitor facing the sunlight in the AM. The brightness is so great that I can easily see or read the content on the screen. Not much information in the manual about the mini LED. The only reference I found was in the cover page, elsewhere, the product is referred to as an LCD monitor including the spec sheet.

I wish the monitor had USB-A ports, most monitors at this price point have them. I own a couple of 24” HP EliteDisplay monitors, they have a couple of USB-A ports which I use. The USB-C port is great, when connected to my 2016 & 2020 MacBook Pro, it charges the battery as well. This monitor has all the tilt, rotate, height adjustment features normally seen in a top end monitor. I am impressed with the brightness and sharpness of the monitor. The LED display is wonderful, I can see great details displayed. Auto brightness is turned off by default. I did enable it and had to turn it off after a few minutes as it was changing the brightness almost every second or two. Watch the google search page at the end of the video showing the brightness changing quite often when auto brightness is on.

I use this monitor with my work HP EliteBook (Win 10), personal Lenovo IdeaPad (Win 11) gaming laptop and 2020 MackBook Pro. The application icons look super sharp but a bit saturated too. Generally, when I attach a monitor to a Windows 7 office computer, it works with very little adjustment. Not so with this BenQ PD2700Q monitor. It's a nightmare and their tech service was no help. The problem is when the office cad computer is shut down at night. the 2 monitors go down too. In the morning, the computer is started, but the BenQ monitor must be physically started again by pushing its power button. Annoying, but whatever. The real issue is that every time the monitor shuts off and is turned back on, the desktop icons are all rearranged. Every time. That's NOT ACCEPTABLE. I tried to find an option to lock the desktop icons, but I couldn't, so I finally called the BenQ service tech. Their response was that "it's a Windows issue, not a BenQ issue." Well, I've never had this issue with any other monitor; only BenQ. So unless BenQ quickly changes their minds and figures out a solution to my problem, I will return this monitor and try another brand - and then it WILL BE a BenQ issue.

03/31/2019 UPDATE: I think that I may have fixed the problem on my own, as follows:

I right-clicked on the desktop and reviewed the dropdown list of choices and I pulled up the NVidia NView Desktop Manager. Everything looked normal - except for one box labeled: ENABLE. So I clicked on it and apparently, the NVidia video card system is now in charge of the BenQ monitor. I've turned the computer on and off a few times and everything seems to be back to normal.

Apparently, when I installed the BenQ monitor using BenQ's instructions, it automatically disabled the old NVidia control system and installed it's own. Now, the monitor turns off when the computer is turned off, and turns on when the computer turns on and it remembers everything!

BenQ tech support did finally contact me with fixes after I finally figured out the problem, so kudos for that. However, BenQ techs should have been initially savvy enough to assist a little more than just dismissing it as a Windows problem and not as a BenQ problem without offering some suggestions as to how to address the problem. I changed my rating from a 1-star to a four-star as the monitor does seem to work okay now, so I guess it's a keeper.

April 2, 2019 - NOT SO FAST. I cancelled the return of the monitor and this morning - the very next day, the monitor returned to it's old ways of rearranging my entire desktop upon startup. I've never ever had this much problem installing a monitor. What has this thing done to my computer? Rating changed back to one-star.

June 6, 2019 - Now the problem of rearranging my desktop icons has become intermittent. Some mornings, I will turn on the computer and all is well. Other mornings I come in and turn on the computer, and the desktop is completely rearranged and all the icons from both screens are crammed into the BenQ monitor. However, I found a solution: DON'T PANIC! Simply turn off the computer and re-start it. For some reason, everything returns to normal. This BenQ monitor has a fine display, but it's not worth the hassles of screwing up the software on your computer. I would recommend purchasing from a more reputable company with a long history of providing good products. Buying this BenQ monitor was a crap shoot and I'm still trying to figure out if I won or lost.

I read every single reviews and then...waited...waited and waited some more before I finally took the plunge and bought one. If you're sitting on the fence like I was  - don't! The buttons on the front do feel a bit old fashioned (they need to be pressed firmly to work) but I think that makes them feel more durable. There should have been mounts on the left & right sides. I'm also not crazy about having the buttons on the pen. It's too easy to click on them while you're drawing. Also, the rubber grip surrounding the buttons gathers dust and dirt instantly which I'm sure will eventually affect the button's functionality over time. Other than what I've mentioned the pen is very comfortable to hold/balanced and feels great to draw with. I truly believe that this tablet will be game changer in my future work. It's reignited my passion for creating artwork and I can't wait to see where it will take me. Just get one!

I got the BIM111 version, and out of the box it's pretty awful in regards to contrast. The black colors are blended in and you can't differentiate any blacks. HOWEVER, once you go into the settings and change a few things, the contrast is pretty good and far superior to stock/default settings.

I have this panel right next to my 27GL850, which is known as a pretty good monitor for 1440p. I duplicated the screens so I could do a fair comparison, and the HP monitor is very similar after changing around some settings. I tested a few games like red dead redemption in the night time and forza horizon 5, and a few 4k videos. I would absolutely pay the $160 for this monitor over the $300+ I paid for the LG monitor. Insane deal at $160 for this monitor, and not terrible for anywhere around $240 either.

What settings did I change to get a better picture? Here they are:
Gaming - response time: Level 3 (haven't really put to test, you can decide what's best for you)
Color: Native
Image - brightness: 60
Contrast: 100
Dynamic Contrast: ON
Black stretch: MEDIUM (THIS MAKES A MASSIVE DIFFERENCE, ALSO GOOD ON HIGH)
Sharpness: Sharp

After implementing these changes, the picture quality is more than acceptable, especially given the price.

I'm upgrading from a 27" MSI 1440p monitor and the difference is huge. The MSI could barely pull 350 nits, which makes HDR content unusable.

Really sharp image, amazingly rich and vibrant colors, wonderful design (I'm a big fan of edge-to-edge glass displays). This monitor is one heck of a quality product, especially for that price ! Now, here's a few things to remember if you want to enjoy it to its full potential :

-Obviously, it's highly reflective, so just make sure you have no window or strong light source right behind you, and you should be ok.

-When connected through HDMI, you'll likely experience the "limited RGB range" problem, which will give you poor contrast and color saturation. Not the monitor's fault, it's kind of a standard for HDMI. But depending on your graphic card, there are different workarounds for this problem ;-)

-Some people complain that text look jaggy on 27" 1080p monitors, but I've come to realised that this is mostly due to the way windows handles fonts (Clear Type). So I came accross a little utility named gdipp, which can make fonts looks way better and even similar to how they are rendered on Macs for example. And after installing it, I really witnessed what a 27" 1080p monitor is really capable of in term of text rendering. Fonts in my text editor are now extremely smooth and sooo easy on the eye. Incredible difference. !

-My settings for a great image : in the monitor's menu : custom colors (red 95, green 91, blue 98). Gamma = 2.0 (based on the gamma calibrating tools found on this page : [...]

The only thing I really miss on this monitor is an adjustable stand, but with a nice monitor riser or even a book, it's not that much of a big deal. :-)

Review from the tech geek in the immediate family:

For reference, comparisons are being made to the 4 year old Asus VG278 27in/144hz gaming monitor I've been using as my primary monitor.

The good: The increased refresh rate is noticeable, and the color depth is as good as a TN monitor can be. The monitor comes with an easy to use built-in menu which lets you quickly configure settings, including color palette, sharpness, and contrast, based on some configurable pre-made profiles. The curve makes the monitor feel a little bit bigger than a flat monitor, though it's right on the lower end of where a curve would actually help at all. It comes with a DisplayPort cable, and has HDMI ports as well for additional inputs.

The bad: The stand is, quite frankly, primitive. It's a flat tripod stand, and the only adjustments you can make are to the tilt of the screen--I had to put it on a small stack of textbooks and an old plastic dice box to get it stable at the right height for my desk setup. It could be fine for someone who's alright with looking downwards at a screen, but it takes external support for any sort of height adjustment. Compare this to the VG278, which has a swiveling, circular base as well as built in height and tilt adjustment. On top of that, it would take a very powerful machine to max out the monitor's 240hz refresh rate, making this a monitor best suited for enthusiasts building PCs in the $2000-2500 range--who probably have better options as far as monitors.

My overall rating is a 3/5: it's a good monitor on its own merits, but the curve is a bit gimmicky, the price is a bit high for what it offers, and the stand is uninspiring to say the least.

I would like to mention that I have 34GP950G-B and a 32GP850-B that I use for a combination of gaming and work. I decided to pick this monitor up for the fact that it has HDMI 2.1 and I recently got a PS5. The 32GQ850-B.AUS was clearly designed around supporting gaming consoles as it shows most of the video features that current game consoles display on their video menus, for example, VRR setting. Of course, previous monitor versions with G-Sync are VRR capable it is just not shown on the display menu. I would say if I compare my last year 32GP850-B with the new 32GQ850-B the only differences I see are (not comprehensive):

1- Higher Refresh rate.
2- More console centered Display menu showing the status of VRR. See attached pictures for detail.
3- HDMI 2.1.
4- DTS headphone Jack.
5- Higher price.
6- RGB-LED lighting at the back of the monitor.

That being said, in terms of day-by-day work and gaming I saw not much difference. During work the difference between 165Hz to 240Hz was not visible from my point of view. For PS5 gaming there is no difference due to the capabilities of the console itself. However, I must say that both of these monitors support 4K@60Hz on the PS5 which is quite awesome. I believe these monitors come with a 4k panel and limited by software to 2k resolution.

I used my 34GP950G-B to compare the RGB lighting with the 32GQ850-B and I have no say there is no comparison. The RGB on the new 32GQ850-B are quite lacking on features and flexibility, one main drawback is that you cannot really control the intensity of the light you can only turn them on or off. In addition, this can only be done thru the on-screen menu there is no hardware on the monitor do adjust the lights. The 34GP950G-B does have a dedicated dial to adjust the lights brightness. Do not get me wrong the lights looks amazing but could be quite bright if looking at them directly.

All of these 3 monitors suffer of the usual IPS issues, black not being pitch black and panel edge light bleeding. Of course, this will depend from panel to panel and I have been lucky enough not to have panels with light bleeding. To me these IPS issues are not a deal breaker and I enjoy all of these 3 monitors. HDR works great on all of them as long as you do not enable the response to the FASTEST this will cause a horrible halo effect when you scroll.

In conclusion, if you own a 32GP850-B I do not see the need for an upgrade to the new 32GQ850-B. If you are in the market for an excellent overall monitor then the 32GQ850-B could be the one you are looking for as it checks all the boxes for gaming and work. Now if you do not care about the extra 80Hz of refresh rate and VRR being displayed on the menu or HDMI 2.1 then you can save yourself tons of money if you go for the 32GP850-B. I would recommend the 32GQ850-B only if it is on sale otherwise, I would still pick 32GP850-B.

This screen has a stunning image with a litany of options and settings. However, there is one caveat.

Samsung knows how to make a screen. This screen shows it if you weren't already acquainted with the brand.

Product assembly is clever, using the packaging box as a foundation and requiring one Phillips screwdriver (you have been warned). It came together in minutes with a heavy metal base, a mounting arm, and the three cables required for full functionality. One power cable (standard PC), one HDMI, and one USB cable for hub-utility.

The level of care in the instructions and assembly show throughout the design as well. Menus are easy to navigate with a large rubberized control mechanism on the bottom of the monitor (pictured). The device rotates and angles smoothly into vertical or landscape view. These features just work. No unexpected twists or turns.

There is a hub located on the back and left side of the screen that features three USB ports and a 3.5mm headphone jack. Handy. These work through the aforementioned USB cable that plugs into the monitor.

Assembly complete the moment to inspect the screen arrives.

Very little light bleed. Deep blacks. High contrast. Eye-bleeding brightness. Dense pixel count (1440p at 24 inches). This screen is a looker. Turn the settings to Game Mode and the colors will burn into your retinas (it may actually be a bit too vivid). Turn up the responsiveness in-menu and this thing can game (or movie) very well.

Despite the natural quality of the screen, however, this is not a device where you should attempt to capitalize on the HDR feature. It is disappointing. Not only does it fail to properly highlight brights and darks, it reveals the fine pattern of lighting grid while doing so. Just turn it off. Life is better. I don't generally expect strong HDR from most back-lit screens. I did not count this against the rating.

The missing star is due to the odd behavior this monitor exhibits when switching tasks.

Whether starting (or leaving) a full-screen video, jumping into or out of a game, or any number of other focus-based tasks the screen will cut to black briefly before popping back to life. It is almost as if it had switched inputs. The current input appears in the top left corner much the same, and the menu controls will flash for a few seconds as if you had forgotten how to maneuver. This sort of behavior I would expect from a resolution change or an actual input change, but it happened during regular use every time I would switch tasks (seemingly without fail).

To counter the issue I attempted to update drivers and tested various settings (I was not exhaustive, however). I do not think this behavior is hurting the monitor or is a sign of disrepair. It appears to just be an odd design choice. Perhaps it is associated with a setting I did not discover, but research proved difficult to narrow down amid other flicker-based monitor issues (which this is not).

I can recommend this monitor for its great utility and its beautiful picture. As an accompanying screen it would (literally) shine. Vertically it can handle text, and the built in eye-saver options will help with documents. The resolution is dense here. The screen makes an excellent content viewer or gaming monitor for those who don't mind the size and the just-above adequate refresh rate.

Just be mindful of the mentioned issue.

The blacks are very deep. Picture quality better than IPS in most ways, especially for contrast and dark scenes. I think most people complaining about their IPS bleed would be amazed by a VA monitor, and there are not enough VA monitors for sale just because VA is heavily lacking behind IPS in the public consciousness.

That being said, I'm not totally satisfied, due to the matte coating which introduces "snow" or "graininess" into the scene, and is most noticeable on big sections of uniform color like a blue sky or white background. Ever since buying an iMac I've realized how ridiculously better-looking glossy displays are, as long as the monitor is positioned to eliminate glare. Ever stopped to wonder why high-end TV's and smartphones are all glossy, yet gaming monitors are all matte? Me too, I could never figure it out. To add to the irony it actually makes even less sense to make a matte gaming/entertainment monitor than a matte phone/laptop screen, because a phone could be used outdoors whereas a gaming monitor would already be positioned to eliminate glare 100% of the time, which makes anti-glare pointless.

The matte coating is actually worse than some other matte monitors (my work monitor does not have noticeable graininess). It's the only flaw. If someone would make a monitor exactly like this in every way except glossy or semi-glossy, or at least a less-grainy anti-glare, it would literally be the best monitor in the world. That being said, picture quality is perfect in every other way, and vibrancy is just stunning in spite of the matte.

I will warn you ahead of time, be prepared to possibly go through one, two, or more RMA's for the perfect monitor. If you're researching this monitor, I'm more than positive you're aware of the QC issues running rampant with this monitor. You should know that the ROG Swift also has similar concerns, so don't let the thought of RMA scare you off to an inferior product. I consider myself lucky, as I went through one RMA to get my somewhat-flawless monitor. I'll start with the positives.

What I like:
- G-Sync is a complete game changer. You no longer have to toss between screen tearing or input lag, this fixes both issues. Not only that, it keeps the image looking fluidly smooth when frames drop. This compliments 144hz perfectly.

- 144hz. Experience it once, experience it forever. Having to RMA my first monitor, when I went back to my temp 60hz monitor, I got headaches. The fluidity of 144hz is much more life-like. To those who comment that one cannot perceive past 60hz, they say so because they haven't had the luxury of affording this puppy and seeing 144hz in action. It's night and day.

- IPS. This IPS panel is superb, not cheap. You're definitely getting your money's worth. Through GTA V alone I can notice a significant difference in color reproduction versus that of the standard TN panel. Colors pop, and blacks are defined more than ever. TN is a thing of the past.

- 1440p. Before you purchase this monitor, ensure you have the hardware to let this monitor shine. I had to upgrade to a 980 Ti (from a 670 ftw) before I could really take advantage of 1440p. Be prepared for your frames to drop if you're coming from 1080p. The 980 Ti was the perfect upgrade, I can easily get 144hz with some graphic setting manipulation (very high-ultra), though playing some current gen games at ultra will bring you to 60-90 range.

-Flexibility. This monitor, like most standard high range gaming monitors, can tilt, turn, adjust up/down and angle back and forth. You'll have no problems getting it up for whatever your circumstances might be.

What I don't like:
-Light bleed. I said this was an somewhat-flawless monitor (the one I'm reviewing). Light bleed is still there, but it's now almost unnoticeable because I received one of the better monitors concerning this issue. The first monitor I had was completely unusable as it was so distracting, however now I truly have to focus on the light bleed areas to notice the difference. Regardless, it would be better if there was no light bleed at all.

-Glossy plastic. To begin with, the overall design isn't that nice compared to the ROG Swift. The glossy plastic looks toy-ish, but you're likely not to notice these things when you're more concerned with what's on the panel and not what's around it. In early August 2001 I bought an NEC/Mitsubishi LCD 1700M+ from Amazon.com after a considerable amount of product and price comparison shopping. This display was to replace a CRT 17" display that had failed after years of good service. I expected that the new NEC monitor would be an adequate replacement, taking up much less space and drawing less power from my UPS, while not presenting too many of the problems I thought would be inherent in the LCD technology. In fact, NEC's LCD technology appears to be superior to my previous CRT in almost every way. The colors are more vivid, black is totally dark, the overall image is sharper and much, much brighter. There is no viewing-angle problem: the image at 70-80 degrees off axis is still as bright as my CRT used to be on-axis. This is a very good product.
Update after six months: I still think this is a great monitor, and would recommend it highly. My only complaint is that there is no jack for headphones to bypass the built-in stereo speakers. My old monitor had a jack, so I could listen to webcasts without bothering other folks.
Update after 4 1/2 years: The monitor is still as bright and clear as ever, even after years of service. I keep it powered 24/7, and actually in use over 40 hours per week.
Update after almost ten years: It is still working well. This old monitor just keeps chunkin' away. It takes a minute or two to reach full brightness, but then the image is flawless.
Final update late 2013: The monitor developed an intermittent fault and had to be replaced, but only after it had provided 12 years of steady service.

While I'd never use this monitor on my dual-screened gaming system, this has been a very nice upgrade to my home work station since my job is 100% WFH and I sometimes use that workspace for other tasks.

The included KVM makes it easy to flip back and forth between my work or personal laptops, although my work laptop occasionally likes to make a fuss about not seeing the microphone I have hooked up. Swapping the KVM between the two machines usually clears that up, and is a pretty quick process.

Image quality is quite good overall, it's no high end display, but it's bright, constrasty, and has good color overall. Although it lacks high refresh rates or fancy VRR features, it is a good responsive 60Hz panel when used in split mode. 120Hz is available, but you have to use it as one super wide display.

The limitations of how these displays operate with respect to multiple input/screen dividing options is ultimately where I feel like they're all lacking. For instance, although it's easy to get a USB C adapter to hook up two monitors, you can't seem to run this like two side-by-side monitors over USB-C to a single computer, even though that would be pretty handy.

Additionally, getting in to the settings can be frustrating with how the panel likes to time out and reset the display when trying to make connections. You have to keep pressing the button to get the OSD to come up, and heaven forbid a computer resets the video signal or the whole monitor reinitializes.

At the end of the day it's little QOL things that could make the experience better, but it's still a great workhorse and keeps my laptop charged to boot.
