I bought this G-Sync monitor and was extremely excited to get it up and running. The minute I got home from work and plugged it into my video card, things started to go south. First off, my second monitor which was plugged into my VC via HDMI was no longer recognized. I went to reboot to see if that would fix the problem and suddenly my computer had a crash dump, which it hasn't had in 3 years. Once the crash dump was finished processing I decided to reinstall my video card drivers to see if that would fix the issue. Once again, I rebooted, and once again, I got a crash dump. Something about this monitor was conflicting with my video card. I decided to give up on trying to fix that and instead moved on to see how the monitor impacted my gameplay. I booted up Diablo 3 and was prepared to be blown away--but no change. I started to google "how to set up my G-sync monitor." Pages upon pages of instructions until I finally found a simple process. I opened my NVidia control panel and went to the place where enabling G-Sync was supposed to be and it wasn't there. I even verified through the NVidia site that my video card supported G-Sync so I was at a loss. Another googled webpage told me to turn on Vertical Sync via my NVidia control panel and that that would enable G-Sync---once again to no avail. I finally called a computer expert friend of mine for some help. He remoted in and tried to do all the same steps I did and wasn't able to find any option to enable G-Sync. He was though, able to go into my monitor setting and turn it up to 144hz, which DID look amazing. But no G-sync for gameplay.

So that's all the technical headache. Aesthetically, this monitor has much to be desired and is really showing its age. The monitor is thick, with a thick frame. Doesn't match the look and feel of modern, sleeker monitors. Looked like an ugly stepchild when sitting next to my Dell monitor.

Pros:
144hz looks nice
Very reasonable price for a monitor

Cons:
Very difficult to get working properly
Not compatible with some NVidia video cards
Ugly aesthetics

I've been using an old low-end 24" 1080p Viewsonic for years, and this 27" ASUS ProArt WQHD monitor is a huge upgrade. Beautifully bright and clear display, no dead pixels that I can see, just the right resolution for this size monitor, IMHO (no need to scale to make text readable at "normal" web and application sizes, as is needed at 4k). The stand is excellent for a monitor in this price range, very adjustable (it even rotates, and there are degree markings), and goes high enough for comfortable viewing without needing extra lift as most monitors need, good tilt range, too. Overall this looks and feels like a very high quality monitor.

The only weakness is the speakers, which sound thin and tinny. They work, and better than the previous monitor, but I wouldn't want to use it for watching TV/movies or for gaming without much better external speakers or headphones. For work and watching the occasional video, it's find. I wanted built-in speakers just to keep my desk clean, and I guess it satisfies that requirement, but I can see that being an annoyance that'll bug me, especially if I find myself needing sound more often. They aren't loud enough to listen to over a fan or my (admittedly quite loud) air conditioner running nearby. So, I wish they'd spent an extra couple bucks for better speakers.

But, visually, this is a stunner.

Got this monitor to upgrade a very old 24" monitor my son was using with his laptop. He plays video games a lot... nothing too intense but this Acer CB272 27" monitor has worked really well and was a great upgrade from his old monitor. It's really pretty nice for the price... it's adjustable vertically and swivels, has built-in speakers, and has a good set of video inputs (DVI, HDMI, VGA). My only big gripe is that it ships with a VGA cable! Who uses VGA any more?! Wish it shipped with an HDMI cable which has been industry standard for years. Fortunately we already had an extra one.

For the price, this is a great monitor for productivity or light gaming. It's got a sleek design that looks much more expensive than it actually costs, and it's got really solid performance too.
Absolutely love this monitor. The picture is grisp, bezel-less, and my desk got de-cluttered since I don't need two monitors anymore. It also looks stunning overall.

The dot pitch is quite good, as is the viewing angle. It's definitely an improvement over my laptop's (HP dv7-6c20us) normal screen, which has moderate issues when it comes to viewing angles. I haven't compared them side by side, but it's roughly as good as my old 20.1" ViewSonic VX2025WM in regards to the viewing angle, and I absolutely loved that thing, so that's quite a compliment! This one is obviously bigger, though, and has better resolution as well, so the picture quality is maintained.

The viewing angle on this is only slightly superior to that of the Acer S271HL, so it seems like the IPS vs. TN diagrams are exaggerations, as the Acer's viewing angle is very reasonable despite being a TN monitor. The diagrams would be pretty accurate if you were to compare an IPS monitor to the screen that comes with my laptop, though, so I guess it depends on which two monitors are being compared.

It allegedly has 5 ms response time and extremely high contrast ratio (50,000,000:1) for an IPS monitor. To be honest, it almost seems too good to be true and I wonder if some of the features are an exaggeration. According to the specs, there is simply no competition for this monitor in its price range. It's also extremely thin for an IPS monitor. I can't really complain about the picture -- it looks really good -- I just wonder if it's THAT much better than its competition.

The construction of the monitor has its high and low points. It has a really sleek look and the buttons on the stand are well hidden, but the plastic on the top of the base is a bit flimsy and the stem that connects the screen to the base could be more sturdy.

This item comes with a VGA/D-Sub cable, AC->DC adapter, and a 3.5mm TRS audio connector. It does not come with an HDMI cable.

The speakers are terribly quiet, so if you're relying on this monitor to provide you with sound, expect to be greatly disappointed.

Overall, it's a very nice monitor. It has a few minor flaws, but it definitely has more than enough highlights to make up for them. I'd like to give it 4.5 stars, but I'll go with 5 because it's relatively easy on the pocketbook and has a sleek look.

When you unbox this monitor it's clear immediately that this is a well built, solidly constructed item, much heavier than an equivalently sized TV, and with a solid metal stand. There is a small white LED "on" light just below the LG logo at the centre of the bottom bezel, and the bezel at the bottom is slightly thicker than the other three sides which improves the overall appearance. One nice touch is that the LED light can be turned off in the menus. On the bottom edge of the bottom bezel is the joystick control used to navigate the menu system and configure the monitor - I found that to be easy to use.

Picture quality is excellent. I bought this to use as a second monitor for my Retina MacBook Pro (2015), and use it mostly for Adobe Lightroom. You should be aware that running any 4k monitor at the full native resolution of 3840 x 2160 will give you menu bars and other interface elements that are so small you'll need to sit with your face about a foot from the screen to comfortably read text. The other scaled screen resolutions offered in the macOS Displays system preference are

1920 x 1080
2560 x 1440
3008 x 1692
3360 x 1890

Of these only the first two work really well, the others are somewhat fuzzy due to the math involved in scaling. I mostly use the monitor at the 2560 x 1440 resolution which provides a big increase over the 1680 x 1050 of my 13" MacBook display, with the occasional foray into full native resolution. To run this monitor at the higher resolutions at 60Hz with a MacBook Pro you will need to separately purchase a mini-Display Port to Display Port cable. The monitor came with Display Port to Display Port cable, and an HDMI cable, but the HDMI option will not work at higher resolutions than 1920 unless the refresh rate is dropped to 30Hz which produced noticable flicker of anything moving across the screen.

Finally please note that this monitor does not have built-in speakers. It does have a 3.5mm audio out jack so you can plug in headphones or external speakers, but you will get no sound otherwise if your Sound system prefs are set to output to the monitor.

I love working in Lightroom on this monitor, images look stunning, colours are vibrant but accurate, blacks are very black. A great screen.

The monitor came in today. I was excited to plug it in and start using it. The exterior truly looks great, the color and structure truly looks great. I had one major issue though. About two minutes after I plugged it in, I went downstairs to get water. I come back upstairs and the monitor is on FIRE. Now my first instinct is to put out the fire ASAP, like a normal human being, so I did not take pictures of the active fire. I did however, burn my hands in attempting to put out the fire.

Now of course this is extremely concerning, if I had left for any longer while the monitor was plugged, my house would have burned down and both my dogs would have died. Now I checked the monitor before plugging it in and because of the sleek black surface I did not see any damages.The monitor was clearly damaged prior to me plugging it in and the picture clearly shows that.

It could have been damaged through handling and shipment, but that is unlikely because the packaging was packaged in a manner where the monitor would not have cracked even if dropped very hard. So it is likely defect occurred in the period where Samsung manufactures the product and ships it to Amazon for product inspection. This is very concerning and I would like Samsung and Amazon to attempt to make sure this does not happen to anyone else.

I will not order this product again, I do not feel comfortable using a product that somehow caught on fire, especially not in a situation where I am not present and it catches on fire. I also do not know if this happened to anyone else, this product has high review and I hope they are not fake reviews because this is a true danger to ones life.

I've always used Samsung Syncmaster monitors (well, at least for the past 10 years) and this latest in the series doesn't disappoint. Some of the great improvements made include:

- big, bright display with excellent color and depth; as usual, the Syncmaster monitors never fail to look great and just work
- extremely flat screen: aside from the stand, the screen is less than an inch thick, and it just looks so good
- vertically-mounted inputs: having the video/power cables coming off the side keeps them from peeking below the monitor (a problem with previous models)
- cable management clip: route all the cables on your desktop (video, speakers, keyboard/mouse, etc.) through the plastic guide on the back so you never have to see them (actually, other models have had this function too, but the difference here is a longer, thinner clip to "line up" cables one by one and keep them tighter to the stand)

The only thing I dislike about this new model is the front panel buttons. They may look cool, as they flow and are made from the actual plastic outer panel, but there are problems with this. First, it is impossible to tell which button is what in low-light settings. Similarly, it is no longer easy to power off the screen at night. Previous models had the blue LED light for power ON the power button, but this model places the power light NEXT to the power button, so when you're staring at a big, bright screen, you basically have to feel for the power button to switch off. Finally, these buttons require more force to push than I would consider appropriate. It causes my screen to flex and rotate just trying to push a button. Poor design here, so I take off a star.

Overall, though, a great display that looks cooler than the last. I love the 1920x1200 resolution. I've never had a monitor burn out pixels. If they do develop problems, it only happens after a long period of time (years) and is worth fixing (I've had many Syncmasters refurbished for a fraction of the original value - in most cases, it involves the simple replacement of a capacitor). I highly recommend this monitor - or any of the other Syncmaster models that may better suit your own tastes in resolution, style, etc.

However, the LED backlight is very bright, and if you prefer a low brightness setting (this is especially useful when your ambient room light is low), decreasing the monitor brightness setting to zero still results in a brighter glare than most of the monitors I've owned in the past. In other words you cannot reduce the LED backlight strength to be as low as some people (like me) would feel comfortable with.

Of course, you can reduce "brightness" using the display card driver settings (with some cards at least), but this will not affect the LED backlight, only the LCD matrix colors in front of it - so it's not quite the same and not as helpful as being able to further reduce the backlight itself.

However, there are two absurd issues that are most likely software-related:

1. 4:3 aspect ration doesn’t work at all. It is greyed out. It’s the only option greyed out, and is required for console play as all of the other aspect ratios stretch the image.

2. When in PBP mode, all adjustments are disabled and some kind of default color/contrast/brightness scheme is enabled. This results in the image when gaming on console to be dim and almost completely without color. PBP mode could be a workaround for issue number one if this wasn’t also defective.

Here’s the worst part, though, and something that will absolutely sink this company in the US market: the customer support is terrible.

I called their support line and wasn’t greeted. Nothing formal was said to indicate that I had reached a support department. Just something along the lines of “yes, hello?”

While explaining my issue, I was interrupted rudely by the agent who stated “our monitors (are) not for game consoles.” I explained that the 4:3 ratio wasn’t available on my PC either, and he responded that I should try another cable.

I tried two different DisplayPort cables to no avail, then two different HDMI cables to no avail. These cables work with other high resolution monitors just fine, including a 34” ultra wide and a 28” 4K.

He said at this point there was nothing he could do to help. I responded that, per the manual, the 4:3 aspect ratio should be an option. I asked what could cause the monitor disable that option. He responded that he doesn’t know.

I asked for my case to be escalated to a more technical department, and he said they don’t do that. I asked to speak with a supervisor, and he said there is no supervisor, that he’s the only person in support in the United States.

That’s it. That was the whole conversation. I’ve worked in and managed support teams, and this is the worst support experience I’ve been exposed to.

I’ve did the next logical thing; I described my experience on twitter and tagged AOC. They responded that they’d like to help and that I should DM them my contact details. I did so and didn’t hear back for a day. I messaged them again asking if they received my info and when I should expect to be contacted. They said they needed more time. I waited another day and received no contact.

I’ve returned this monitor to Amazon as of today.

Regardless of the stats and features of their hardware, I’ll never buy another AOC product simply because of this customer support experience. I would have been willing to work with them and await a firmware update to resolve my issues, but this type of treatment is not worth it, not acceptable.

If you're looking for a quality 19" monitor at a great price, give this one a try!

Important distinction: this is for the USB-C ZenScreen. These three models should really be separate listings.

With that out of the way, I picked this up to have a secondary screen while traveling for work and I've been really pleased with it so far. Worked instantly with both a 15" MBP and two thunderbolt-3 equipped Windows machines. Just make sure your ports are compatible (look for the DisplayPort or Thunderbolt logo if you're unsure).

The biggest benefit of this over the USB 3.0 (type-B) models is that this monitor will get driven by your machine's GPU, so it's perfect for playing games in a hotel or similar situations.

Short conclusion: if you have a need for something like this, and your computer is compatible, definitely pick one up. If you want the longer breakdown, keep reading.

Screen:
I don't have any color accuracy or coverage information, but next to several other IPS monitors it looks above average. At 1080p on a 15.6" screen it's not going to keep up with a high-end laptop these days, but games, pictures, movies, and text all look as good as you can ask for given the specs. It gets decently bright, but the surface is a bit more reflective than I'd have preferred.

The different display modes are present, but the menus are clunky enough that once you find your preferred one, you'll generally want to stick with it.

Design:
The top and side bezels are small and not distracting. The bottom chin is a bit more visible, but since it houses the buttons and power connector, this is understandable. As a side effect, it also makes it so that the bottom of the actual screen is almost perfectly at the same height as a MacBook Pro, so that's arguably a plus.

Weight is as good as you can expect given the screen size. If you're trying to minimize weight, you probably shouldn't be packing an extra monitor in the first place. But if you're already packing a 15" laptop, the extra weight won't be a deal-breaker.

In hand it feels really solid. If Asus made a 15" tablet like this, it would get full marks from me on construction.

The Stand:
The smart cover is pretty good. Protects the screen and back from scratches in transport. When set up horizontally it has two different angles it can sit at, once you figure out how to fold the magnetized sections into place (the Amazon product image was helpful there). The pen and hole on the other hand, is pretty silly. There's no locking whatsoever, and it just takes one bump to topple it. But hey, free pen!

Also a word of caution about USB-A mode: Rather than providing two separate cables, Asus instead includes an adapter with male USB-A on one end and a type-C port on the other. While this is probably fine for this specific device, do not use this adapter with other type-C devices or accessories. You could easily fry your laptop or phone. Better to just get a type-A to type C USB 3.0 cable, there's plenty of good and cheap ones available.

Confession: I am not a fan of Dell. My most recent experience is buying four tablet computers from Dell. Three have already been returned for refund because of quality issues (they didn’t work) and the fourth may be returned as well if Dell is unable to resolve a significant operational issue. Dell technical support is a very bad joke, though customer support – if you push hard – is okay.

That said, my most fervent wish is that this monitor continues to work properly throughout the remainder of its 3 year warranty: it’s a nice, usable monitor.

This happens to be the largest screen monitor I’ve ever used. What a jerk I’ve been. I should have ignored space and cost considerations and gone big-screen years ago.

I’ve had 1920 x 1080 pixel resolution on 17 and 15.6 inch screens, but frankly on the 23 inch display, it’s a different world – and I love it. Now I’m thinking of two 23 inch displays, side-by-side, but that may be just a dream.

In any event, this monitor turns in a solid performance. Colors are crisp, definition is good, but not breathtakingly fantastic. Set-up is very simple, with a wide variety of input options including HDMI, DisplayPort, VGA and a couple of others, including USB 3.0 if you’re using Microsoft Lync.

I would strongly urge you to at least skim the user manual, which is available on the supplied disc or available from the Dell site. Choosing the input you want is not necessarily intuitive. Ultimately, though, you can learn the entire interface by trial-and-error, but the well-illustrated manual will make it a faster and less frustrating experience.

I didn’t bother using the internal webcam and microphone. A 2 Megapixel webcam won’t hold a handle to my Logitech C920 webcam. Microsoft Lync users, however, may prefer to use the monitor’s built-in facilities.

I used this with productivity applications like Office and appreciated the roominess. Where it really hit home, though, was on group calling applications like Google Hangouts and Skype. In particular, my graphics editing and video productions programs are totally unreal on this big screen. I really can’t understand why I held out this long. Worrying about space and cost, as I did, seems pretty silly compared to the advantages of going big-screen.

I like this Dell monitor, though I hope I never need any kind of support from Dell. In one place on the Dell site, it says the warranty applies only when used with compatible Dell computers and then goes on to list the models. For a monitor, that sounds like a pretty silly restriction, but that's the kind of thing Dell does.

I bought this monitor to go in my bedroom so I could watch TV shows at my desk, or in bed. My laptop's built in 17" display just wasn't cutting it. I'm quite pleased with my purchase, and I continue to believe that Samsung makes excellent monitors.

Pros:
*Fast start up speed. My 32" Samsung HDTV in the front room takes a roughly 15-20 seconds to turn on and have a picture, but this monitor turns on in roughly 2-3. It's REALLY fast.
*Some of the other reviews said they had difficulty turning on the monitor and using the buttons on the front. I've found if you swipe your figure over the button instead of trying to "push" it, it turns on every time. It's actually pretty slick.
*I was worried that the "touch of red" was going to be ugly. I like red, but nothing in my room IS red. Thankfully, the red is very subtle, and looks really nice in my room.
*Very sharp quality. I regularly watch HD content on this monitor, and it looks amazing.
*Samsung included all the cables and adapters, and while it isn't a huge deal, it's kind of nice.

Cons:
*When watching very dark scenes, I can see some white bleeding through on the very bottom and top middle. I've only seen this happen a few times, and it was during pitch black moments of a TV show. Even when it's happening, it's not super noticeable, but I did want to mention it.
*I know it's not a TV, but I wish it had a remote, or at least let me buy one separately. It's not a huge deal, but it would be kind of nice if I could turn it off remotely.

Summary:
It does everything I wanted it to, and even though the white bleeds through every now and then, it doesn't detract from my enjoyment of the monitor.

After some extensive research on a portable monitor to use with my Nintendo Switch, I finally decided to purchase this product. I found the internal battery to be an attractive feature compared to other portable monitors that I’ve owned. Especially considering how thin the actual device is. The impressive presentation of the packaging is of a very high and professional quality with all of the cables that you’d ever need with this. This leads me into the first problem that I have with the device. After trying numerous USB-C to USB-C cables, I’ve noticed that the only cable that seems to work is the shorter one that’s included with the unit. That’s disappointing. The clarity and quality of the matte screen itself is fantastic. The included flip cover is serviceable with a magnetic connection and few viewing angles. Colors are bright and vivid with plenty of adjustable settings. This leads me to the next and probably most glaring issue I have with the device. In order to enter the settings menu and access the different display and audio options, you must first hold down the brightness up button on the side of the screen for 3 seconds. Once the menu is open you need to use the volume buttons to cycle through and make your selections. This is needlessly convoluted and made even more frustrating by the fact that the menu seems to automatically time out after a few seconds if you haven’t made a selection. This could’ve easily been remedied by including a simple remote control in the packaging. As for performance, most of the games I’ve tested seem to run at full speed without any problems. I say most because in theory all of the games should run as expected in docked mode. Animal Crossing New Horizons in particular is one of these exceptions. The game runs at almost half speed when using this screen. I’m not sure why this happens with this game specifically, but it is certainly annoying and after adjusting multiple settings I still cannot seem to get it to run correctly. If this changes I will update my review. I have not personally tested the performance of this screen as a secondary PC monitor or while connected to any other game consoles and therefore cannot state an opinion on the touch screen feature. That said, all the needed ports and cables are included in the box should you choose. As far as the on board stereo speakers go, they’re not the loudest but decent enough for what this is. However, there is no headphone jack. All said and done, this is a great device with a lot of value for the money. Especially if you’re planning on using it for a dock less connection to your Nintendo Switch like I am. However, considering the meticulous packaging, for the money it’s a damn shame that a remote control wasn’t included or that simple menu buttons weren’t added to the unit.

Edited to show comparison: May 2022. I recently purchased another one of these after my old one was damaged. First of all I was disappointed to see that the packaging for the unit seems to have changed. What was once an impressive box is sadly now a much more simple cardboard box of noticeably lower quality. There also seems to be less branded accessories in the box. Like generic cables. I can not speak on the quality of these yet. They seem to be serviceable. One of my biggest issues with the previous unit was a noticeable performance hit with certain Nintendo Switch games like Animal Crossing running slow. I was pleasantly surprised to see this was no longer an issue. I’m not sure whether this has to do with the OLED Switch itself or if PJ has upgraded the internals of the monitor. Battery life has been fantastic with even the most demanding of Switch games. My only real complaint remains the dated design of the unit and lack of a remote control to navigate through the frustrating menu system. Which is why my rating remains 4 out of 5 stars. I’d still highly recommend this device to anyone interested in a portable battery powered monitor particularly for using their Nintendo Switch on a larger screen without having to connect the dock.

V3 Update: November 2022. Having purchased the newest iteration of this monitor, I thought I’d update my previous review to reflect the improvements and also address some of my very few criticisms. Firstly, the packaging experience has been greatly improved. While the presentation is not quite as premium as the original model, the new box and included accessories are of much higher quality than what was included with the previous model. There is also a proprietary one-piece charging cable/power brick that is nice to see. It seems pretty sturdy, but I am not sure how it will hold up over time. The usb-c cable seems to be more durable than the one included last time. The new display glass is now glossy instead of matte for better or worse. One massive improvement over the previous model is the introduction of a touch enabled settings menus. PJ is clearly listening to their customer feedback. Long gone is the frustration of having to awkwardly press and hold buttons to access and navigate the settings menus. It’s better, but is far from a smooth touch experience. I’ve found myself having to tap and swipe on the screen multiple times to register input and having to use the physical buttons to make fine adjustments. For the price, I would expect this to be a fluid and seamless touch experience. There also seems to be less options in this new touch menus than the previous model. For example, I don’t see screen ratio options or a panel uniformity setting. This isn’t a big deal as it’s still easy to adjust color and brightness settings like gamma, contrast, saturation, sharpness, and even HDR. It would be nice if these settings were given some kind of usage explanation (which are both absent from the included operation sheet and the product website). I was disappointed to see that the quad speakers have been replaced by noticeably quieter (by comparison) dual speakers. However, this time they have included a headphone jack. There are a couple extra ports on the new model and a full sized hdmi port (no longer mini) which is nice to see, but as I mainly use this monitor for my Nintendo Switch specifically, I won’t be covering those in more detail here. My biggest gripe with the new model doesn’t have to do with the monitor itself, but rather the magnetic kickstand cover it comes with. The new cover has fewer grooves which make propping up the monitor (aside from a very narrow angle) difficult. This wouldn’t be a big deal if I could use the cover from my v2 model, but it doesn’t fit v3. To summarize, there are definitely better (and probably cheaper) monitors available, but PJ differentiates themselves by including a large built in rechargeable battery for a truly unmatched portable experience particularly with the Nintendo Switch. With each new version of this monitor, they’ve come closer to perfection, but as they haven’t quite gotten there yet, my review will remain at 4 stars. Still very highly recommended.

Since I owned both monitors, I'm going to write my review as a comparison between the ASUS VG278HE and the BenQ XL2720T. Ultimately I ended up returning the BenQ and sticking with the ASUS VG278HE and I'm happy with my decision.

FYI: I used this website to calibrate the colors for both the BenQ/Asus: h t t p://w w w.lagom.nl/lcd-test/

BenQ Pros:
- Biggest advantage with the BenQ was superior contrast for dark colors. This is really noticeable when playing a game that has a lot of dark maps where you're playing in pitch black. With the BenQ you can see several shades of black from medium/dark grey all the way to absolute black. So spotting objects/enemies in darker games will yield a better experience with the BenQ. With the Asus, if you choose the Game Mode preset, you can get similar results, but it's at the expense of giving up some white contrast. Not a major deal if the map is mainly dark. Most games I've played don't really require much white contrast so this may be a non-issue for some.
- User Interface: Wow! BenQ got it right here. The S-switch makes navigating the settings menu (OSD) a breeze. The S-Switch is very intuitive as is the well thought out OSD settings menu. The S-switch gives you the ability to jump between 3 different monitor presets (ie game mode, Windows mode, cinema mode) or you can configure the 1-3 presets as other monitor shortcuts. However, there was one thing that really disappointed me with the BenQ in this regard. When you're toggling thru the different picture modes, the BenQ goes black for maybe 1/2 a second before displaying the next preset. This is kind of annoying because not only does it make comparing 2 different picture modes cumbersome, if you're starting with a picture mode at the bottom of the list, and you want to go all the way to the top of the list, you have to go thru each picture mode and endure the screen temporarily going black.
- Fit/Finish/Functionality: Again, kudos to BenQ here. The styling is very modern and portrays a classy and sophisticated appearance. The screen bezel is matte black with the BenQ to cut down on reflections, whereas the Asus features a gloss black bezel. Ultimately this made it difficult for me to settle on the Asus, but my room has blackout curtains so it ended up being a non-issue. I think the gloss black was a poor decision by Asus, but even when I have my curtains open, my eyes are drawn to the subjects on the screen and the bezel reflections aren't noticeable. The headphone hook on the back of the BenQ monitor was a very thoughtful addition and shows BenQ's dedication to providing the ultimate gaming experience. The BenQ has more connections than the ASUS including a second HDMI input and USB pass thru.
- Downloadable presets for various games. If you don't like the headaches of tweaking/calibrating your monitor, you can download custom presets from BenQ's website which are reportedly created by professional gamers. Personally I had a better experience using the URL I provided above for calibrating my monitor as I found the downloadable Battlefield preset wasn't ideal. Nonetheless, this is another well thought addition by BenQ. On that note, BenQ, if you're reading this review, what would be an even better experience would be if you allowed the end-user to set a custom name to the presets. So instead of having FPS1, FPS2, FPS3, I could rename the presets to Battlefield, Counterstrike, Diablo for instance.
- Tilt/swivel adjustments. I personally didn't benefit from this, but the BenQ blows the Asus away here. While both monitors had ample height adjustments for me, the BenQ is able to swivel into portrait mode and the back/forth tilt range was also much better with the BenQ. Since I'll never use portrait mode and I sit at eye level with the monitor, the BenQ didn't benefit me here, but I felt it's worth noting for those who might have these needs. OK well that's not entirely true, the ability to swivel the BenQ into portrait mode made accessing the DVI/HDMI connections a breeze, whereas I found the port accessibility on the Asus to be rant-worthy.

Ben Q Cons:
- Unfortunately, as much as I wanted to love the BenQ, there was no getting past an annoying checkerboard pattern that's visible when viewing moving objects, which incidentally is a major component of most video games. For me, the issue was most noticeable with lighter colors. For instance, playing Battlefield 3, when panning from side to side, as the sky moves across the screen a very noticeable checkerboard pattern would appear. This was also very noticeable in desert maps with sand colored buildings and terrain. If you do any video editing (I've got 2 little rug rats) this issue will also show when viewing skin tones. I asked several Amazon reviewers if they also experienced this and most said no. That's why I'm hoping it's only a temporary manufacturing issue. FWIW, I tried the monitor on various computers and different GPUs including both ATI and NVIDIA cards in the test. All had the exact same issue. If you end up going with the BenQ, this would be the first thing I'd look for after getting it set up. I only used 2D display modes and the issue was there regardless of whether I was running at 120Hz or 60Hz. I found BenQ's tech support to be a huge let down here as well. When I called the support number, it was immediately apparent that the "support" person was technologically challenged. Rather than trying to understand the issue or check any PC or monitor settings, I was told to return the monitor since I was within my 30 day window with Amazon. I really didn't want to go this route because the monitor was otherwise perfect with no dead pixels and I wasn't confident that a replacement monitor would have remedied the issue. I inquired whether or not there was a Tier 2 support team and the rep offered me 2 phone numbers, neither of which were toll free. The first number sent me to a voicemail inbox where the greeting wasn't setup. The second number reached voicemail as well but at least there was a greeting, albeit no mention of "BenQ Support" in the greeting itself. From a user experience, this left a lot to be desired...was I reaching a well structured tech support call center of a modern day tech company, or was I given the personal cell phones for support staff who work out of their homes? Anyway, the Tier 2 experience was equally disappointing and on par with the overall support structure. The Tier 2 rep walked me thru a couple settings and confirmed everything was configured fine on my end. He advised me that while he didn't have an XL2720T monitor readily available to try to replicate what I was seeing while I was on the phone, he would look for one in the office and call me back. Well I was never called back. After waiting 2 days I called back and only got voicemail. I called the original tech support number and told them I would like a call back. The rep said they would send an email to Tier 2 to have someone call me back. I never received a call back. After waiting another day and a half I called Tier 2 again and reached the person who originally said he'd locate an XL2720T to try to replicate my issue. He didn't find a monitor and just advised me to return my monitor to Amazon and request a replacement. Preferably what I would have liked to see happen was Tier 2 cross-ship me a new monitor so I wouldn't be without a monitor and they could look at my monitor first hand to verify whether or not there was a manufacturing problem. When I got my replacement from Amazon, it also had a February 2013 build date and suffered the same symptoms. Having experienced the same issue with 2 monitors, I can only surmise that this is a manufacturing defect. But again, what would have been really awesome, since I had the same problem with 2 monitors, would be for BenQ to unbox a new monitor and try to replicate my issue. If they experienced the same issue, then they would know there's a manufacturing issue. If they couldn't replicate the issue, then they could have shipped me that very monitor that they've personally tested and verified as functional without defects. Maybe that's asking a bit much for a single customer.
- I didn't like that some adjustment settings (ie sharpness) were not available depending on the picture mode you selected. The colors on the monitor were somewhat washed out yet there was no ability to select the "Saturation" setting in any picture mode to adjust this. Fortunately you can typically tweak these settings in your GPU's software, but if you're running multiple monitors, you may not be able to adjust each monitor independently, hence the desire for the ability to calibrate saturation using the adjustments on the monitor.

ASUS VG278HE Pros:
- Superior color saturation. I found the colors on the Asus to be much more lifelike while not being overly vibrant.
- Better sharpness vs. the BenQ. Particularly in Battlefied, I like to set my sharpness higher than you normally would use for reading text. The ASUS had a better range of sharpness adjustments that better suited my needs.
- 144Hz capabilities. Although the Asus markets the monitor as 144Hz capable (vs. 120Hz for the BenQ), I doubt most people would notice a perceivable difference. From what I understand about the interaction between your GPU and your monitor, if your GPU cannot sustain a frame rate greater than 120 FPS, there's little significance of having 144Hz capabilities.

ASUS VG278HE Cons:
- Monitor buttons / Settings adjustments. While the menus themselves have a satisfactory layout, the process of navigating them could be better. Granted, you probably won't be monkeying with your settings after your initial configuration, you may find yourself coming back to the adjustments if you're installing a new game and you want to tweak some settings. If you're the kind of gamer that has a wife and kids, you're probably like me and your only time to hop online is after the kids are asleep, and trying to find the right buttons to navigate the OSD menus in the dark will probably result in you keeping a small flashlight nearby. Possible improvements here could be better consideration of how the buttons are grouped, adding textures to the button surfaces, or illuminated button labels (and please if any manufacturers entertain this option, don't go overboard and make the lights unnecessarily bright just to be flashy, a subtle backlight is all that's necessary, and preferably only illuminated when using the buttons vs. constant illumination). The problem I found with the way the buttons are laid out / grouped is that menu key is right in the middle, so you have to fumble around to find it. If you hit the wrong button, you'll have to scurry to find the menu button to exit out of that adjustment mode. What's somewhat confusing here, again we're operating in the dark, is when you go in to the adjustment settings using the Menu button, you use the "S" button on the far left to back out and exit the settings which is easy enough whereas if you're doing any adjustments where you don't start with the menu button (ie changing picture modes or using the brightness or volume shortcut buttons) instead of using the "S" button to back out or exit the menu, you have to hit the Menu button. So if by mistake you want to hit Menu to adjust something, and you hit another button by mistake, trying to find the Menu button gets a little frustrating. Again, this is likely a non-issue for those who never configure or just do a one-time setup of their monitor, but for people like me who sometimes change settings depending on what Battlefield map I'm playing, the navigation could be better. Grip tape provides a decent workaround.
- Gloss black bezel. Some people will shy away from the Asus solely on the basis of the reflections that are symptomatic of a glossy bezel. For a gaming monitor, I consider this a major oversight on the part of Asus. I can't imagine any avid gamer would prefer a glossy bezel over a matte finish, what were you thinking Asus? This is supposed to be a gaming monitor, not a piece of art I'm displaying in my living room.
- This is kind of nitpicky since you likely won't consider it after initial setup, but accessing the ports such as DVI or HDMI inputs requires you to contort your body to get to them because they're low on the back of the monitor and the ports face down towards the ground and furthermore are slightly obscured by a small overhang which Asus apparently added to tidy up the appearance of the back of the monitor. The BenQ also Mind you, I'll say it again, this is a gaming monitor, not something I'd consider putting in a business office or other environment where I would care would people saw if they were able to see the back of the monitor so I don't get what Asus was trying to accomplish here. I have a different "lower-end" 60Hz 27" Asus where the ports on the back of the monitor face outward making connections a breeze...almost seems like Asus took a step backwards here.

In summary, I'm content with my ASUS VG278HE, however the BenQ XL2720T would have been the clear winner here if it didn't have the checkerboard issue I experienced with it.

**ONE MONTH UPDATE**

Not only was I depressed to find that the monitor is now more than $100 less than what I originally paid just over a month later, but now the HDMI ports don't seem to work at all. Just a week ago I was switching between DP and HDMI Port 1 to stream with no issue. But now? Nothing from the HDMI Ports.

I've plugged in multiple devices and nada. In fact, when I even attempt to use my Elgato HD60 S+ in said HDMI, the device loses ITS signal, and I have to unplug the output HDMI and reboot the device. So I'm removing two of the stars from my original rating. It's a beautiful image, and if you need a budget 4K Displayport and can get it for less than $300 (as it's currently listed March 23 2021), by all means this monitor is fine. But don't expect it last...

******

So, this is my 2nd AOC monitor as the first one I ordered was apparently the official ball of the World Cup before being delivered... Getting that mini-vent out of the way, let's start with the cons:

I will say this is definitely a budget 10-bit 4k. It has a light-plastic casing, only 3 inputs (1 DP, 2 HDMI), and while it is 3/4 borderless, the display does not completely fill the screen. I've attached the monitor to a VESA desk mount (the mount is a full 100mm, fyi), and I feel like I need to be extra careful moving it. The other major issue is the 108% sRGB description is wrong; It is 100% sRGB, calibrated using a Spyder5 Elite. This is what I am most disappointed about, since I bought this for video color correction, and the other color ranges suffer in turn (pictures attached). I know if I want accurate an accurate range I'm going to have to really jump up, but for now, this is where my budget took me...

That said... For $400, you could do a whole lot worse. To the eye, the image is bright and vibrant. I've watched a few 4k60 videos online, as well as some 10-Bit 4K GH5 footage I shot, and I'm pleased with the presentation thus far. I'm going to do some gaming on it, but at 60hz, I'm mostly just looking for the cinematic vs response.

I will come back to this review in about 6 months or so, as some of the reviews here to tend to have issues, and I want to keep as accurate an account as I can. I put up with a lot of BS to finally get this monitor, it's only right to now to my due diligence in giving and accurate review.

I had no problems hooking it up to my laptop with an HDMI cable (not included, but a VGA cable is, which should be adequate for most computer use). Colors appear true and resolution is sharp, and there are several predefined display settings from which to choose. I use "text," but "gaming" and others are also available, and users can define their own. It also comes with something that's increasingly rare--a full color glossy manual, written in recognizable English, like Sceptre chose to take pity on someone with an M.A. in English who was living in a van down by the river. Why don't all tech companies with a North American presence do this? Consumers would be happy, and grinding poverty would be reduced. I'm knocking off a star because of the small transformer at the plug-in end of the power cord, which needlessly takes up real estate on your power bar and/or wall outlet. Also, the on/off and other buttons are on the back of the monitor but are all the same shape and have no tactile differences, so it's easy to confuse them. First off, I’m always skeptical of getting a monitor or TV, and need to see the product myself IN PERSON before I purchase displays because I am a snob about the picture quality and color fluidity for not only PC games, but entertainment and work purposes. I bought this in the Amazon Prime Sale so I got a heck of a discount, almost 50% off. This is the newer model of these gaming monitors, so no need to look for the newest product (as of July 2021). Even if you buy this monitor at MSRP, you’re getting exactly what you pay for. I was surprised at the pixel and refresh rate quality the moment I plugged it into my 3090. Before I was using a curved gaming Samsung Odyssey monitor, great picture quality on that as well, but the difference is very clear. I think Samsung focuses more on hues of blue, where this monitor is focused on warmer colors like yellows, reds, and browns. White and black colors are optimized very well, which makes choosing brightness for your games much easier. No more upping the brightness because you can’t see. The HDR10 on this is some of the best quality HDR I’ve seen on a gaming monitor except for another high end Samsung Odyssey my brother owns. But keep in mind, the Samsung one ran him around $1100 and you can get just about the same quality on this LG monitor for ~$800 less. Be sure to change the refresh rate in your setting to get the maximum quality out of this monitor. The rest is up to you :)

I bought this to pair with a Pixel Slate i7 model. If you've tried to do this, you know how difficult it is to find one compatible with whatever Google's got going on in the USB-C ports! It powered up correctly the first time and is doing the job!

Pros:
- It works with the Slate! This also means it works with Pixelbook and should be fine across any Chromebook or regular laptop.
- The Slate will power it without needing external power, which indicates other laptops will do the same! External charger isn't needed.
- USB-C ports are located on both sides of the monitor.
- Included cover seems durable enough and works as a stand with two angles.
- All cables needed are included, as is a USB charger.

Cons:
- Colors are a little washed out in normal mode, a little oversaturated in HDR mode. Gamers might balk at this but it's fine for regular work.
- The 3-way menu button is horrible. The contacts inside are either loose or too close together and register movements when you don't move them, or go the wrong way. You can't be gentle with it, I've found you must smack the button with authority to make the intended press register correctly. Don't know if it's a defect or just the build quality at this price.
- HDMI-mini instead of HDMI, and it's thick enough to accommodate a regular HDMI port.
- 3/8 inch thick without cover, slightly over 1/2 inch thick with it is a bit thick for a monitor without a battery. It could be thinner...

Overall:
- If you're pairing this with the Pixel Slate, buy this one and be done. You'll have significant problems finding another compatible monitor!
- If the thickness isn't a turn-off this is a good monitor at a decent price.
- If you're gaming, you might choose a different model.

While it technically works with my iPad, it doesn’t have any functionality, but only mirrors exactly what is happening on my iPad. Because my tablet is significantly larger than this display, it’s not helpful. It is not compatible with my iPhone 13 pro max.

The resolution is quite good, though the sound is horrendously bad, and so I recommend using headphones. There doesn’t appear to be any internal battery, so expect that to suck some power, making this less than ideal for travel.

Included items: HDMI cable, USB-C—>USB-C cable, standard USB—>USB-C cable, little adapter box thing to plug into an outlet, adjustable plastic stand, screen protector + microfiber cloth that you have to apply, which does not go so well because it’s almost too big for the screen.

For the size and lack of internal battery or quality sound, I can’t say this is the best value out there, but it is pretty good, and I really appreciate that it can work on Mac or PC. 4.5 stars rounded up to five! I took a little video of it playing a YouTube video, so you can hear the sound and see what the picture looks like, as well as how it navigates.

Unlike some other LG models (and possibly an earlier revision of this model), the VESA mount on the 27UD58 is not inset. LG has installed a plastic cover to fill the cavity, effectively making the VESA mount flush with the surface of the monitor. Because of this plastic cover you have to go down 5mm in the holes to reach the actual threaded screw holes. I tried prying the plastic cover out but was unsuccessful so I ended up buying 16mm screws: [[ASIN:B01BBRA7EE uxcell M4x16mm Stainless Steel Truss Phillips Head Machine Screws 50pcs]].

From my own research as well as the questions answered by the manufacturer, this is in fact NOT a cut down 27GL850-B, but a slightly updated LG 27GL83A-B. The only difference as noted from it is some of the OSD options as well the lower quality stand it comes with. The panel quality is on par with the other model. This does not include the nano IPS coating of the 27GL850-B.

Onto my own experience, the monitor itself comes in a nondescript box noting its name and with the LG ultragear logo on it. Included is a large wall wart as its stand and a decently long DP cable.
After getting it set up, I was surprised to see that the panel I got was of great quality having both no dead pixels as well as some outstanding panel uniformity for an IPS of its class. The backlight bleed was negligible and the blacks themselves were very good. (at least for my unit)

This is where my positives end, as to get the best picture out of this requires a large amount of tinkering within the OSD, as the pre-calibrated profiles it came with were horrendous. Mostly crushing the blacks and leaving out detail in dark areas. The panel comes calibrated relatively dim in all profiles. I could mainly chalk this down to a possible bias, as the old TN panel I had previously been using for the past 5 years may have not been the most color accurate. Do not expect a perfect white either, as no matter how I calibrate it, the backlight always exhibits either a very slighly greenish or bluish tint depending on profile. Personally, this does not annoy me, but I would look elsewhere if you think that it may be an issue. (this could also be down to the panel I got in particular aswell)
Once calibrated though, I would say I am very much satisfied with the picture quality.

One item of note though, I would not recommend that you use the included stand as IPS glow is very apparent at the fixed angle that the stand is at. A VESA compatible mount is a requirement in my opinion as the stand it comes with although built well and aesthetically pleasing, has literally no available adjustments.

Finally, with motion clarity, this is another thing that this monitor is very good at. At 144hz and with the 'fast' OD option, there is no noticeable motion blur from what I could see. Very impressive for an IPS panel.

Do note though, the advertised 1ms response time only applies for the 'fastest' OD setting, which causes a great deal of overshoot on the pixels making the already good lack of blur into a mess of visual oatmeal.
The 'fast' OD setting already is responsive enough for me not to notice a difference. Just keep in mind the advertised 1ms response time is more likely to be around the normal 4ms for IPS panels, at least if you want decent motion clarity.

Both G sync and HDR work as expected on this panel, but don't expect the HDR to be particularly good, as this is still only an edglit panel.
G-sync seemingly does work from the advertised VRR amount from its 48hz to 144hz. There are no complaints to be had on that front, just make sure you use the DP connector.

This monitor, being my first foray into 144hz as well as IPS technology came as both a shock and a pleasant surprise. I add this with the caveat that I would not recommend buying this with its full $400 price tag. If you can get it on sale, you are unlikely to regret it, but if not, just go for the LG 27GL83A-B, to save $20 and get a better stand as these are basically the exact same monitor bar the stand.

I use this on a MacBook Pro for building presentations, editing documents and some graphic design (photo editing, photoshop, inDesign...). No gaming. Lots of steaming video. It works well and greatly increased my workstation “real estate.” I did have to download a patch and watch a couple youtube videos to unpack and install a better display setting, but that is really the fault of Apple, not this monitor -- a lot of people need to install a patch to make external monitors work better on Macs. To be clear, the monitor worked great without the patch, it just has better detail when the patch is installed.

this monitor has a DisplayPort out feature that allows you to daisy chain monitors, that is a huge plus (unfortunately the monitor you are connecting to must also have a DisplayPort in.... no adapters or converters -- passive OR active -- will work. This is a DisplayPort problem, not the fault of this nice monitor).

I’ve read some reviews that complain about light bleed…. I see no problem there, but maybe I’m just not cool enough to complain about “light bleed.” The Black on this is very nice.

The not-so-good parts: This monitor only has one DisplayPort in, one HDMI in, one classic VGA in, and one DVI in. The HDMI in also functions as a MHL in. Would have been a lot more useful if the monitor had multiple HDMI in ports and/or multiple DisplayPort in ports... the monitor has a split screen function (picture by picture). This allows you to have two sources displayed side-by-side HOWEVER, you can't really do that because you can only plug in ONE HDMI source and ONE DisplayPort and ONE DVI.... If all your devices use HDMI, you can only employ one…. Soon everything will utilize DisplyPort, but you will only be able to plug in just one…. Also, if you are already using the HDMI in source, you obviously cannot use it's MHL function.... so again, more would be better and having only one "in" port for each type makes the PBP function mostly unusable. And to put the final nail in the coffin regarding the monitor’s PBP: why no remote? This does not come with a remote; you have to toggle from screen to screen, or engage the PBP function, by hand… it is a large monitor, not necessarily placed in arm’s reach—so if you want to use that function you have to climb over your desk or up the wall you mounted it on—just a bad BAD decision to not include a remote.

[[VIDEOID:36bcb1a3aef20ac8a01d6fd290d384fe]] Portable monitors that are USB bus powered (and display connected) are nothing new but a 2K version at this prices was a great deal. I only wish it was not currently unavailable so more people could get it.

Monitor comes in a nice box with all the cables you could need Type C to Type C - power and display, Type A to Type C - power only, and HDMI to mini-HDMI - display only. Monitor allows input switching between both Type C ports and the single HDMI. It also comes with a cover/stand (PU Leather) that uses magnets to hold case to monitor and then rest of case folds under/around for stability and angle preferences. There is also a power supply included which I appreciate on one level consumer flexibility and wish it was not on another (environmental) level.

I am using it as a second monitor on a old Dell Inspiron 13" 2n1 so HDMI and Type A to Type C for monitor then keyboard and mouse dongle connected to the laptop ports . I mention since the video of me walking around the monitor (above the laptop) looks pretty messy and is due to older laptop needing two cable to connect monitor and and the other connections. Also not the screen refresh issue off axis on laptop but not monitor. I have connected monitor to a proper modern laptop (HP Dragonfly G2) and it is one short cable but don't have images of that supper clean setup.

Monitor display is crazy good looking since 13.3" 2K has a high DPI so text is crisp, images clear, and overall makes the laptops 13.4" 1080P look pretty bad when side by side. Monitor is very light and in the video you see it is connect to laptop via two mounts, the mounts are for using an iPad or iPhone as a sidecar off the MacBook display. I have used the monitor in portrait orientation using only one clamp. I used two since I did not want to cover the Windows Hello camera by putting it in the middle. Again I mention this to highlight how the monitor is very light and easy to transport. The speakers are not super loud as expected for something this small but are good for what they do. Monitor has HDR setting which is the auto color setting or you can do color via manual adjustment.

All settings are accessed via a single toggle wheel, you push in to get to the menu and then toggle up and down for your selections. There is a reset option if the settings go out of control or or off that you need to get back to defaults. There is a power button if you want to turn it off without disconnecting the connections or placement. Also a 3.5mm jack which I have not used so no idea if it will accept a TRS and TRRS or just stereo no mic TRS.

The one issue I have found with the monitor is the inability for it to report unsupported display resolutions. I connected my Samsung S10+ and its highest resolution WQHD+ (3040 x 1440) via USB C to USB C and the monitor would flash on and off trying to display something it could not fit on the screen. If it could down scale the image and display with window boxing it (black bars top and bottom) that requires more complex image handing system and equals higher cost. So when it tried and tried but failed, I lowered the resolution to FHD+ (2280x1080) and it was able to display since it was below max native resolution for the panel. I don't see this as a problem worth a star off or similar since it is reflective of an edge use case and also the pricing. I wanted to note it since someone else could connect a phone and be above max resolution and wonder what was happening. A full size monitor would likely report input out of range and not try to display anything.

Overall love the monitor image, the portability, multiple use cases, and functionality. My old eyes might want it in a 15.4" version but that again does not detract from how great it is in the 13.3" model.

**Disclosure: I received a sample product from the Amazon Vine program for an honest and unbiased review of the product.

This continued after I switched to the power plan to maximum. After it kept happening I did an online search and found that Lenovo also has a power mgr, and when I went into it even on the max performance plan the monitor was set brightness 0. I adjusted this on all the machines and now all is good. Odd because everyone has dual displays, and only this display dimmed. Whatever, it is fixed. So just beware often times CPUs have two power managers

This monitor is outstanding for gaming and 4K HDR content, but it's not flawless.

Gaming: Magnificent! Graphics and animations look terrific and input lag is very low. The high refresh rate is great if you have a GPU that can provide it. I have a 3070ti, so I can max out the 165FPS limit at 4K in some modern titles like Forza Horizon 5 and it is exhilarating!

General visual quality: looks great from the right viewing angle and distance. The curve distorts the image somewhat and doesn't really add to the immersion since it's not an ultrawide that would extend to your peripheral vision. The local dimming zones allow for deep blacks with very minimal blooming around bright objects. There is a significant degree of "black crush" that causes a loss of detail in shadows and dark scenes in movies. Colors look great but the monitor needs calibration and there is no adjustment for white balance. This monitor doesn't reach anywhere near the 2000 nits advertised. My QN90A tv is far brighter with 1800 nits even when not at maximum brightness.

Build quality: Very good. The stand is easily adjustable for height and swiveling but it does wobble. The control panel buttons are a bit squishy and the monitor wobbles when you press them.

Aesthetics: Will look stylish on your desk and it has tasteful RGB accent lights that can be controlled through the monitor's control panel.

Overall I would rate this monitor 4.5 stars. It's top of the line, but not perfect. The black crush and misleading brightness claims bring the score down from a 5/5.

I decided to completely re-write my review. Everyone who purchases this monitor, and is not a seasoned pro, should spend some time carefully reading the detailed TFT-Central review. It takes time to get to know this monitor. I am still getting to know it but I have learned a few things. Out of the box the brightness on mine was either 80 or 100 percent. This setting isn't necessary to get the most out of this monitor. At max brightness looking at this monitor is like looking at the sun, it could burn your eyes and give you a tan. No wonder there is back-light bleed! After spending some time reading the above mentioned review, I altered the settings similar to what they did. I dropped the brightness to 50% and that made the back-light bleed issue go away for the most part(the two pics are before and after). Truth is, I could crank down the brightness even further but I am all good now. I originally gave the monitor 2 stars and said I was would have to return it. I now give it 5 stars and will be keeping it. Fabulous ultra clear picture quality and serious eye candy in game or watching a 4k movie (I have a 4k player on my desktop). I am still trying to figure out the color space possibilities, response times, adaptive sync, etc., etc. It will take a while to get the monitor where I want it, or learn how I want to change the settings depending on what I am doing.
The only potential minor cons for me, aside from a few plastic pieces, are:

1. OSD access. All monitor manufacturers should just go ahead and copy the LG method with the centrally located joystick. The LG joystick is completely intuitive, can be easily accessed and used in the dark with one hand, doesn't require you to hold the monitor still while using it even if the monitor is mounted on an arm. The buttons on the right rear of this Acer monitor are hard to pick out and the monitor moves around on the arm I have it mounted on unless I hold it still with my other hand. No fun!
2. Dual monitor quirks. This may be the case for all dual monitor setups, but it seems like I have had more black screens upon starting my computer than I have had in the past with other dual monitor setups. Sometimes I have to restart my system or reboot one or both monitors. Just a quirky issue that is a very minor annoyance.
3. Settings resetting automatically. I have noticed that upon exiting HDR mode in the OSD, the visual settings revert to Standard with brightness jumping back up to 80%, color temp going to "Warm", and a few other changes. Most likely user error on my part and a minor issue now that I am aware of it, but that brightness jumping back up might confuse someone if they don't catch it and the back-light bleed increases.

I am now very satisfied with this monitor and highly recommend it. It isn't perfect but extremely satisfying at this juncture in time. Eventually, this technology will be old news. For now, great job Acer and kudos for bringing a more affordable 4k high refresh monitor to the market.

Any time you scroll on any website, you will see this massive trail of whatever color that seems to linger for a few hundred milliseconds. The computer mouse will also ghost. You can find videos of this monitor doing this on youtube.

Also worth noting if you use this for a dual-monitor setup with the DisplayPort cable (which is the only way to get 165Hz), your desktop icons will shift all over the place every time your screens turn off or enters sleep mode. In pandemic times--and we're not ever going completely back to normal--the ability to make your workplace look like your workspace is critical (<--feel free to use that tagline, Mobile Pixels!). At very least, working as conveniently as before assures your boss that you are as productive at home as at work. This monitor extends my laptop--a 13" MacBook Air--to a two-monitor setup like I have at the office so I can do all those multi-screen tasks we've gotten used to. It attaches to the laptop cover with magnetsm, and slides out to create a screen that's at the same tilt as your normal screen, right next to it. And it's lightweight so it adds very little to your bag when you carry it around. I extend my desktop (versus mirroring, which you can also do) and the colors and resolution are easily matched. You can use it standing on its own next to the computer as well. A feature I love (but haven't used yet)--when you attach it, you can flip it totally around (still attached) and, mirroring your own screen, it becomes a small-scale presentation screen on the back of your own screen so others in the room can view your screen along with you. Mainly, the plug-and-play nature of the screen is so foolproof that it was really simple to get working. When I had a question, the CEO him/herself responded practically instantanteously, and graciously. Inexpensive LCDs have Twisted Nematic (TN) panels, which change brightness and color tint with viewing angle and are not very fast. MVA and PVA try to fix those problems with TN.  IPS (in plane switching) panels are the most color accurate but also not fast ... and they're expensive.  I really wanted a VP930b which has an IPS panel, because I do photo editing, but went with the VP920b, which I've read has a TN panel, due to price. It's quite bright, crystal clear, and when set to the sRGB preset color accuracy is surprisingly good!  The last couple of bright white shades are difficult to distinguish; dark grays down to "black" (actually, just very dark grey - TN panels don't really get to black - keep the room lights on and you won't notice) are nicely distinguishable and are a good neutral grey - not tinted. Color/brightness variation with viewing angle is there but is substantially less than I expected, less than I've seen in some other LCD  monitors - still, you will want to sit directly in front of it. All in all, it's a keeper; I am pleasantly surprised with what I got for this price.

I've used Dell monitors before and liked them, and since this one was less than $300 on Black Friday, I ordered it as a second monitor for my Mac Studio. The main monitor is the Studio Display. I need accurate colors for photo editing, and Lightroom works best with 2 monitors. The was one trick need for setup. Initially, I plugged the USB-C connector for the Dell into one of the 4 Thunderbolt ports on the Studio Monitor. The Dell didn't detect a signal and the Mac didn't detect the Dell. I plugged the Dell in using an HDMI cable, and it worked fine. So then I plugged the Dell's USB-C directly into a Thunderbolt port on the back of the Mac Studio. Then in worked fine. So the Dell might have problems if there is another device between the computer and the Dell in a Thunderbolt daisy chain.

Compared with the Mac Studio Display, which is a 5K Retina display, the Dell has accurate colors, but as would be expected, they just don't have the brilliance and pop of the Studio Display, which costs 5 times as much. However, getting accurate colors after calibration with my Color Monkey is better than what I had with my previous display (Philips) which looked nice, but the colors couldn't be made to match up.

So I'm giving 4 stars for now (will revise if the Dell develops problems in the next few months) but for the price, I don't think you could get a better display. I like the number of input ports, and it also has some USB-3 ports which those using it with a laptop will find useful.

The displayport on my 32" unit failed after about 10 months of use. So keep that in mind when looking at this unit. It does offer an HDMI 2.0 port, but most modern graphics cards only come with 1 hdmi port nowadays. Initial impression. This review is ONLY after one day, so take it with some reservation. I'll try and update it a few months from now. I bought the 24 inch version. The OEM box (inside a typical huge Amazon box) was remarkably small and light. Everything packed well. Comes with both HGMI and DisplayPort cables. MacBook user beware if your laptop uses the mini-DP Thunderbolt ports you will need an adapter. Also comes with a CD, but it was easier to just go to LG's website and download the manual in PDF. The stand looks tedious, but it actually was quite easy to assemble. You will need a Phillips screwdriver. It also has a convenient cable clip to gather the cables together. I found it to be a little short for my desk and chair hight, so you may want to place the stand on a another small monitor stand.I connected my MacBook Pro 13 (2005) via DisplayPort, an older Sony laptop via HDMI and an Amazon FireStick via HDMI. On boot up, it recognized all three devices without changing any configurations. MacOS does recognize the monitor and loaded the correct config file. It does support the maximum "scaled" resolution (4K) that my MacBook supports, however I cannot read anything that small on a 24 inch monitor, so have it backed off by two settings. This is an Apple issue, not a monitor issue, as Apple still will not support global font size adjustments like Windows does. However I have tested 4K Youtube videos using a 4K-capable web browser (Chrome) and Vimeo 4k videos and they play fine. There can be some lagging at the highest setting, but that's because despite supporting 4K, my graphics card in the Mac is still not able to fully support 4K as Apple claims. The Sony running Windows supports its max resolution just fine, which is 2K. The 1080p Amazon FireStick works fine with both Prime Video and SlingTV. The setup button is a bit annoying, being a joystick button located under the bottom of the monitor. It's taking a bit of getting used to. I do not use the built-in sound (I use BT speakers connected directly to my laptops and the FireStick) so I cannot comment on audio. Over the next few weeks I'll play with the settings and calibration and update this as needed.

I bought this directly from Dell because I was worried about the warranty issues that people have mentioned. I received revision A03, which fixes the wake from sleep issue. Although I did have that issue once. I had to unplug the power to get the monitor to wake. Hopefully it doesn't happen again.

PROS

- Price. This is the cheapest 4K monitor and it provides such a great quality.
- The picture quality is amazing. I didn't test the colorspace, but the colors really pop.
- The tilt, swivel, and height adjustments are great. The adjustments are very firm so once you move it, it won't feel saggy or wobbly.
- Plenty of connections. I'm using the supplied cable from my PC (DisplayPort) to the monitor (mini-DisplayPort). No issues at all. I'm getting 3840x2160 @ 60hz no problem. My video card is a nVidia GTX 970. I also tested the HDMI and it can do 3840x2160 @ 30hz. I have an Early 2011 MackBook Pro, and I hooked it up using the DisplayPort cable and I was able to get 3840x2160 @ 30hz (which is the maximum supported by the MacBook Pro). So no issues with a Mac or PC.
- The 3.5mm audio out port is very nice. The audio will come through the DisplayPort or HDMI, and the monitor will pass the audio to the 3.5mm port. I need to hook up my M-Audio speakers to test the quality of the DAC.
- Gives off very little heat. When I put my hand over the vents on the back there is very, very small amount of heat. I'm hoping that's a good thing, meaning it's energy efficient. Hopefully the heat isn't being trapped inside.

CONS

- I found 1 stuck pixel. With such a high resolution, it is hard to notice any pixels. I was able to notice the stuck pixel when I was installing Windows 10 and the image went black. You can only notice the pixel when you are very close to the monitor. Once I move 12 inches back, I cannot see the pixel at all so it's no biggie. I hope there aren't any other stuck pixels that I haven't noticed. Can anyone comment on whether I should get Dell to replace it under warranty?
- The bezel doesn't sit flush to the panel on the left side. So if I put my cheek against the LCD panel and face the left, I can see a gap between the panel and the bezel. I am able to see the individual LEDs' glow. If I push on the bezel then the gap closes. So the bezel is wavy, but not easily noticeable.
- Does not auto select the input. It has 3 inputs, so for example, if I have my PS3 hooked up to the HDMI and PC through mini-DisplayPort. I have both running, and I turn of the PS3. It does not automatically switch to the mini-DisplayPort connection which is active. You have to manually change the input.

Because of the 3 cons listed above, I took off one start.

OTHER THOUGHTS

- I think this issue exists with all IPS matte screen panels, but when the screen is black, you can see a glow on the screen. I would prefer a glossy panel to fix this annoying issue.
- The bezel looks outdated. I like the bezels on their UltraSharp models. It's thinner on 3 sides which make it look much nicer.
- The OSD is just scaled up, so the icons and text are jagged as if it's MS-DOS. They should have done the OSD at 4K to provide a better look and feel to it.

I'll update this review periodically as I use is more and find new things.

UPDATE 2015-10-26 - In regards to small text people are complaining about. With 4K monitors you need to set the DPI scaling at a minimum of 150%. I have mine set to 200%. Majority of the apps look incredible! I use MPC-HC as my media player, and for that app I disabled DPI scaling because video and pictures are not supposed to be scaled. You can google how to disable DPI scaling for specific apps. The default picture viewer app in Windows 10 handles images properly (it does not scale them).

I love that it not only has speakers but it also has a physical volume control level as most of the time you have to go into the menu to change volume on built-in speakers. It works great as a power source for the Raspberry Pi 4 with a USB C connector. This is great as I only need one power outlet for the whole system. I really only have one complaint and a possible suggestion... my complaint is the HDMI interface. I don't like having several adapters and/or different HDMI cables to connect everything. I'd prefer something a little cleaner. With that said, this is not a complaint of the monitor at all but more of the 3 different options for HDMI that are out there. A suggestion might be to include a battery right into the system so it can be truly portable.
