This item is really a class
I’m using it with Samsung Dex and my several raspberry pi. Working exactly as expected and the picture quality is above my expectations for its price.

The Bad:
Like others have said, Asus has hasn't quite nailed the whole "quality control" thing with this monitor. My monitor shipped with one pixel that is stuck and either stays off or green depending on what's asked of it. I would never have noticed it unless I ran a pixel test, but it's nonetheless frustrating given the cost of this monitor. The only other gripe I have is that the included USB cable is rigid and doesn't connect firmly to my laptop or the monitor itself. Simply shaking my desk causes the monitor to flicker. I replaced the included cable with a better one and the problem disappeared.

The Good:
This was the only monitor on the market that is anything approximating an all-around-good portable gaming monitor (trust me, I spent 3 months talking myself into just biting the bullet and buying this one). It was literally plug-and-play with my 2020 Razer Blade Base, and now that I've put it through benchmarking I can confidently say that it's everything it is advertised as. If this (or a comparable monitor) would release at ~$350, I think it would be one of the most popular monitors of all time. I would personally buy another at that price, and I'd be okay with a lack of battery and even 144hz at that dollar amount.

One final note for prospective buyers:
If you have even the slightest inkling that you might want the package with the tripod, BUY THE ONE WITH THE TRIPOD. I tried a few other tripods and ran into a variety of issues ranging from "not strong enough" to "not balanced enough" to "can't adjust angles enough" and I can confidently say that you're not going to find a decent tripod that will actually serve this purpose for less than $50 - and that's pushing it. Just spend the extra $50 and be done with it.

Takeaway:
If you think you want this monitor, you do. It's not worth the asking price, but neither are the $300 travel monitors.

I bought this on impulse because it was on sale. I'd wanted a dual monitor setup in my home office for a while, and since my existing monitor was a 22.5" Samsung 2253LW, I thought this would be a good second monitor.

I was totally wrong.

The 2253LW, which I've been using since early 2008, is an excellent monitor. The color and value ranges are very, very smooth, the blacks are dark, and it's large enough to work comfortably on. I'm a graphic designer, so the monitor is one of my most important pieces of hardware, and the 2253LW definitely measured up.

So why was buying the 2333SW such a bad idea?

Because the 2333SW is a work of art. I knew that the new screen would be larger, but they're actually the same height (the original might even be a little taller, the new 2333 is just wider). The problem is that as great as the 2253LW is, the 2333SW is sooooo much better. I had no idea that white could be so much whiter, colors could be so much crisper, and the picture could be so much clearer.

So now I've got to get rid of the old monitor and buy another 2333SW to replace it. Thanks for nothing, Samsung.

I find no compromises with this display, period. For years, I waited for LCD technology to improve enough to pull me away from my very color accurate, very responsive (and very heavy, very power-hungry) CRTs. The IPS panel in this Planar represents the technological leap I'd been waiting for--and it was worth the wait! No dithering colors like 6-bit TN panels. No painful refresh rates like PVA and MVA panels. No color shifts or viewing angle limitations, either. That last point is crucial--how can you accurately adjust the colors on one of your photos if the colors shift when you move your head? How would you know which angle is "right"? This monitor lets me do it all: accurate color for photography, accurate gamma and geometry for reviewing patient x-rays and CT scans, and fast refresh rates for watching DVDs.

The USB hub does work, contrary to the other review below. The upstream port is located on the back, tucked up and behind the plastic facade along with the DVI and VGA ports. It is hard to see (I needed a flashlight) but it is there. Endure the minor pain of wiggling the supplied USB cable into it once, and you'll forever have convenient access to 4 USB 2.0 ports on the left edge of the screen.

It would be nice to have a white-balance preset so that I could choose, say, 6500K. But I was able to work around that and get it calibrated nonetheless.

Overall, this is a terrific display.

I'm a software developer and have used a lot of monitors, mostly Dell. This one is my first 4K monitor due to the price and it being IPS. I'm using it on a Windows 7 64-bit system with an AMD 7700 series graphics card.

Overall I am pleased with this monitor except for one major issue and some minor ones. First the minor issues:

Minor:
* I have at least one dead pixel. Fortunately it is very small and difficult to notice unless you look for it.
* Lightbleed is there but acceptable because I doubt it's visible during most normal use
* Some parts of the screen have a slight yellow tint, but again, it's difficult to see unless you change the screen to all white and look for it.
* When powering on with the monitor power button, sometimes my computer detects this monitor as a lower resolution monitor (2560x1440) instead of a 4K monitor. If I turn it off and then on again with the monitor power button then it will often correct this issue and be detected as a 4K monitor and thus run at 4K resolution.

Major:
* When turning on, the monitor's DisplayPort connection seems to occasionally go dead and the computer won't detect the display. The solution seems to be to turn the monitor's power off by pulling the plug and then plugging it back in. Other people have reported this annoying issue as well and that exchanges to another monitor did not solve it. Fortunately it doesn't happen all that often but it does happen and is quite annoying when it does.

Other:
* It's HDMI 1.2, not HTMI 2.0 so if you want 60Hz at full 4k resolution then the DisplayPort connector must be used.

So overall 3 stars due to the minor and major issues. I would wait for a fix or a newer revision if you can wait for Dell to get some of the bugs out, but if you understand the potential problems with the first revision of this new model and are willing to take a chance and need (or really want) a good 4K IPS monitor at a relatively low price then go for it.

UPDATE 2015-04-28: Looks like there is an A01 revision out now. That may address the issues I've mentioned but I have not been able to test it for myself since I am still stuck with the original version.

I was needing a new some more screen real estate. I had two 1080p monitors. My primary monitor is a 23 in acer 144hz monitor and my secondary was a cheap small HP monitor (not sure what size but definitely smaller than my main monitor). I thought about upgrading to a third monitor but I was running out of desk space and the HP monitor didn't have any mounting on the back of it. So instead of getting 2 new monitors that I could mount, I opted for an ultra wide.

I DO NOT USE THIS AS MY MAIN DISPLAY. I do a lot of gaming and I care more about refresh rate than resolution so I use my 1080p 144hz monitor for my gaming needs. I use the the sceptre as my second monitor for discord, music, web browsing etc. It's great for when I occasionally stream I can have all my necessary windows open on the screen and fitting comfortably (discord, obs, alerts, twitch chat).

The resolution is really nice. It's hard to find other monitors at this size with a 1440p res, most are 1080. It allows me to fit a ton of things on it. I didn't find any dead pixels on it upon arrival and I couldn't see any visual issues with a static screen. The refresh rate is nice, but not butter. There is an obvious difference when going from my 144 to this 100hz. The difference is even more apparent from the small amount of testing I did for gaming. I would not recommend this as a main monitor for gaming. The colors are also not super accurate so if you work a lot with photos or film, this may not be ideal for editing colors. I will say the wide screen is great for music production or editing videos (as long as you color grade the videos on a more color accurate monitor first). If you can find the media that is in the proper aspect ratio (21:9) it looks amazing on this display. The extra resolution along with the size is very immersive.

The monitor also comes with speakers but I don't ever use them so I can't comment. It has 3 HDMI ports and 1 display port, although if you want to use 100hz you need to use the display port. It does allow for multiple inputs and you can split the screen to display both. You can't tell from the pictures listed, but there is VESA mounting on the back. Currently I have mine mounted to my desk which I highly recommend as the default stand takes up a lot of space. The default stand is metal and has a good weight to it, but is a bit on the wobbly side and doesn't offer much for adjustment.

All in all this is a great prodcut for my use case. If you are just looking for some extra screen space that comes at a nice resolution and price, this is for sure the product for you.

Well, my major problem with this monitor is that text is not as sharp as my old CRT used to be, and that seems to cause me eye strain and headaches with prolonged viewing, also looking at the screen is like stareing at a fluorescent bulb (I don't know if it is a common thing with lcd sreens). I tried different resolutions and 1024*768 is the best compromise (800*600 is very blurry and 1280*1024 is the sharpest setting but text is way too small). I tried other LCD monitors at the store and I noticed that this text bluriness is a common feature with most LCD screens of many brands, some are better than others and this Proview model falls in the middle. All monitors I tested obtained the sharpest text at 1280*1024 (Sony models were among the best). Therefore I conclude that this monitor is good enough for occasional viewing, but if you do a lot of reading or surf the web a lot, than look for something else. Overall: DECENT!

This portable monitor is easy to set up. Just plug & play, no need to extra driver. This laptop screen extender is easy to carry as it is portable & small enough to carry with my laptop bag. So, I can easily carry when I travel to anywhere. This is the biggest advantage of this extender. It easily fit with my 15 inch laptop. So far for me, this extender is very good one for my working purpose.

+ Form factor: Slim with a slightly thicker base, nicely integrated with the cover/stand.
- Portable use only: No vesa holes for use with a custom mount. Only the magnetic cover/stand.
+ The cover can be used at a steeper more vertical angle (viewed across) or a flatter angle (viewed downward). There are rubber feet on the cover and the monitor itself to ensure a stable stand.
+ Matte screen finish reduces reflections
+ The monitor’s construction and bezel feels metallic
+/- Like most portable monitors, uses micro hdmi even though full size hdmi can also fit in a slim monitor like this
+ Volume control is a joystick like dial, but the volume only moves at one speed. The center of the dial is the options/confirm button, while the power button doubles as source select and back depending on context. You might need to reference the manual for first use as the buttons aren’t labelled, but is overall quite intuitive.
+ Power draw from USB: 11.1W max, 4.9W min, as measured by a power meter. It is good that this monitor has adjustable backlight, as some portable monitors do not have adjustable backlight, where the brightness option only affects color and looks washed out at max brightness.
+ 60hz is a normal frame rate. 1080p is a good resolution for this size. The monitor was also able to downsize higher resolution input from my laptop.
+/- HDR is not a advertised feature. There is an HDR option in the menu but the monitor only supports 8 bit with dithering.
+/- In terms of value, I’m seeing full price of $199.99 with a “prime price” of $159.99 as of this review. For the feature set the price of $160 is fair, but $200 would be a bit steep considering at that price point you can add touch screen or wireless video features.

I had a 27 inch 1440p IPS that worked fine, but every time I glanced at the TV I couldn't help but envy the contrast on the other screen. I figured I've been playing on this monitor for 5 years so a monitor upgrade to go along with a PC upgrade was reasonable.

I decided to go 21:9 this time around for a more immersive experience. I also wanted to go VA for the contrast. I know it has worse viewing angles, but I was the only one staring at the monitor 99% of the time anyways.

I've only used it for about 2 days so far but it's been amazing. I hooked it up to my monitor arm because it's 75mm Vesa compatible so I can't speak to how the stand works.

I used the calibration settings recommended on TFTCentral and it looks amazing. I also checked out a monitor testing site and as far as I can tell mine came with no dead pixels.

I set the settings to fast overdrive to reduce smearing without introducing overshoot (fastest might introduce overshoot) and also enabled Gsync trough the Nvidia Panel Settings. Played a few games of Rocket League and Mortal Kombat 11 and it's just amazing MK11 in particular has darker visuals so the added contrast and Quantom Dot colors make for an amazing experience. You also get to see more of the stage at once, just wish I could turn off the HUD for when I play locally. I also didn't noticed any smearing. This could be because I'm not predisposed to noticing it and because VA panels have gotten much better over the years

The OSD takes a little while to get used the and the speakers aren't great (monitor speakers never are). As I mentioned before, the viewing angles make it look washed out if you aren't positioned in front of it, so a monitor arm helps with aligning it to your specific height.

Movies look amazing on it too, especially when they fill up the whole screen.

I'll update my review in case something goes wrong with it, and if so I hope the 2 year warranty covers it. For now, I'm very happy with it.

I travel a lot for work. Having this extra monitor for productivity purposes has been super helpful. It's easy to connect to my Surface Pro tablet and Lenovo laptop. It fits in my 45L backpack easily. Carrying around an extra 5lbs may or may not be convenient for you.

I have a small list of minor problems with it:

First are the USB C cables. The ones it came with are too short. They're only 3' long. I think 6 ft would be better for my usage because I like having the flexibility to move it to the left or right side of where ever I'm working. The 3' cable restricts me to only placing it on the left side due to where the USB ports are located on my laptop and tablet. The problem could also be solved if USB C ports were available on both sides of the monitor. Right now, they're only on the right side of the monitor.

The USB cable required to connect it requires a higher wattage. The regular USB C cables I use for charging my android phones won't work. The USB cable needed has to be of a higher quality. I'll need to research and shop around for a longer cable that can handle the wattage requirement.

The USB C cables it came with aren't angled. That causes the monitor to be a little farther than I'd like. I'd rather place it right next to my other screen. The last thing I noticed is that the cover tends to move around a lot. It's not always flat and flush against the monitor. I find myself having to adjust it be perfect every time when I'm ready to work and when I'm getting ready to pack it.

The last annoying thing is the cover. It works. I notice it moves a lot and it moves easily. When I put the monitor away, it's usually crooked I have to make a quick adjustment so it's flush against screen and all edges. When I staging the monitor, I found it awkward to placing it down and standing properly. Sometimes the flap is a little too thick when folded and it causes the monitor to be a little higher than I want. Eventually I make the adjustments and it works out.

Overall I really like this monitor. The issues I listed are minor and I hope Usparkle can improve them in the near future. If you travel a lot or need to move a monitor around easily, this one works extremely well. I'll be proud to show it one off to my coworkers the next time I see them.

Pros
-Great for Travel

Cons
-USB C cables are too short (3')
-Requires higher quality USB C Cable
-USB C cable isn't angled
-Cover gets crooked easily. Makes standing the monitor imperfect sometimes.

Now you might be wondering, what do I mean by "was not acquired correctly by the seller?" I have no idea. The seller admitted to me on the phone that they knew their monitors were not covered by the warranty.

I followed Amazon's process and communicated with the seller via the messaging system. The item was returned. I do not have it. All of this is documented in the messages.

No refund was issued. No explanation was offered. I've called several times but can't seem to get anywhere. I paid for it. I returned it. Now I do not have the item or the money.

DO NOT PURCHASE DELL EQUIPMENT ON AMAZON IF THE WARRANTY MATTERS TO YOU.

It was great until worked. It does not power up all of a sudden. I contacted with LG and it promised me to send me return label. Nothing for a week. Sent email two times. No response. I chatted with customer service who created a reference number. He told that a return label will be email to me. Nothing. I contacted with Amazon. Amazon created a return label with refund money but took off 80 dollars.

I bought two of these, and out of the blue the other day a weird dotted line appeared horizontally on the monitor, along with a small vertical blue line intersecting with the horizontal line. (see picture)  I would not recommend this monitor since I have only had it since November (now April) and it is already malfunctioning.

Cannot get this monitor to consistently keep a signal on my Macbook Pro. Have tried multiple cables, power on/off, disconnecting power to no avail. My macbook recognizes the display as seen in my settings, but nothing comes on the screen and the light is amber. My screens will flash on and off like it is trying to connect but still nothing.

I don't have this issue on windows though it does take about 5 seconds longer to activate compared to my Dell monitor. Likely will return this

Also the image quality is absolutely terrible, very pixelated and can barely read text.

Wanted this, desperately, to work as advertised. I have an LG Gram 15 with Thunderbolt 3. According to the manufacturer, this monitor could be run on a single cable (USB-C to USB-C). I tried this method with several different USB cables I had around and none would work.

Ultimately, the only way I could get the device to work was using an HDMI cable to the monitor AND a power cord to the monitor. The power cord worked both from a standard USB-A port or the USB-C port. For HDMI, I used HDMI over USB-C, which worked (as long as there was separate power); I also tried HDMI from a dedicated HDMI port and that was also fine.

The thing I wanted most was a one cable solution, as advertised, and it failed in that respect. The monitor comes with a small metal stand that is adjustable, though the monitor isn't especially stable on it. Comes with a full sized HDMI cable, 3-feet. The problem with the bundled cable is it's just way too bulky to be travel friendly. Definitely a slimmer, braided cable, possibly shorter as well. For travel, monitor isn't going to be three feet from the computer. Maybe different if you're using for console gaming .

Edit: customer service reached out to me and resolved this issue, satisfactorily. Happy with the result!

The monitor itself is good, but the connector is THE DUMBEST thing I have ever seen, and it actually matters. If a piece of space dust a trillion miles away moves a little bit wrong, the monitor connector will come undone, because it's the loosest, most tenuous connection I've ever seen on a piece of electronic equipment.

I've occasionally dropped something (say, a stack of papers) on my desk, and my screen went black. That was all it took. It's taken me as long as three or four minutes to get the damn thing to stay in place, because unless I take my fingers off it with the delicacy of a surgeon, I'll knock the connection out again.

Yeah, it's just a dumb little thing, but it really impacts the user experience enough that I would encourage you to go with an equivalent monitor, because chances are it won't have this dumb little problem.

I needed a bigger screen for work and this screen doesn't disappoint. One downfall is the speaker, I wish it was louder. I didn't want to buy external speakers but I may have to. I have to hear peoplle talk. Otherwise, great product!

Was a little iffy about buying refurbished electronics, but this one is working great. It's actually bigger than the size I wanted and for a lower price. Great deal! Shipping was quick too. I love it.

I used this screen maybe 4-5 times and it worked OK. Yesterday, I tried to use it again it would not power on.  The indicator light is green indicating a full charge but the screen is dark and unresponsive. Checking the message boards I found this is a common issue. Contacted Asus Chat support and they told me they needed the serial number before they could issue an RMA. There is no serial number on the device and it won't start so I can't get it from the menu. They told me I would need the box.  Do NOT buy this or any Asus product. I will not buy another Asus product, nor will I let my clients buy Asus products.

The speakers actually sound pretty good. It's nice to have the brightness keyboard shortcuts working and light sensor for auto brightness. The downfall is the backlight. Be prepared for swaths of pink, swaths of green, swaths of blue, mainly visible when looking at something with a white or whitish background. It's kind of like the Aurora Borealis on this thing. I'm on my second one, and they both have it but in different places across the screen. The current one has a bright blue smear across the right side. Your sensitivity to this sort of thing may vary; just don't expect perfection if you order one of these.

I always try to buy from Amazon, but since they have a fulfillment center in my state, sometimes I am charged state tax (if what I'm buying is fulfilled by the fulfillment center in my state). Therefore, I sometimes get a better deal elsewhere. Although I bought these monitors from a competitor, I figured I would share my review anyway, because the majority of you can buy from Amazon and benefit from their amazing customer service without the fear of added tax.

I was initially going to get one of the Dell monitors, but the ones I was interested in have a glass screen. I remember my old CRT monitor, and how it was difficult dealing with the glare, so I opted for 3 of these monitors. Well, let me say that I am absolutely impressed!! I went from 3 24 inch monitors to 3 of these 27 inch monitors because my eyesight has become a bit worse over the years. These monitors really fit the bill. First and foremost, you can get these monitors for about $50 cheaper if you opt for the stand that cannot be adjusted. But I am so glad I opted for these. The stand quality is great, and you can easily flip them from horizontal to vertical! Also, if you get these, you probably won't need a monitor mount system, which costs the same as 1 monitor for a good one!

B&H sent these nicely packed and not 1 dead pixel on 3 monitors!!! Needless to say, I was very happy!! If you read reviews that say these monitors aren't crisp and clear, don't fear. Just turn off the vivid pixel and take the time to adjust them. Although, right out of the box, they were near perfect for me. There are a lot of menu options and these monitors are such a great bang for your buck!!! I can't give you specific details about the performance, because I am not very well versed on monitors, but for a guy with weakened eyesight (after staring at computer monitors for 25 years) this monitor is really impressive!! Oh, and they are really slick looking too. Thin bezels make them ideal for multi monitor setups!! Hope this helps in your decision...

I purchased the Mimo 710S for two applications. At work, I wanted a third monitor to dedicate to Outlook while my other two monitors were used for applications that require more screen real estate. I also wanted some elbow room for my netbook, to give room for notes or a chat window while I worked on the main screen. The Mimo 710S accomplishes both of these things.

Don't let the USB cord fool you--if your computer has USB 2.0 ports, then you only need to use the data cord, not the second integrated power cord. This is great for netbook use if, like me, your number of USB slots is limited.

Setup was simple on my work computer (Vista), a little more challenging on my netbook (XP). For those without an optical drive, the drivers are easily accessible online, and a simple reboot took care of my connection issues on the netbook. The work computer managed it all without a blink.

This is not a large monitor, and it doesn't pretend to be. The resolution is good enough for YouTube, and doesn't take up that much processor to run, even on my netbook. It is good for your ancillary applications that you don't want to have to ALT+Tab to check on, or to have video or something else you're monitoring while you work on the main screen.

I bought this off of Costco this past week for $299. Since I buy 99% of my product from Amazon, I would like to review it here.
This monitor is a great all round monitor. Nothing for the price can come close. I have seen many monitors come and go these past 9 weeks, and this OMEN along with the ASUS PB328Q are the only two monitors that are staying on my desk.
The ASUS PB328Q is an exceptional monitor color wise, and this Omen is not far behind. The Omen has good blacks and good contrast. Unfortunately it lacks saturation control, and it's not quite as good as the colors as on the ASUS PB328Q, albeit close.
My life revolves around two thing :
1. Color correction
2. FPS gaming.
My job is to photograph and color correct fashion products. Color is extremely important to me.
Both monitors are 60-to 75 native. Both are buttery smooth in FPS games, and very immersive. The Omen skips frames at 75hz, the Asus does not.
If you are using an Nvidia card the OMEN skips frames. However, during a game I cannot notice it.
The reason I am comparing both monitors is because they both have an AMVA panel.
I have grown very fond of AMVA over IPS. I prefer deep blacks and good contrast. I agree that IPS colors especially reds and green and a tad more vibrant, but there are simply too many problems with screen uniformity, inferior blacks, and BLB.
I also find that 1440p and 32" is perfect with no scaling. Many people says that 1440p is best at 27". I do not agree that 1440p and 27" is the best. I find that 27" at 1440p is a little bit crowded.
Forget 32" at 4k, way too busy without scaling. 4k great at 40~43".
It is the best thing that's happened in office so far. An office mate paid 4 times more for hers and mine work equally as well as hers. Oh well.

Screen arrived quickly, was the right part , and installed easily.  Gave new life to a dead Chromebook.
This monitor exceeded my expectations!

I replaced my older Apple Displays with these. Resolution is killer and I love the ability to adjust resolution as a I wish and not be locked. The monitor has more USB inputs than the Apple Display, lacks the camera and built in speakers but who really needs that anyway?

I use this mostly for programming in a well lit room. The small size makes 1080 text look as crisp as any 4K monitor. Compared to my MacBook Pro, I can see some backlight bleeding, but, it's not unbearable. As to color reproduction - I'd say it rivals my Samsung 27" 4K Monitor

My biggest complaint is the button to get to the menu. The button has 4 positions: up, neutral, down, in. Pressing up and down is super easy. Pressing in, it seems hit or miss. If there's even the slightest up or down movement, the "IN" movement seems to get over-ridden and the menu item up or down gets highlighted (but not selected)

After some practice, it's easier to navigate.

The second complaint is the power cord is REALLY short - maybe 4 or 5 feet. and finding a USB-A to USB-C cable is pretty hard. Luckily, my power strip is right below my desk, so, I don't have any issues, but, just something to consider.

THE GOOD

They provide a Micro-HDMI cable that is truly awesome. I mean, it's really good quality, and not stiff. Again, the color is REALLY good for a monitor this cheap. It's as good as a $300 Samsung, but I have a $500 LG that looks way better. But again, it's about 4x's the cost.

The case turns into a stand and it's really good. It's the perfect angle for working, it doesn't take up a lot of desk space, and the case it soft. I trust this case to protect my screen.

OVERALL
I'm happy with my purchase and my buy another one. The bad, are not bad enough to NOT buy if you need a small monitor for programming and need more desk space. It also works perfect if you want to hook an AppleTV up to it to watch something while traveling. The included cables are good quality, the case protects the monitor amply, and the monitor is small enough to fit into a laptop backpack with a 13" MacBook pro, and both chargers and not be too big or bulky

I have been wanting to upgrade to a 4K 120hz monitor for a few years now, and finally pulled the trigger when this monitor went on sale. I am able to use it with my Xbox Series X at full 4k 120hz. It is also great to  use with both my M1 MacBook Pro and Windows laptop.

I'll start off with the cons

Some text looks a blurry if the text color and background color are sorta close, like light blue and white. For 4k, I was expecting more sharpness.
Needs a remote or easier to access controls. It has a joy switch controller on the right side which is nice, but it's a far reach to adjust. Below the monitor would be better. The power button is also an I/O switch that you find on electronics on the back you have to "flick".
It's really big which is also a con.
The stand could be better but I'm probably going to mount this later.

Pros:
Did I already mention, "IT'S BIG?" 4 applications can run on the same screen and you can comfortably manage and view all 4 when you split it/PIP.
No external brick. I love that since almost every one of my old monitors need a brick PSU. Just a generic power cable is all you need for this.
IPS, 4k, and the price? Look at the specs. I will update later if I find something false, but so far it lives up to the expectation from my initial 10 minute impression. $442 for all this is a steal.
No issues with this so far, no dead pixels or anything unlike another 32" 2k monitor I ordered somewhere else.

Overall:

It's huge, bigger than I thought but my desk can fit it with my other 27" monitor. Multitasking/having multiple programs up won't be a problem with this. Honestly, if it had a higher refresh rate, I would just use this for everything since I bought my other monitor for mainly gaming (144/OC 165hz). But for the price and everything it offers, I am pretty pleased so far.

Will update if anything changes.
I love it. It does not have an HDMI port, but it is still amazing.

I bought this to replace an older 4:3 Princeton LCD. Worked fine right out of the box with my Lenovo T61 laptop and its built in NVidia graphics card. My previous experience with 15-17" widescreens was poor because they had a much smaller effective screen height than 4:3 which resulted in very small text and graphics for a given screen size. For example, my 15.4' laptop widescreen is very difficult to view with the native resolution of 1920x1080. The 25" diagonal width worked out to be about the same height as my 4:3 LCD which is what I wanted: I didn't want to lose any screen height. I was concerned that the resolution might be too small to read but it worked out about the same. My 19" LCD 4:3 displayed 1280x1024 and this HP displays 1920x1080 so the text and icons are about the same size. I use this for business purposes so the widescreen format wasn't important but having the extra screen width with the same height as the monitor I was replacing was important for viewing multiple windows side by side. I wound up buying a Planar dual display mount and have the older monitor and the HP side by side in dual display mode. The Lenovo T61 NVidea card is capable of dual monitor displays. I have PLENTY of workspace now and although it looks a little odd with a 4:3 next to the widescreen, it works well for me.

This HP monitor has VGA, DVI and HDMI ports and it comes with one of each cable in the box. The stand is a little flimsy and the monitor will wiggle if bumped. As I stated above, I took the stand off and use the Planar dual mount which is very rigid. As for the screen, the monitor has excellent contrast and color. Yes - The screen is a little shiny and has a little reflection but it is no worse than CRT's and I think this shiny front gives the monitor the rich colors and excellent contrast. I use this in a very bright office with lots of sun and have no problems. You might have some issues if light is behind you though.

I recently started working from home more which meant I needed to be switching peripherals between my home PC, my media server PC and my work laptop (which is required to access secured files via the company VPN). With my old dual monitor setup (2 x 27" ASUS), it was just too painful and required too many cables. Plus, my work requires me to have more windows open and visible at once than ever before. I manage cancer clinical trials and constantly have to have at LEAST 4 things open simultaneously to be really efficient, including a mix of outlook email AND calendar, web browser with at least 3 tabs, windows explorer, 2-3 excel documents, one word document, one access document and more. It means I have to constantly compare patient demographic information to verify it in their appointments and to verify that against what is in the trial protocols etc. It's a LOT to say the least. With the addition of this monitor and the free Microsoft Power Toys/Fancy Zones software, I have found the magical combination of productivity and efficiency that I never thought possible, AND it has freed up SO MUCH space on my desk!!

In case you're wondering about my setup, here's what I currently have connected and how:
*HDMI 1 - My daily driver PC that I built which I use for heavy duty audio and video editing
*HDMI 2 - My media server PC that I built which houses all our media and lives connected to the network. It's what we stream locally stored content from movies and TV shows to music and photos to all our devices
*USB C - My work issued laptop (HP Envy), powered AND connected all through a single USB C cable which was included with the monitor.

All of these devices share my Logitech MX Keys wireless keyboard and Logitech MX Master 3 wireless mouse, both of which connect to and switch between all three devices with the push of a button.

I won't lie, it took quite a bit of fiddling to figure out the best way to get it all set up and to adjust all of the different computers to display on this ultrawide monitor appropriately. On my oldest PC (the media server) I kept dealing with a badly stretched screen issue until I finally rolled back the Nvidia driver to a previous version and that fixed it. I also struggled with trying to get the monitor to share an old mouse and keyboard between devices by plugging the USB dongles into two of the USB ports in the back of the monitor. Allegedly you're supposed to be able to do that, but I could never get them to work no matter what I did. I've been wanting to upgrade mouse and keyboard anyway though and I'm SO glad I did! Now I don't need the monitor to control them for me and just use the 1,2,3 buttons on the keyboard and mouse to switch between devices which is easy enough for me.

Another thing that took a while was deciding what stand to use for the monitor. I had purchased and originally set this monitor up with a desk clamp articulating arm mount. Technically it worked, but it was a HUGE pain to set up and in the end, I didn't like how much the monitor bounced on the arm, especially when I have my desk in standing mode, so I ended up returning the arm and just using the stand that came with the monitor. I'm very happy with the switch. It takes up hardly more space on my desk and is much more stable.

I have to say, now that everything is set up to my specs and needs, I am absolutely IN LOVE with this monitor! It is absolutely enormous, haha, but it fits nicely on my desk with one PC tower, speakers and plenty of other things. The picture is gorgeous and the curvature provides a very natural viewing angle to everything on the screen at once.

Aside from my original struggles getting it setup that I already mentioned (ie. sharing the mouse and keyboard via the USB dongles), the only other complaint I have so far is that sometimes when I press the toggle button to switch between machines, it takes a little longer than I'd like or think it should to bring up the menu that lets you toggle between attached devices. Most of the time it happens immediately, but sometimes it takes a good 10 seconds or more (which isn't that long in the grand scheme, but just feels very sluggish sometimes).

My favorite thing about this monitor besides the incredible amount of screen space I now have to display and organize everything I need all at once on all three of my machines is the sheer number of cables it has cut out and the amount of desk space it has freed up. My dual monitor setup required two power cables and three HDMI/displayport connector cables. Using my laptop required the laptop charger and AND required the laptop to be open on my desk. Now I only have one cable to power the monitor, 2 HDMIs to my PCs and a single USB C that both powers and connects my laptop, which means I can leave it closed on a stand behind my monitor.

I am so, SO happy I bought this monitor and am kicking myself for not doing it sooner. That being said, when I bought it, it was on sale for $848. At that price, I can pretty much promise you there is no better monitor for your money. I know most people who buy these types of super mega ultrawides are gamers, and I am not (at all) but I do edit audio, video and photos on it which is a total pleasure, and it has completely changed my work life. I track my productivity and it has literally increased by 36% just since I started using it. AMAZING!

If you're in the market for an ultra wide, ESPECIALLY if you want to use it with multiple machines, you really owe it to yourself to give this one a serious look. It definitely has my two thumbs up!

I sincerely hope this review has been helpful to someone else, and yes I paid FULL PRICE for it! Feel free to ask any questions!

Just got mine in today and can easily the see source of other people's complaints: They didn't spend any time whatsoever bothering to calibrate it. They were expecting this to work 100% right out of the box and I suppose that's fair, but for 180 bucks I am certainly not going to complain if I need to run the Windows colour calibration tool and get a great looking monitor five minutes later. The screen comes out of the box with a pretty high gamma rating (easily fixable via the tool) and a smidge too much red in it (also easily fixed via the tool). This is what's causing the washed out, yellowish look that some people are reporting. Once you run the tool this monitor looks great.

The controls being undermounted is kind of annoying but not bad once you get used to em. My other ASUS has them front mounted and I can appreciate them being hidden on this screen. It's a nice trade off for a much better looking screen aesthetically.

The stand is sturdy though very basic as it only offers tilt adjustment. Not a big deal on my setup but something to be aware of if you have some edge case issues with your desk arrangement.

Finally, yes, the speakers aren't that great but come on, if you're expecting great audio quality from a $180 screen then you're not that smart of a shopper. It's not worth dinging this monitor on. If you want good audio quality then you're going to be spending far, far more than this or should just go buy some actual, dedicated speakers to use.

I will absolutely be picking up two more of these to replace my other two, aging gaming screens.

Purchased this monitor in December and I have zero regrets.

Pros:
- Makes you look more productive than the guy next to you in meetings.
- The 2 input options (HDMI and USB-C) really makes this monitor one of the most versatile gadgets I own. I can connect this to my work laptop or play movies and games on my personal phone.
- I recently discovered Desktop Mode on my Android phone. This has been a game changer. No more sacrificing screen real estate to my touch keyboard makes it possible to actually type emails and edit spreadsheets on the go. I rarely leave home without this monitor.
- The ability to have an alternate power source via the dedicated USB-C port is great.
- Built in speakers are great to have and Audio output is an added benefit.

Cons:
- Built in speakers are great to have, but I thought I was losing my hearing the first time I used them. Very low sound. Luckily there is an audio output jack.
- On screen menu is difficult to navigate. If you're using this for a presentation, adjusting the settings might force you to have to reschedule.
- Putting on the included screen protector is an exercise in futility. Unless you like grit and bubbles.
- Magnetic cover tends to fold up/slide at the worst times. Don't place this monitor on the edge of your desk. It also leaves the inputs exposed to all the Nutrigrain bar crumbs in your briefcase. (My previous monitor came with a neoprene case to cover the device.)

In my opinion, the cons are small annoyance, and are far outweighed by the benefits.

If you're in the market, look no further.

Flat panel momitors have two major advantages: the entire surface of the display is usable (thus a 17" flat panel monitor gives as large a working surface as a 19" CRT monitor) and they don't take up a lot of desk space as CRT monitors do. But most flat panel monitors have the disadvantage that their image is not quite as sharp as the best CRT monitors. This Apple monitor gives you the best of both types. It has the size advantages typical of flat panel monitors and it has the crisp, sharp image quality of the best CRT monitors. Yes, it's more expensive than the average flat panel monitor; but if you want CRT image quality with the size advantages of a flat panel monitor this is the one to get.

If you're buying this monitor for use with an nvidia graphics card and gsync then you need to set the power settings for your pc to now allow the screen to turn off. Regardless of whether you have AMD or Nvidia (though AMD benefits the most out of this), this is a great monitor to have considering the price and specs.

A 27" TN panel that supports 144Hz at 1440p with DisplayPort 1.2, TWO HDMI ports, and a Dual-Link DVI, as well as a built-in USB 3.0 hub with two ports easily makes this monitor a great utility monitor for gamers that do everything at their desk and only need or can only have one monitor.

The OSD is pretty standard, with some nifty little features from Asus. My only gripe with the OSD is that the controls are on the side, which makes sense for a single monitor user, however since I have a multi-monitor setup and the MG278Q is in the center, it gets a little annoying try to reach the controls whenever I need to change my input from DisplayPort to HDMI-1. Wish they were still on the back but bottom, or a easier to access input change.

In terms of performance, it's high quality when response time is priority. The jump from a 60Hz to 144Hz was night and day, as well as the bump from 1080p to 1440p making games like Counter-Strike: Global Offensive so much better to play.

One disclaimer, is that I have FreeSync disabled since I prefer the running at 144Hz, however for the games that I don't care about having the higher frequency for, I'll turn it on and enjoy the tear-free experience.

The stand is nice and sturdy, and it has a fairly small footprint on the desktop while being able to use the base's surface if needed. Easy to remove the monitor from stand if you prefer using the VESA mount, and even more bonus points for including screws pre-installed in the VESA holes.

Bezel is fairly slim, but not as thin as the ROG Swift PG279Q. Do love the fact that the top of the monitor is flat and the back is flat near the top for easier webcam mounting, unlike my Asus VH238H that has a curved top.

Color is pretty good on the sRGB profile. Wouldn't say it's accurate, due to the fact that I've yet to use an IPS monitor and don't have any proper tools to gauge the color accuracy, however for general gaming and media consumption, the colors are accurate enough with very little noticeable bias towards warm or cool temperatures.

Would I buy another MG278Q or two for a replacement or triple monitor setup? Possibly. Would I still consider it, even if I was running an Nvidia card instead of my R9 290X? Definitely if I was on a budget. Do I regret getting the MG278Q instead of the ROG Swift PG279Q since I know I'm going to go Nvidia on my next upgrade? Not at all, especially since this would still be an excellent auxiliary monitor.

Lastly, customer service has been excellent and very quick to respond. I had a few issues initially with a minor defect in the shipped product - just seems I was unlucky, but they completely remediated it promptly.

Very happy with the purchase and completely achieves my goal of an economical large truly portable screen.

I'm going to start off by saying that this is a 60hz monitor, not a 165hz. After messing around with the monitor for an hour I decided to contact Samsung through live chat. After some back and forth conversation, the rep alerted me that I was unable to achieve anything over 60hz because that was the max the monitor could handle. I should have known that after some research and seeing like monitors that are 4k and 165hz are around $1200. In this day and age, 60hz is just not viable for any type of competitive gameplay. Though it will be fine for single player, and non competitive games.

For everyday use, I can not suggest this monitor. I attempted to use it, but found that with webpage viewing, or any type of scrolling there is tearing. It is not smooth by any means. Video was fine on it, and looked beautiful. I was even able to play some Cyberpunk in 4k and it was amazing looking. But that's about the extent of it. Trying to use the monitor for every day use messed with my eyes as when scrolling though an article or Facebook, it was jittery.

This should work very well as a secondary monitor. For anyone that does any type of digital creation in 4k should be able to set this up as a secondary monitor and work off this screen for just that, leaving the main monitor as something a bit more usable for every day use.

For some reason, the menu continued to pop up whenever I entered a new application and it set the resolution. It went away after about 20 seconds, but it was still a bit of an inconvenience.

Overall while this is a decent monitor, it just is not suited well for me. The low refresh rate is all that is really holding this monitor back. This monitor will be perfect for someone who wants to add it as a secondary, and only assign certain tasks to it. Watching movies, and playing some single player games is where this monitor really shines.

I purchased this monitor to hook up to an NVR/Camera system, so it runs 24x7 in my environment. I was looking for something with the small size, and have been quite happy with the way it fits my countertop. However, like some others posting reviews, the power supply seems to be sub-standard. In my case, the unit went into production in 12/2020, and it was dead by 02/2022.

Luckily, I happened to have another 12V power supply with the same +tip configuration, and when plugging in the new power supply (which is actually a 3A supply, not just the 2A that came with it), the monitor sprang back to life. Testing the bad power supply with a multi-meter, I found it to be putting out 1.2V, not 12V! Time to junk that one and get a permanent replacement.

So, in short, because I know how to troubleshoot things like this, I can get it to work and I can find a replacement power supply that won't fail quite so quickly. If I didn't know how to do that, though, then I'd definitely be tossing this unit out. Note to the manufacturer: Increase the price by $5 and spend that on a better power adapter!

The AOC2236 looked like a very good choice to replace my old monitor and I am very satisfied with it. It is a very inexpensive 16:9 HD monitor, which provides an excellent and crisp picture and is an excellent value. Two previous reviews have given a lot of information about this monitor, so I will try to keep my information regarding its features brief.

INSTRUCTIONS - The assembly of the monitor to the base was straightforward, and the pictorial instructions adequate, even though they contained absolutely no words.

PICTURE ADJUSTMENT - The instructions are on an enclosed CD, but again they are very minimal. When I hooked up the monitor and turned it on, the picture came to life without any preliminary adjustment, making the monitor really plug-and-play. The picture was, however, stretched because my previous monitor was a standard 19-inch and this monitor has a 16:9 format. To correct this, I had to make an adjustment, which I made through my video card software (in Windows) prior to looking at the enclosed CD. My video card recognized this new monitor exactly, making the adjustment process quite simple through the automatic adjustment feature. I suspect that the correct driver was either already in my system (which would be surprising because my computer is several years old), or it automatically downloaded from the Internet or from the monitor itself, even prior to using the CD. If this does not work for you then you will have to use the enclosed CD, which contains the drivers. Interestingly enough, using the video card was exactly what the CD told me to do. I also contacted AOC and they provided a file, via e-mail, with much more complete instructions. Their response was very quick and much appreciated.

MONITOR CONTROLS - They are located on the right side of the monitor, just above the power button, but they not easy to find as they are flat and only touch sensitive (as is the power button) and I could not find any instructions for their use. Fortunately, the detailed instructions provided by one of the reviewers (and the file provided by AOC) solved this problem for me. Be sure to look at Tony's Amazon review if you buy this monitor, it will save hours of frustration and if you still have problems go to the AOC web site and contact them to get the file containing the instruction manual. Without these instructions the monitor adjustments are useless.

PICTURE QUALITY - I found the picture to be very sharp on moderate resolutions as well as on the highest HD1920x1080 resolution (which is recommended and the one that I use). With all monitors, the greater the resolution the smaller the font and icon size, so I had to go properties-appearance (right click on the desktop) and enlarge the font and icon size to compensate for this. For word, excel and alike, I view at greater than 100%, which given the larger width of the display is not a problem. I like the 16:9 format because it gives me more room for icons on word and excel, and when I use the Internet I can leave my bookmarks in place without any loss in area for the site itself. (Be sure to set the monitor up correctly, so that the 16:9 format is enabled and is not stretched.) When I first got the monitor I found that the brightness and color intensity were not completely uniform, but this effect was slight and with use this problem has gone away.

CORRECTING PICTURE QUALITY - The picture characteristics of this monitor are very adjustable, but the lack of instructions makes this difficult. I get the impression that the manufacturer does not actually want the customer to alter things and run in other than the automatic Dynamic Contrast Ratio (DCR) mode. Unfortunately, my initial difficulty with the uniformity of the brightness and color intensity were most noticeable with the turned DCR on, so I had to turn it off and go to manual settings. I was able to largely eliminate these problems using manual settings. Unfortunately, this required digging out the information on how to adjust the monitor and then going into the ECO mode of the monitor control to turn the DCR to off, and even with more detailed instructions this is not easy to do. With the DCR off, I was able to make brightness, contrast and color intensity changes. I also had to use my video control card to reduce the brightness and contrast ratio. These changes mostly, but not quite completely, eliminated the brightness and color intensity variations. Brightness variations are an inherent problem with all LCD monitors when viewed off-angle, and here the off-angle reduction in brightness eliminates any observed variation, so the angle at which the monitor is tilted relative to your eye position is important.

I am very satisfied with this monitor and give it 5-stars.

My particular use case is for a homemade luggable computer of sorts. I'm a fan of 8 bit era computers, as well as certain modern takes on that era of computers, such as the Color Maximite 1, Color Maximite 2, BASIC Engine, BASIC Engine (Orange Pi edition), et al.

My needs for this particular project were:
1: DC input, using an external power source - so that I can easily power it via battery.
This input needed to be of relatively low watt draw.
2: HDMI, VGA, and composite inputs
3: At least standard HD resolution of 1280x720 or more
4: The overall monitor size needs to be relatively close to a smaller keyboard. Not necessarily a 60% sized keyboard, but something close to that.
5: $ cheap $, just in case the project doesn't work out

I've machined a prototype case out of wood (somewhat similar to a Pi-Top Raspberry Pi laptop) and fitted the original BASIC Engine retro computer, BASIC Engine OPi edition, and a Color Maximite 2 inside the lower half of the luggable laptop. My power supply needs, at least for now, is a Makita LXT battery adapter mounted to the outside of the rear part of the upper laptop housing - with wires routed into the lower half and connected to two buck DC-DC converters (one set at 12V and one set for 5V). The BASIC Engine Orange Pi edition is connected to the HDMI input, the BASIC Engine original edition is connected to the composite input, and the Color Maximite 2 is connected to the VGA input. All are working flawlessly. The color reproduction is 100% acceptable for my use case.

The only negative, at least for my use case, is the tight fit of the VGA and HDMI connectors. Fortunately, I had a small HDMI male to micro-HDMI adapter that is barely larger than just the male part of a full-sized HDMI cable by itself. I connected a small gauge, coiled cable to that.

TL:DR version: DO NOT BUY THIS IF YOU WANT TO EDIT VIDEOS AND PHOTOS for professional or similar purposes!!! You are not and cannot spend $80 and get what you want....you HAVE to spend much more than what this monitors costs. This monitor is the bee's knees if you want to game on the go, from 8 bit retro computers over composite all the way to the 2020 generation of high end consoles over HDMI and all sorts of devices in between. This would be a great tool to put in an IT tool kit. I most definitely recommend this!

The good: the monitor looks great. The colors are vivid and I can actually see minute details in photos I couldn't on my old monitor. The stand is phenomenal. It feels weird to make a big deal about the stand, but It's hard not to do so: it's sturdy (doesn't wobble while typing or anything), it is very adjustable in every direction, and the pivot feature is awesome for doing anything that is more vertical in nature (although you have to manually change the orientation from landscape to portrait). It has a bunch of different connection options, which makes multi monitor use easy, and it comes with a lot more cords than I'd expect (VGA, DP, and DVI, though no HDMI). The USB 3.0 hub is really quite useful.

The bad: The biggest problem I had was that when connected through a DVI cable, the screen flickers a fair amount. Sometimes it'd just be a few lines would pop up quickly and sometimes the whole screen would go black for about half a second. Very annoying. Apparently this is a frequent problem for this monitor and the advice on the boards is to use a different input. My graphics card doesn't have DP it has HDMI-mini, so I had to order a cable off of Amazon ($10). I imagine I could have contacted ASUS and gotten a replacement monitor, but that would have been a pretty big hassle and I'm not sure if it'd fix the issue. For what it's worth, this isn't a problem with my graphics card as my second monitor does not have this problem when connected via DVI.

One other weird thing: unless the screen is actually connected and receiving signal, it seems that it's impossible to load up the menu, which meant that I had to connect both my DVI and HDMI cables to switch the input from DVI to HDMI, which was weird.

Anyway, my verdict is that this is a great monitor at a good value with the exception of the annoying DVI problem. If you're connecting through DP or HDMI (or I guess VGA) you shouldn't worry about this at all. If you know you need to connect via DVI, I'd just save yourself trouble and order a different monitor.

I just got this for my Xbox One X after doing a ton of research and finding this was the only monitor that supported 4K, FreeSync over HDMI, HDR, and was made (at least partially) for gaming.

Overview: A great looking monitor, but for $550 I expected a little bit more.

PROS:
-Great picture quality
-4K
-HDR compatible
-Supports FreeSync over HDMI

CONS:
Noticeable Input Lag (see details below).
Not "real" HDR
Does not have built in speakers

About the input lag. Its not awful, but it is noticeable if you're looking for it. I have no way to measure it precisely but I could notice a very small delay between pressing a button on the controller and seeing a response on the screen. When compared to my BenQ RL2455HM (a monitor known for having very little input lag), I could not tell a difference so it might be in my head or maybe my expectations are too high.

About the HDR. I say its not real because technically, full HDR support requires 1000nits of brightness and this monitor tops at 450nits. That said, there are no good gaming monitors out yet that support full 1000nit HDR (at least non in the sub $1000 range) and 450nits is still a lot and enough to support a noticeable HDR effect so its not that big of a deal.

Not having built in speakers was not a big deal for me since I only use it for gaming with headphones, but it would have been nice to have at least basic speakers and for $550, I would expect speakers. That said, it does have a handy audio out/headphone 3.5mm jack for external speakers or headphones and the Xbox One X does allow you to output audio over HDMI or optical to headphones, external speakers, or an audio receiver.

In the end, I was very satisfied with this monitor and would rather keep it than wait another 6 months-1 year for a better monitor to come out. I knocked off one start because of the input lag and HDR issues but again, this is a fantastic gaming monitor for the new Xbox One X and the only one (to my knowledge) that supports all the most important gaming features of the Xbox, but its not perfect so I knocked off a star.

UPDATE March 2018: I just learned that Freesync does not yet work on the Xbox One X but IS coming with the Spring 2018 update! Its actually Freesync 2 which decreases input lag and increases HDR performance as well as supporting console/monitor synchronization at much lower framerates!

Original review: Pretty good portable monitor. I work remote for two companies (two laptops) and since I also set up my mobile office in different locations weekly, I wanted a second monitor that could travel with me. It is also nice to be able to close up shop each weekend and not have a large external monitor sitting unused and looming in my home office (which happens to be my dining room with other uses!).

So the portability aspect is great. Highly recommend if you are mobile, remote and a fixed external monitor just doesn’t make sense. I am showing a pic of the monitor hooked up to my 13in MacBook Pro (middle), with a 15in MacBook Pro on the far right. Definitely helpful to have additional screen space for the 13in.

Downsides are brightness, which just isn’t as bright as the Macs, but I took another reviewer’s advice and adjusted the blue light setting. It just loses a little contrast but I don’t do design specific or image work on the external monitor.

Other downside was I shelled out a few more bucks for the “upgraded” model and it’s not amazing. In fact the silver accent piece with the logo is popped up off of one corner (see pic). There was also a weird bit of plastic sticking over the screen under the frame, which I sort of just slide underneath the screen. Not a deal breaker, just feels like a value monitor vs a higher end monitor.

Overall, it’s nice enough to keep, and the mobile aspect is tremendous. I’d buy this again, but maybe go for one of the cheaper Lepow models with coupon.

BEWARE--NOT COMPATIBLE WITH MAC OPERATING SYSTEM! There are reviews on tech sites that recommended this for Mac. In fact, ASUS and Apple recommended it. Look on the Asus website on the "Support" tab for the monitor and you'll see that it is only Windows compatible. It over 1.5 years of ownership and problems to find this out FINALLY from Asus. It shorted out two MacMini logic boards, which has to be replaced.

It DID work until April 2017 with just one problem (would have a random electrical storm and make a racket if left on idle, so it really is NOT Mac compatible) until I upgraded to the Sierra operating system. That's when it shorted out my Mac's logic board the first time.

On the PRO side (based on 15 months of use before upgrading to Sierra):
1. It is a very attractive monitor.
2. Display is beautiful.
3. Great price point for how nice it is.

I was excited at the possibility of extending my late 2009 27-inch iMac's desktop at 2560x1440 when I saw the price of the Auria EQ276W LED IPS LED monitor. So far (and I will update if something changes) I have not been impressed. Here is why: A) I have not been able to get the resolution above 1920x1080. I have tried connecting it via the mini-DVI to a Dual DVI with a cheap adapter and an Apple one to no avail. I have now ordered a mini-DVI to Display Port cable in hopes that this will work, if it does not then this monitor goes back. B) I have read several reviews about the glossy nature of the screen. For me, I want the glossy screen. My currently iMac and MacBook Pro have a glossy screen and I am used to it. The official product description makes no mention of the "glossyness" of the screen. Nor does any of the documentation that comes with the monitor or printing on the box. I was surprised that I received a version of the screen that has an anti-gloss matte to it. This does not match my current iMac at all. I would not have ordered if I had known that but my bad for going by the review versus the lack of info in the product description. C) Picture quality is very bad at the current 1920x1080 resolution. I have tried several calibrations and cannot get this to look right. Considering all the rave reviews about the picture quality I just expected better even at lower resolutions. If this is not resolved with the new cable and at a higher resolution then it is going back. D) As many others have stated, not having a height adjustment is just a pain in the rear. I built a wooden stand that makes sure the monitor is at the exact height as my iMac but that was a lot of work. I will post a follow up once the mini-DVI to Display Port cable arrives. Until then, I have an overly priced poor quality 1920x1080 monitor.

June 28, 2013 UPDATE
I received the StarTech 6-Feet Mini DisplayPort to DisplayPort Adapter Cable ([[ASIN:B002XVYZ82 B002XVYZ82]] and I now have the 2560 X 1440 resolution I hoped for. I am happy at the real estate that I now have for my desktop. Some other follow on items: 1) I was not able to get the iMac and Auria calibration to match in terms of color, white and black. I really was not expecting to be able to considering the price of this monitor. I used this website ([...] as a guide then used Apple's built in calibration wizard. I was able to get them close to one another. 2) When some of the more animated screen savers kick in I get terrible flickering on the Auria. Not sure why. It does not happen with the iTunes cover art screen saver and goes away when the logon pad appears when you press a key... if anyone has a tip on how to fix this please post the answer. I am changing my star rating from a 2 to 4 stars mostly because I was able to get the resolution to work and the price.

I wanted to make the ascension to dual 1440p monitors (easier to push than a 4K monitor graphics-wise too and still looks great) and make my main monitor 144Hz so I didn't have to swap them around. It's a little problematic though, because using Windows at 144Hz makes my 60Hz display feel laggy. I have rarely written a hardware product review. The main reason I'm writing this one is that I'm impressed with this product.

But First: Like most PC users I've spent my money on what's in the box, i.e., CPU, Memory, hard drive, GPU, etc. Then I skimp on the sound and monitor. I've never realized how dumb that is before. So you've got a fast, powerful and likely, pretty box to play your games on. Really what else do you need all that power for, e-mail, web browsing? Come on, get a net-book for $350. 1500-8000 Dollars worth of Computing Box is for playing Modern Warfare, Assassins Creed, Dragon Age and there ilk. So we spend all that cash on the box, and get a $150 bargain monitor that will make you blind and use speakers that sound like tin cans tied together with string. When for really only a few Dollars more you can get a good monitor and decent speakers.

Now the gush: This is a good monitor, its a 27 inch wide screen HD LCD monitor. The picture is beautiful, the sound is poor*, the stand doesn't swivel, flip or rise/lower, it just tilts front to back. The reason to buy this monitor is its picture (display for you techies), its bright and clean looking.
I've been playing Civilization V and Dragon Age: Origins on it with an Nvidia GTX 460 (1Gig Ram) driving the monitor to its factory max for the last three weeks and I've never seen anything like it on an affordable home system before.
I said affordable, the monitor can be had for under $300. Now that's a bargain.

* = Its been my experience that most all video monitors have from poor to stinky bad sound quality. Why manufactures can't put a decent set of speakers and a $20 amp on a chip in the monitor box, I don't know. You'd think that an $800 video monitor would have at lest average sound quality, but no, stinky bad to poor at the high end.

Let's get right to this:

Pros
* The 22" screen looks great. Vibrant, bright, decent at angles, and quite crisp. No dead pixels to be found in mine. I can't say enough nice things about the color on this monitor.

* Minimal decoration in the bezel means this will fit in nicely on your desk.

* VESA standard connections allows for wall-mounting.

* VGA and DVI connections both work well and support up to 1050p.

* Cord-wrangling base is easy to connect, stable, and solid.

* Doesn't seem to put out very much heat.

* OSD controls are nice. Instead of labeling the controls on the bezel, the OSD actually lines up the control functions with their corresponding buttons in the corner of the screen. Fairly decent selection of options, including a nice auto-set feature, and different monitor color/contrast schemes.

* Relatively lightweight and quite thin.

* Buttons are decent. You can tell when you've pressed one.

Cons
* Limited connection options compared to other displays. No HDMI, no component, no optical audio; This is definitely a budget monitor.

* Speakers aren't really that good; They're really tinny. You're better off with external speakers, because these shouldn't even count as "speakers".

* Ships with no film over the monitor, which is odd. Most LCD panels have a protective film. While mine was in perfect condition, I could see the lack of this film causing shipping problems for some people.

* 16:10 format is acceptable, but rapidly becoming outdated compared to 16:9 LCD panels. 16:10 means your 16:9 media will have black bars at top and bottom, or be stretched out- Be aware of this if you intend to use this for a video game console or other media device.

* Sensitive to electromagnetic devices in the immediate area. My electric fan caused the monitor to "wave" or "flicker", while my brand-name monitor has no problems with the same fan at the same distance. I suspect one area where they save money is in the amount of shielding they used.

I was pleased with the value represented here. This monitor's display looks every bit as good as my brand-name LCD panel's display, and while it's not as versatile, it works great if you're using VGA or DVI sources that are comfortably viewed in 16:10 format. Computers, of course, are the perfectly fine on this monitor.

Everything about how this monitor performs is really, really good. It has good color reproduction, thanks to the IPS panel, and fast response time for gaming along with plenty of brightness. The blacks could be darker, but overall, I can't complain much. There's a little bit of light leakage in the lower left and right corners, but it's not noticeable except in a completely dark (or nearly so) room.

Now, the one thing that just makes me so angry about this monitor... the power "button", or rather the touch sensitive spot where the power button is located, only works about 10% of the time!! If the monitor is on, touching the power button, whether lightly or firmly, with fingertip or thumb... whatever... nothing happens. The monitor will NOT turn off! Now, the same thing will also happen when it's off... no matter how much I touch the power button, either on or around it, it will NOT turn ON!!! The ONLY way I can fix this problem, is to unplug the power cord from the back of the monitor, wait about 10 seconds, then plug it back in. After that, the power button works like it should. It may work again 30 minutes later, or 3 hours later, but before long, it goes back to not working again and touching the power button does nothing and I have to repeat the power cord trick! SO SICK OF THIS PROBLEM!!!

Maybe I got 1 lemon out of a thousand, I dunno, but it's a very STUPID problem to have! If it wasn't for that, I would have given this 5 stars.

The product I ordered was the Viotek GN24CW, the white 1080p variant of this monitor (not sure if it's still available or not), from the seller Better Choice Online -- and I got it tax and shipping free for $199.99. Keep this price in mind -- if I seem exaggerated or excessive in my review, remember how cheap this monitor is.

RESPONSE TIME / SMOOTHNESS:
- For the money? HOLY SH*T. Let me start by saying DISREGARD the response time -- this thing felt more responsive and smoother than any 1ms TN panel I've tried, including my $400+ 1440p 144hz 1ms TN panel back home. While true, the response time isn't as fast, the INPUT LAG is significantly lower, which MORE than makes up for the meh response time.
REFRESH RATE:
- Now, 144hz? Hell no. Try 163hz! I was able to overclock the monitor out of the box to 163hz (however, artifacts would on and off be noticeable, so I currently have it set to 158hz, 100% artifact free). STUPID smooth.
COLORS:
- The colors and overall contrast are also pretty excellent as well, especially the blacks (at least compared to TN panels, though that may not be saying much), just keep in mind you may need to adjust the colors yourself to get that great picture. Also, it's probably worth adding that my monitor had zero dead pixels upon arrival.
CURVE:
- Alright, so the curve..... Honestly, I really dig it. However, it without a doubt totally niche and pointless -- with how small the monitor is (24"), there is no real noticeable benefit from it, as opposed to a curved ultrawide. It just looks fancy / neat, so no real con. For the price, the curve is a more than welcome inclusion in my opinion.
SPEAKERS:
- As for the speakers... They're about as expected. They work. I wouldn't expect them to be great, especially in this price bracket -- the fact that they were included in the first place is more than enough for me. The sound quality is fine, if anything they're just a lil quiet (but again, THEY WORK. A separate pair of speakers will always be ideal, but for what these are they suffice).
BETTER CHOICE ONLINE SERVICE:
- There was a mixup upon ordering, where my monitor was being sent to the incorrect address (not sure if this was an error on my part or Amazon's, but I'll Amazon the benefit of the doubt and say I screwed up), and upon contacting the seller, they replied quickly and efficiently and rerouted my package to me properly and with haste. Incredibly hassle free; one email and done. If you're reading this, Better Choice Online, I appreciate the service.
PRODUCT ADVERTISEMENT ACCURACY:
- Alright, this is why this product is at 4/5 Stars. Is the product exceptional, especially for its price bracket? Absolutely; without question. HOWEVER, the product is NOT quite as advertised in the pictures. For starters, the stand isn't even close to being similar to what is shown. Also, the bezels are really nice and slim on this monitor!... but that doesn't change the fact that they're pretty exaggerated and photoshopped in the advertisement. Last (but not least), there are a few small symbols on the bottom bezel to label the buttons on the bottom / rear of the monitor, and as you can see those are photoshopped out in the advertisement.
CONCLUSION:
- For the price, I have no complaints. It's an aesthetically pleasing, SHOCKLY well performing gaming monitor, and I can genuinely say I don't feel like I am missing out on ANYTHING a "true gaming monitor" / TN panel could offer. This is possibly the best budget curved gaming monitor you can get. Just wish the advertisement was a little less scummy and more honest.

This is a good low-cost 144hz WQHD gaming panel, it is a TN panel so the viewing angles are not quite as good as an IPS.. but that is really only an issue if you are watching movies on it from across the room or something, it won't affect gaming at all. Like all TN panels this has super fast 1ms refresh rate and low input lag, perfect for FPS gaming.

Some TN panels are known for having poor color quality and contrast but this is not one of them, it has excellent color reproduction and the one I received was very well calibrated right out of the box (tested with spyder colorimeter). When placed up next to my Asus 27" IPS monitor, they look almost identical.. really the only way you can tell this is a TN panel is by the viewing angle which is not as wide as IPS, in all other respects it performs as well as my IPS panels, while also having lower input lag.. for that reason I definitely recommend this monitor for competitive FPS gamers looking for a low cost but high quality monitor.

The stand and aesthetics of the monitor are minimalist but high quality, like most Dell monitors. I like the fact that it doesn't have any special 'gamer' emblems like Asus and many other brands tend to do with their gaming monitors.

I knocked off 1 star due to the reduced viewing angles and because it has AMD Freesync instead of G-sync.. but this monitor really is an excellent value and performs as well or better than many more expensive monitors.

I read a lot of reviews before I bought this monitor. Even this model had some very negative reviews, but if you're interested in a 23-25" 1080P monitor with an HDMI input (this model has two) and integrated speakers, I think this one is a good choice.

Setup is very easy. All you have to do is attach the one-piece stand, no tools required. The cord to the approximately 1.5" x 4" x 1" transformer is a good length to reach the floor, and the AC end of the cord is separate, so you could swap that end out for a longer cord if needed.

The menu buttons are easy to reach on the bottom-right of the bezel.

Notable is that the MX259HS does come with an HDMI cable, though that's listed as "optional" in the manual.

The picture is sharp, the colors are good. I'm using the monitor in a fairly bright room with lots of windows, and have not had any issues with reflections on the screen. It's plenty bright even with the blue light filter set on "3".

I found the screen to be a little too dim with the blue light filter on "4", the maximum setting, but I think it would be fine in a darker room or at night.

This model does have 3 W stereo speakers that I have found to be more than adequate for the occasional video or for some background music. At 3 W, they're not going to be filling a large room with sound, but they work great at a workstation or in a small office without the clutter that external speakers would involve.

I would have given the monitor a five-star rating but the menus are a bit clumsy, and it's difficult for me to read the little icons for the various buttons when I have something bright on the screen. The menu is certainly no worse than any of the other monitors I've used, but it's no better, either. The monitor does support different "Spendid" modes and has the adjustable blue light filter, both of which some users may want to adjust now and then, making easy to use menus more important. I wouldn't rule out this monitor for the menus, but it keeps it from getting a five-star rating from me.

Overall, I'm very happy with it.

Have great resolution and it's lighter than similar monitors, it can be connected by HDMI (cable included) and USB C (you have to buy separately a cable that supports video and video but it works), good quality build materials, it is pretty awesome.
terrible idea for office word processing - text is very blurry and hard to read. Cannot be improved by updating video card. A monitor good for viewing video only
