I am currently writing this review on the monitor pictured. The quality of the monitor is nice. I have had it for over a year now and it still has a crisp clean picture.

I run a dual monitor setup. I am a gamer and this monitor is used for my non-media needs. I run movies and gaming on my other monitor as it has a larger screen and is hooked up via HDMI.

This monitor does not have that capability and is hooked up with DVI. If you are looking for a LCD monitor with a good shelf life, I would suggest this one. It has lasted the test of time and for the price you can't beat the quality.
Everything I need in a monitor. So easy to switch its input. Bright colors!
Received the monitor as advertised. Works great.

This is my second replacement. Both have terrible back screen bleed in dark settings. Very annoying to play dark games and it looks like the image above. The second image is my cheap old spectre 1080p monitor that has zero bleed for comparison.
Bought for work.  Great Dell product.
I'm in valuation at a large accounting firm and use these for my home office. They are great!

I ordered this monitor as a replacemet for my old one. Because I was short on money, this seemed like the perfect choice... and it was.

The display is awesome, with only one little problem: the lower left corner's color tends to be a bit more green.

As a replacement for old monitors, or a cheap solution to a monitor problem, this is a great choice, but if you have money to spare, go with a better known company's product.

Excellent monitor for the money. Especially if your video editing and need to work in an HDR or 10bit color space. One thing I might mention is a reviewer on here ( Dnd0220 ) states that the monitor is not 60hz at 4k. That is false. To get 4k at 60hz you have to use the displayport connection. If your using The HDMI Connection it will not work as HDMI does not have the bandwidth for 4k@60 hz. If 4k 60hz is important to you just use displayport and an appropriate displayport cable if your computers video card supports it.

I’m a visual effects artist for commercial television and film work and this monitor works just fine for the types of demanding 3D rendering and editing we do. Just a year ago you couldn’t find a monitor that supported this color depth at this resolution for less than $1200.

Overall I am pretty satisfied with this gaming monitor. The 144hz refresh rate makes a huge difference coming from my standard 60hz HP monitor.
Setup and installation was perfect with the tooless-assembly. And the form factor, design, and curvature are all satisfactory.
My main concern is the backlight bleeding effect. When the lights in my room are dimmed I can clearly see multiple white-glowing areas, noticable when playing dark games or anything involving deep blacks. Lookup "backlight bleeding," it's very noticable and annoying.
The only reason I am not returning this monitor for a replacement is due to the sale price I got it for, and the likely event I'll get a replacement monitor in worse shape than this one.

Overall, if it wasn't for the backlight bleeding I would be 100% satisfied.

Colors were always way off and you could never save changes. Anytime I would boot the computer up, the colors would reset and the crosshairs would come back on. For almost the same price, get the ISP version.

BTW, I do have a VESA monitor arm, and it works great. I don't really know if FreeSync is helping at all, I think you'd need to have another next to it to see/understand the difference.

Never having personally used a LCD monitor before, I took the plunge and decided on this one. In general I'm very happy with the results. The color, clarity and response time are great! I see almost no difference between this and my old CRT monitor. My fears of a fuzzy picture were unfounded. Though I never realized that you have to be directly in front of a LCD monitor to get accurate color. If you view it from some other side, strange things happen. But this is nothing I can't live with.

There is a strange but insignificant problem. Since I've started with this monitor, I sometimes get Runtime error messages when surfing the internet. That never happened with my old CRT monitor. I can't tell whether it's because of this new monitor. But it's a rare problem, easily brushed aside.

The slim size of the monitor is perfect for my tiny desk. Unlike my original HP CRT monitor, which took up more than half the desk. Now I just have to unload that old behemoth.

Considering the picture quality, price, and size, this is a great buy. But watch out for the shipping. Originally, Amazon.com said they would ship in 1 to 2 weeks. It took them almost a month to ship. I hope you have better luck. Now that Target is handling the shipping, things may improve.

I thought I was buying a quality product with the ViewSonic brand but this is pretty bad. I bought the ViewSonic XG3220 and the first problem I discovered is that the arm that holds it up is too short for the monitor to be swiveled into portrait mode. You can do physically do it by tilting the screen back all the way so the bottom of the monitor clears the ground, but then it has to stay that way. If they had made the arm two inches taller, this would not be a problem. BAD DESIGN! I looked at the manual and while this monitor is sold as being able to swivel 90 degrees it says in the manual that is for TRANSPORTATION ONLY. The other problem this monitor has is that it powers itself off after a few moments without HDMI signal, but then when the signal returns IT TAKES FOREVER TO COME BACK ON. I would not recommend this monitor to anyone.

Anyone with the washout problem, READ ON!! I was going to return this monitor because the picture quality was really washed out and next to my IPS panel monitor it looked like garbage. That is until I found a setting in my NVIDIA Control Panel for "Output dynamic range." in the "Change resolution" section. The default setting for this option was set to "Limited." MAKE SURE that that setting is set to "Full." This made all the difference. As a result, the blacks are blacker and there is not washed out or "fog" effect.

I'm really surprised with how good this monitor is for the price. Sure its a TN panel monitor so the colors are not as good as an IPS panel but they're still decent. This is a great gaming monitor. After playing with some settings, it a keeper!

Bought this as a display for my 2012 Mac Mini (headless Mac) and also as a 2nd monitor for my MBP. When I first looked at my wallpaper image on the screen, all I could think was Wow! The image has a lot of pinpoints of light as stars which I never knew were sharp. Everything was bright vivid and clear. I had not seen such clarity on a screen since I briefly had an Apple Retinal Display. I sent that back because it would not work right with the scroll-zoom feature that we low vision users depend on. I got a MAC Mini instead, which gives me choices on displays.

I intended to order the 30" and accidentally bought this one. It's not a bad size and I didn't return it for an exchange because the picture was so good I was afraid the larger one might not be as clear. This one does not have any internal program that allows you to tinker with the settings which the 30 inch does. On my MAC Mini, the only thing I can do is go into system preferences and adjust the gamma and also opt for the blue light reduction. Frankly, the picture is so good that I don't feel the need to to alter settings other than using the blue light feature.

I opted to leave the blue light reduction on from 10 am to 10 am so that it is always on, because the screen is too bright for my extreme light sensitivity. I can always turn it off when I need to tweak the colors of an image, etc.

What impresses me most about this screen is that the various tones in the darker shadows can be distinguished, whereas on my Viewsonic, they were all muddled together. The extreme range of tones on this screen is remarkable, especially given the price category.

You can't beat the curved screens for low vision users, because when you sit closer to the screen, the sides are the same distance from your eyes as the middle. The 30 inch would have been better but this is definitely functional in that regard. I will never go back to a flat screen. Even though I do graphic design and being able to judge straight lines is critical, my brain has adjusted to the curvature. I don't even notice it anymore.

I had no problem attaching the stand. If you move the attachments to the proper angles, they fit perfectly and I used a standard screw driver.

UPDATE: After using for several weeks, I'm seeing flickering/blacking out happening several times an hour. Suspected video card, but issue is not happening with an older 27 inch Dell. Tried different connections and different DisplayPort cables. Definitely a monitor issue. Happens in all types of software.

Searching the Web, I found this is a known issue which apparently needs an RMA to BenQ. I can't recommend this monitor until the issue is resolved!

Furthermore, I'm still having USB issues only with this monitor. BenQ suggested I'm having problems with my motherboard or power supply, but, once again, the BenQ is the only problem. BenQ did not reply when I asked if the USB 3.0 hub within the monitor is powered. If it is not, that is a problem!

***************************************************
Original review:

Fantastic for photo editing. I was hesitant to go with such a large screen and such high resolution, but the combination is great.

I had been using a Dell U2713HM as my primary monitor for several years, but with a recent system build using an Asus GeForce GTX 1070 Ti, I wanted something better that didn't break the bank. I did not go with a much more expensive wide-gamut model because 100% sRGB is sufficient for my inkjet printer, and, of course, is all that any website can currently display.

My concerns about the 4k resolution had to do with reading text and working with programs such as Photoshop and Lightroom, plus a bunch of plug-ins. I also use Word and Excel. They all scale fine for me in Windows 10. For Lightroom, I do all the work on the BenQ, but for Photoshop, I put only the image window on it, keeping the tools and various boxes and tabs on the older Dell 27". For accuracy, I do bring the color swatch over to the BenQ when I need to paint.

As for the image quality of the BenQ, all the positive adjectives have been used in many other reviews! Stunning, jaw-dropping, amazing, gorgeous...

The stand is solid, and overall build quality seems excellent. The placement of the connection ports on the side works well for my set up.

The one problem I've had is with the onboard USB 3.0 hub. It has conflicts with my Asus Z370 motherboard, actually disabling one of the desktop's front ports and even causing sporadic problems with my mouse and keyboard connected to the back of the desktop. For now I've disconnected the monitor's USB cable. Even before ordering the monitor I had planned to install a PCI-e to USB 3.0 expansion card because my Asus motherboard doesn't provide enough USB connections anyway. Once that is squared away I'll update this review. Note that I haven't yet contacted BenQ, which I should! (I'm not dinging a star for this now because, honestly, with a new desktop build, I can't yet say whether this problem is on Asus or BenQ.)

To sum up, this is an outstanding monitor, absolutely up to professional photography standards. I'm very glad I purchased it and would highly recommend it to friends.

This monitor is my main one. I love it. The picture is great, the refresh is perfect. However, I've owned 2, and both have had issues with the internal hardware, it seems.

My first monitor lasted one week. Out of the box, it refused to sleep or turn off, even if I used the power button on the back. There was also one spot on the screen which became significantly hotter than the rest of the screen. I trouble-shooted everything I could think of, and finally sent it back.

I've had my second one, the one I'm writing this review with, for about a year now. As I'm typing, I'm getting black-out flickering in which the entire screen goes black and takes a few seconds for the display to return. It's not turning off, it's flickering. It'll do this about 2-4 times in a row before I get my screen back, but it occurs every 30 minutes roughly.

My very first monitor for my computer is a Sceptre noncurved, 23.5" display. I've had it for two years with no issue which I use simultaneously with my other one. Sceptre makes amazing products in comparison to the price. However, there seem to be very specific issues to this monitor. My advice is to compare this review to others and use your discretion when purchasing. I received two of these (22-inch model) as a temporary solution for my home workstation. Originally, I was using a LG B6 OLED television as a monitor and the sheer size of the screen was just getting too much for me, regardless of the superb quality of the OLED technology. Yes, I know, going from a top-of-the-line OLED TV to an economical ViewSonic 22-inch IPS is like comparing a Lamborghini to a Ford Focus, so I'll be fair with my review of the ViewSonic VX2276-SMHD 22" IPS 1080p.

My first impression of this monitor was, "This is a cheap piece of crap!". It weighs pretty much nothing, including the base, although it seems to be made out of metal. I had some trouble connecting the base to the monitor, it doesn't want to snap-in without being forceful. Which is scary, knowing I saw a review on Amazon of a guy who completely broke the screen because it's easy to snap. Carefully I snapped the base on but I felt like I had to use more force than I should have to knowing how fragile the monitor is.

Everything about the monitor just looks and feels generic and cheap. It looks better in the picture than it does in reality. Coming from a guy who's seen and owned some of the best on the market (LG B6 and my wife has a Dell Ultrasharp) I feel like I have a kid's toy on my workstation. I know, I probably bought the wrong monitor for what I expected, however, this monitor is a bit higher than anything else on the 22" market. Also, we've had Hell with the Dell Ultrasharp with it going into sleep mode while using dual monitors, my wife complains almost daily about that and the reviews online confirms. So, this ViewSonic seemed like a natural choice. However, I feel I made a mistake and should've just gone with a Dell, regardless of its issues.

Here are the positives of the ViewSonic I have so far while comparing to my other monitor I own which is also a thin bezel design U2415 (Dell Ultrasharp:
+It has actual buttons, I hated the touch sensitive controls on the Dell Ultrasharp (Although the buttons feel cheap)
+Minimal back light bleed (Probably because it's not bright at all, the max brightness is still really dull)
+Plenty of menu options but the only one that looks semi-decent is "Web" mode. Everything else looks like washed out or too sharp.
+It's low profile, exactly what I wanted for my workstation to keep my desk clean. That's about the only thing good about this monitor honestly.
+It has a single DisplayPort with 1.1 support.
+It has an HDMI port.
+It has a VGA port.
+It has speakers, although crappy, it has them regardless.
+It has an audio jack for external speakers so you don't have to listen to the ViewSonic's Motorola Razr speaker.

Negatives:
-Plenty of IPS glow.
-The black level is awful and all you can see is IPS glow and grays.
-External power supply and doesn't use a standard power plug.
-No DVI port, although I think DVI is kinda dead now anyways, so not really a con.
-Games I tested had noticeable ghosting, like really bad ghosting, it felt like I was playing on a TN model monitor from circa 2005. It looks far worse than what the advertised MS delay is. It could be the IPS glow/backlight bleeding or something, but it doesn't look right. I tried all the settings by the way in gaming and it's all pretty bad with ghosting.

Other thoughts:
-"Web" mode looks actually half-way decent and "almost" compared to my Dell Ultrasharp on sRBG mode although the contrast is much worse.
-Text is sharp, although tiny on a 22", I recommend going up to 24" if you get this monitor at least.
-Normally you're advised to turn Sharpness down to 0 on TVs and monitors but on this monitor I recommend turning it up to max 100 because anything below that and it becomes annoyingly blurry on this panel.
-I recommend going to Color Adjust - Color Temp. - and switching to sRGB
-After switching, go to brightness and max it 100, contrast won't be an option.
-Make sure to set color range to FULL RANGE
-On your computer, make sure your video card settings is set to FULL outpout dynamic range, my nvidia card was default limited, you don't want limited, unless you like washed out colors.
-Sharpness 100.
-ViewMode: Web
-After all of those settings, I was able to get this panel to look "almost" as good as a Dell Ultrasharp that I own. I have to say, that is pretty impressive seeing as this monitor is half the price.

Seriously though, the stand on this monitor is poor garbage. Both of my monitors wobble a bit if you touch them, it's not even stable to the touch. It's so cheap it's unreal.

TLDR:
If you can get it for $320-350 - then an easy 5 stars. Otherwise, it's the subjective view of how the text quality appears that may cause you to pause on this purchase. If you play games and don't have a super expensive video card (like the RX480) - then this monitor is an easy buy.

Details:
Text quality / Resolution / DPI/PPI ([...])
The one negative thing about this monitor is how the text quality appears when you are up close. Think of how the text looks on a big screen TV when you connect a computer. This monitor has a PPI of 82 (Full HD 55" TV has 40, Full HD 24" monitor has 92, Ultra-Wide 29" has 95).
Basically, it all depends on how close you plan on being to this monitor and using it for office productivity. With web sites the type font is not that noticeable, but with word, excel, and outlook - the "pixelation" is there. There is a "Photo" mode setting that really helps and eventually your eyes will adjust. For me it wasn't bad enough to be a deal breaker, but that's definitely a subjective observation...so mileage may vary.

Physical Size ([...])
The other item of consideration is the the footprint.
The 34" has the height of a regular 27" monitor.
The 29" has the height of a regular 24" monitor.

The monitor really shines with playing games and there are a few sites ([...]) that can help with configuration for those games where you can use the full screen to all its glory. Additionally, there is LG software that will allow you to switch easily between modes and custom profiles for regular use and gaming.

I'm not quite sure where to begin with this review. I guess I should start by stating, right up front, that the VP2290B (and it's twin, the IBM T221) can NOT be used -- or even considered for use -- as a "general purpose" display for your computer. It's a *highly* specialized display that is really meant to be used to display one thing and one thing only -- STATIC images such as photographs, CAD/CAE drawings, and other *very* high resolution graphics.

You simply can NOT use this monitor as a primary Windows (or any other OS) display. You'll need a second "normal" display for that purpose running from a completely separate display adapter (I use a 24" LCD). The VP2290 can't be run from a standard graphics adapter -- even the latest and greatest like an nVidia 7900GTX/512 or an ATI X1900XTX. It needs MORE! You'll need either a Matrox Parhelia 256/PCI or a Quadro FX 4500 or above. To run it require BOTH DVI ports simultaneously via two "funky" non-standard DVI cables. Basically, the display "stitches" four "panels" together into one seamless display -- with staggering clarity.

With the VP2290 you can edit an image from the latest 8MP digital SLR at 100% and fit the ENTIRE image on the display. GULP! The brightness/contrast specs may not inspire awe, but believe me when I say that they are quite misleading. My 24" LCD has double the stated B/C specs but the VP still looks brighter and holds more shadow detail.

You can't play video or prettymuch ANYTHING with motion (forget gaming) because the "refresh" is only 50Hz. Even web Flash animations blur and ghost -- but if you're into Photoshop and have a high-MP camera then this is, in no uncertain terms, THE Big, Bad Wolf.

Forget about text. Sure it redefines "razor sharp," but you'll need an electron microscope to see any of it. Resolution-wise it absolutely DWARFS the current 30" mega-displays on the market with DOUBLE the gross pixel count -- in a considerably smaller area. You do the math. Ideally I'd like to have a 30" for my general-purpose display and the VP2290 as my graphic editor (don't put ANYTHING other than the image on it. Menus will disappear into postage-stamp sized boxes on the VP). Some dreams are just meant to come true.

To sum up, the VP2290 has a very narrow performance envelope. If you are a graphics editor of one sort or another -- STATIC graphics -- then the VP is the apex predator of the display universe. Nothing else can even come close. It makes a 30" display look like it has glowing tennis balls for pixels. The resolution is SO high that it is simply impossible to see any pixels with the naked eye. It looks like you're seeing a continuous tone image even with your face plastered to the screen. Color reproduction is flawless, and I don't use that word lightly. The unit itself is big, thick, and bulky for an LCD, but it's still no CRT.

I'd LOVE to see this resolution in a fast 40" made-for-PC display for $3,000 or less. They couldn't manufacture enough of them.

- I received this monitor two days ago. So far I am amazed with it.

-- Pros:
- I am able to charge, view, and control my MacBook 15inch 2018 model with single USB-C cable.
- The KVM switch works perfectly, I can easily switch between my Work Windows laptop and my personal MacBook with click of a button. I have the bluetooth dongle(connector) for my keyboard and mouse connected to the USB port 2.0 on the back of the monitor.
- The way my setup works is: Work Laptop is connected via HDMI and a USB cable(Coming out of the USB-C port for PC-In) and my MacBook connected to the USB-C port with 85W power delivery. If you want the KVM to work on the HDMI connected device then you also have to connect the PC-In USB cable to that device, haven't tried it with Displayport connection but I am guessing that is how that works as well. Hope this made sense.
- Due to the Matt finish of the screen, there is no glare and I am able to switch to eye-saver mode which displays similar to the color the Mac does only thing is it has to be manually enabled and disabled, not an issue for me.
- Picture in Picture works great, there are a few different modes and sizes you can change it from, which is great when you want to work on different devices at the same time.
- The packaging was done great, the monitor box came inside another box so double the protection, I am guessing some users mentioned that they had issue with screen when they received the it, and must have improved packaging due to that.

-- Con:
- Samsung needs to improve the Easy Setting Box software to distribute screens. Many times it does not work and not all the applications support that on a Windows based PC. The software is not offered on a MAC.

Overall the first impressions and couple days of use has been great, I hope Samsung can improve the software. I will update the review as I use the Monitor more and if I find more Pros & Cons.

No flickering issues yet. Based on other reviews, Acer *seems* to be pretty good about replacing monitors with the issue. Colors and brightness are quite good out of the box (as expected of IPS). Very little backlight bleed which is super common on IPS displays. IPS glow is very minimal. No dead pixels.

It's marketed as HDR400 which is -sounds- good, especially with the "VESA-certified" tag... but good it is not. If you are looking specifically for an HDR capable monitor, I would avoid anything below HDR500, and always make sure it is VESA-certified.

I've seen other reviews mentioning this display might have some displayport issues. The somewhat useless HDR won't work via displayport, for example. HDMI works totally fine though.

You also can't enable some other pretty important gaming-focused features of the monitor (like VRB) with either freesync/g-sync or HDR on.

For gaming, there is some noticeable ghosting & blur even with 1ms VRB set to normal or extreme. VRB set to extreme makes the monitor especially dim, with not much improvement over the "normal" setting. I would recommend sticking to normal. (VRB, or Visual Response Boost, is Acer's blur-reducing tech, similar to Nvidia's ULMB). I might be really spoiled though, since my other 144hz TN display has Lightboost strobing with absolutely non-existent ghosting or blur.

Nvidia 10xx series or newer will supposedly work with this monitor's freesync, but I haven't been able to test it with my G-sync-only GTX 970.

-----

Overall I'm still pretty happy with the display, even with somewhat noticeable blur & low-quality HDR issues. OSD menu is the best I've ever used on a display. Bezels are nice. Great contrast, colors and brightness. A nice balance of all things if you don't mind a little bit of motion blur in fast paced games.

I'm a ruby/java developer who uses a Macbook Pro mid 2014 retina 13 inch. I have a TV for movies and video games so I don't care about those aspects. I bought this monitor for one reason only- productivity. If I had all the money in the world, I'd have gotten the iMac 5K. I was also debating getting a 40 inch 4K TV instead of a monitor, but glad that I didn't make that mistake.

This monitor comes with 2 cables - HDMI and DisplayPort. Neither of these are ideal for connecting a Mac to this monitor- you need the following cable for sure- http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B00C7RIL3C

I first connected the HDMI cable and managed to get the full 4K resolution but only at 30Hz. Now 30Hz is workable for short periods of time, it's not that bad, but you can feel a bit of delay and weight moving your cursor around and when typing at full speed. You can get 60Hz by using the cable above, at the full 4K resolution. As soon as I connected the Mac, I got a default resolution of 1080p with very smooth fonts, which means that Mac was scaling up the text and font sizes- pretty good in general but not for a programmer. So I switched over to the full 4K resolution and opened a project in my Atom editor. That screenshot is attached. Now a lot of reviews say you need to scale up the fonts because it's a 28inch screen, not enough for 4K, bla bla, but I find this size and scaling delightful. My eyes are about 3 feet away from the monitor, and this text is very sharp and crisp and lets me have a much bigger perspective of my code than on the laptop alone. Chrome(and other browsers) at this resolution don't look so good for most sites, so you want to keep it as a small window.

For cons, as all the other reviews say, this is a TN panel and not an IPS panel, so the viewing angles are restricted, but it manages to be consistent through about 45 degrees. This monitor doesn't come with audio or speakers, and the headphone jack is behind at the center, very awkward for putting in actual headphones, and the UX for controlling volume is awkward too. Did Samsung not think this through? Well they designed it to go with computer speakers such as 2.1 systems. The audio interface would have been a lot more usable if the headphone jack and volume controls were right in front. Another place the overly-minimalistic backside might hurt- the number of USB ports is 0, keep that in mind if your keyboard or mouse use USB.

Considering that a full HD monitor of this size costs 4 times less than this, is it worth it for 4 times as many pixels? For a programmer, absolutely.

Okay, after using the monitor for a few months, I must admit that I have found it to have been a better purchase than I had originally commented on. Follow-up from March, 2020 - Pandemic time.
After posting the 3/3/2020 update, the HDMI port began to fail. I had to put my external battery underneath the connector to complete a connection. This isn't very convenient. I am lowering the review to two stars. It probably works okay if you use the USB-C connector.

UPDATE 12/20/2020
Okay although the HDMI connector is shot, the USB-C connector is okay.
So, here is my work around.

I bought the following two items:
1) HDMI to DisplayPort, Benfei HDMI to DisplayPort Adapter Resolution Up to 4K@60Hz Compatible with Laptop, Xbox 360 One, PS4 PS3 HDMI Device - HDMI Input to DisplayPort Output

2)AmazonBasics Bi-Directional USB-C to DisplayPort Cable - 6-Foot

Okay, connect the HDMI to Displayport to the laptop HDMI out. The plug the Bi-Directional USB-C to DisplayPort Cable to the output of the HDMI to Displayport, and the USB-C connector to the input of the Lepow monitor, on the same side as the HDMI connector. Connect the USB-C on the right side of the monitor to power, possibly on an external power bank. I also connect the HDMI to Displayport power to the same power bank. The result works fine. Full monitor use is restored. The cost of the extra add-ons is about $45. So, my arrangement is still less than having to buy a new monitor, and everything is working again.
Not quite as good as the built in screen on my MacBook Pro, but still a great buy for the money.

I had previously had a Samsung S32D850T which uses an identical A-MVA panel to this 32" Monoprice. However, it was a very early batch and suffered from some issues. First, the color temperature sucked and the entire display always had a slightly orange hue. Whites were never true white. Also, it was defective and layers of the display were separating, which caused waves and blobs in areas of the image. Very annoying and made any sort of color sensitive work very hard to do. And definitely worth noting that the Samsung's backlighting wasn't as bright as this display either. Overall the Samsung was a disappointing monitor.

Enter the Monoprice 32 WQHD. Now THIS is a monitor. Flawless display, better color, and Freesync. Great brightness and perfect contrast. A total upgrade in every area except the monitor stand itself which is not quite as fancy as the Samsung's. The Monoprice also comes with a remote, which is awesome. Overall build is pretty decent, and the included stand seems to be aluminum or steel which screws together rigidly. The monitor is very stable when put together, and doesn't really rock or wiggle around on me. The stand is a little wide though due to its design, and takes quite a bit of desk space. I have an AMP and DAC on my desk as well that fit perfectly under the Samsung monitor, but had to be tweaked a little with the Monoprice.

32" is getting to be a common size for large displays, and 1440p is a great match for it. I do tons of work, play, and multitasking on this monitor, and it's always perfectly crisp and detailed. The pixel density doesn't really approach a 4K in the same size, but still very respectable. I have plenty of desktop realestate to display two full browser windows side by side, or have several explorer windows open at once to maintain efficient workflow. Likewise it works great for games, and I can see all the little details in textures and environments. Also fantastic for rendering CRT filters in RetroArch, which aren't quite right with a 1080p display. Added bonus is that it gives you that "more than 1080p huge display" experience without the massive performance hit of 4K. This already takes a pretty healthy 40-50% hit over 1080p in some very demanding games, so every little bit of performance you can get is a good thing.

The color accuracy for this monitor is spot-on, and is noticeably better than the Samsung. It has a factory sRGB preset in the color options, and I have to say it's about as optimized as this panel gets. It even passed my brightness/contrast calibration tests with stock settings. I couldn't adjust or calibrate it any better than it is out of the box without a colorimeter. Super pleasing. Images are vibrant and bright without any sort of artificial gamma boost. Very natural and easy on the eyes. As with any MVA panel, there is a little shift when you adjust your positioning and angle, but nothing that bothers me. Contrast is to be expected for these modern MVA panels. Blacks are very deep and true, with awesome detail in dark scenes and imagery. Backlight bleed is very faint and evenly distributed around the edges of the screen. Not pitch black, but generally very good. I couldn't be happier with the image quality of this display. It's not a high-end OLED or IPS, but most older LCDs will pale in comparison to this.

The FreeSync range on this monitor is 48-75 which is far past the 60Hz this monitor can normally manage. FreeSync is awesome and blows the 59Hz Samsung out of the water. Nothing anywhere near a proper high refresh rate display, but a nice little step over my previous monitors. Scrolling and turning are silky smooth and tear-free in every game I've tried. Even better than conventional vsync can manage. Overall response time isn't the snappiest so there can be a little ghosting and trailing sometimes, and latency isn't quite as exacting as some 1ms TN panels I've used. However there's nothing that ever bothered me in games, films, or desktop usage. Competitive gamers will still want to stick with low latency TN panels. Most people though will be very happy with the response of this display.

The HDR on this panel can be accessed via the OSD. The display itself does not detect as HDR capable, however it does seem to work okay when watching HDR films translated to SDR. Generally though, it's a useless setting, and you shouldn't consider this 32" model as an HDR display. It just isn't. I'm not sure what exactly the display is trying to do, but it sucks. It is, however, a fantastic 8+2 bit SDR display.

Overall, this was a noticeable step up from my previous monitor using the same panel. This Monoprice is an outright better display and retails for far less. I bought mine direct from Monoprice's site at the $200 sale price and it was a hilariously huge bargain. I bought my Samsung used for $350 and it retails new for $500. Monoprice have built a very nice monitor here with some compelling features, and offered it at a ROCKING price. For my needs, this was the absolute best fit possible.

Having just received the BenQ 3205u display I can say setup is not for the faint at heart. The Quick Start guide supplied was insufficient for me to get a working display with my M1 14” MacBook Pro using USB C to USB C connection. This is my first use of an external display; I am mostly unfamiliar with the many port connections available on an external monitor. My first attempt showed the MacBook recognized the BenQ as a connected device. I could see it in the Display panel. In the Preferences/Display panel when I selected the BenQ, the MacBook display dimmed and the BenQ display said “no cable connected”. So I’m left with a dimmed MacBook screen and a non-working BenQ display. No way to control anything. We if I turned off the BenQ the MacBook came alive and showed only its display screen.

Contacted BenQ support who replied quickly I power cycle the display along with checking all the connection, something I already did. Power cycle did nothing, They did inform me that the display does not auto detect a signal, so you must manually using the five-way controller cycle through the three available inputs. That did the trick, the screen displayed the Mac screen. Nothing in the Quick Start guid suggests this choosing the BenQ input step necessary. Did easily find the full manual on the internet, someone essential to understanding this display.

Once I had a working display I found the text too small for my vision. By adjusting the screen resolution I did get an adequate text size at the expense of a less than full screen. I do need to gain a better understanding of the resolution adjustment. I did a quick Lightroom edit i and am very happy with the display image and its color compared to the MacBook. I understand the BenQ has many additional ports, but at the moment don’t have an understanding of “upstream” and “downstream” when describing a port. I would like to connect to my Epson P600 printer using a USB B cable between the monitor and printer. Can I do that?

To summarize I think that customers unfamiliar with external monitors will struggle to get this working. Better instructions are needed. Also the the provided base is quite sharp. I cut myself trying to position the screen to insert cables, which are in an awkward location. The base has sharp edges, enough that I only recognized the cut when blood appeared on the Starter Guide.

Still I’m hoping this reasonably priced monitor will suit all my needs for editing photos.

The display itself is excellent. I'm using it as an auxiliary monitor for a 27" 4K and as a portable display to use with my 2018 13" MacBook Pro w/4 thunderbolt 3/usb-c ports. It worked right out of the box with just the USB-C cable. MacOS Mojave picked it up immediately and offers plenty of scaling options to choose from. I usually have it below my 27" display scaled to the equivalent of 1680x945 so it's easy to read things on it, but it works very well scaled to a higher equivalent resolution when I'm using it as a primary or portable secondary display.

It has a few downsides, but they are just accessory issues. The "stand" that it comes with is technically for a smartphone or a small tablet. It's even labeled that way. The stand also has a built in bottle opener. Honestly, I'm glad they included something, but it's not really sufficient. It's heavy for what it is and it's difficult to get into a useful configuration. They could do a lot better here. I've ordered the linked leather flip case (kind of wish I had bought it when I bought the display since there was combo discount), so I'll update my review when that arrives. It really seems like the leather case should have been included initially.

The other issue I have, and a very minor one, is that while I appreciate the inclusion of two USB-C cables that work both for video and power, they're quite short (looks like they're 3 feet). I couldn't place the display on the other side of my primary monitor because the cable wouldn't reach and if I needed additional power I wouldn't be able to plug it in. However, I can purchase longer cables to solve this problem, so not a big deal.

Overall, I'm very happy so far and if the display had come with the leather case/stand then I would likely upgrade to 5 stars.

UPDATE 2019-08-02: I've had this display for a little while now and it's still quite nice. I did get the actual case/stand for it and while it is a slight improvement from the "smartphone holder/bottle opener" that came with it, it's still a bit disappointing. [Overview]
This is a fantastic monitor. I have used many different monitors over the years and have a number of different monitors that I currently use on a daily basis. I have been using the ASUS 27" rog swift 1440p 144Hz Gsync monitor as my main monitor for about a year now with 2 27" ASUS 60Hz IPS 1080p side monitors - I purchased this monitor to replace the two IPS monitors.

[My Setup]
Now, I use my rog swift as a gaming monitor. It does not have a high-quality panel and that's just fine for games that need a high refresh rate. But I also do work, code, read, and watch movies/shows and an assortment of other multi-tasking items so having additional real-estate on a nicer panel is something that is attractive to me. I'm a bit of a neat freak regarding my desk space so I like to have my monitors up on vesa mounts with the cables tucked away nicely. Neither of my 2 IPS side monitors were vesa mountable, and their bases took up a lot of space - the viewing angles weren't anything special either. I wanted a versitile monitor with a lot of real estate, a nice panel, vesa mount support, and a nice panel.

[Why I chose this monitor]
- Mountability (I think that's a word?): I wanted a monitor with vesa mount support to easily allow me to adjust the monitor's tilt and position, while freeing up desk-space.

- Resolution: This monitor has a 3440x1440 max resolution. The loss of sharpness of 1080p is more noticeable at larger screen sizes so at least 1440p was a must for me.

- Size: The ultrawide 21:9 aspect ratio comes in 3 sizes, 25", 29" and 34". Since I was going to replace 2 27" monitors, I wanted as much real-estate as possible so I wanted the 34 inches rather than the 29.

- Panel: This monitor has a VA panel which is kind of like the midway point between a tn panel and an IPS. It's tough for VA and IPS panels to reach the same refresh rates as a tn panel which is why most gaming monitors use TN panels. For visual quality though, VA and IPS are vastly superior. VA and IPS each have a different set of tradeoffs you will have to decide between. In a VA panel, for true black, the LEDs are literally powered down, whereas they aren't in an IPS panel, making blacks and darks look darker with sharper contrast then in an IPS. Also, IPS panels are notorious for light-bleed from many different manufacturers - whereas that isn't nearly as much of a problem, if at all in a VA panel. The difference comes in the brightness and visual fidelity of IPS panels though. Your colors will likely look brighter and more vivid in an IPS than in a VA. To get an IPS panel in this form factor, you pretty much have to either go with the ACER Predator or wait for the ASUS ultrawide coming out later this year. I didn't want to wait nor did I want to spend $1300.

- Dell Screen Management Software: This software is BAD-ASS. There are a bunch of preset screen dividers on a menu which sits down on your task-bar. Selecting one will set up invisible dividers on that screen so you can quickly snap windows to the portion of the screen that you want it. Me personally, I like to have Netflix/Amazon on the left 2/3 of the screen and then like word, chrome, or whatever else I'm doing on the right 1/3 of the screen. Changing things up is as easy as clicking on the icon in the taskbar and selecting a different pre-set divider.

- Input diversity: This monitor supports HDMI2.0, Display Port, mini-Display Port, and usb 3.0 which is great - I can even plug my laptop into it with a mini-DP cable if I wanted to. An awesome feature which I haven't tried yet is the support for Picture In Picture (pip) where you can simultaneously display content from multiple input sources. I will update this review when I test this functionality, though the quality of this feature will likely not change my impression of this monitor, as all of the other features work so splendidly.

- Refresh rate: This monitor is 60Hz which is pretty par for the course with most non-gaming monitors these days. Since I don't plan to game on this monitor, the 60Hz refresh rate and low response timing weren't an issue.

- Oh yeah, this monitor is curved and I love it. The curve is subtle and not very obvious when looking at it straight-on, but it definitely adds a nice bit of immersion given how wide the screen is.

[Summary]
In conclusion, this monitor is everything I had hoped it would be from the build quality to the software - it's the real deal. If you are looking for a large form factor monitor and aren't concerned with 4k or high-refresh rate gaming, this is definitely one to consider.

Check back later for pictures and PIP support review when I update this review.

The one hassle has been connectivity. There is DVI and displayport, maybe VGA (?), but definitely no HDMI. For a single monitor setup, this isn't an issue. For multimonitor it is a source of frustration. For switching between a work PC and a home PC, or three computers in my case, it is a major hassle. There is no shortage of HDMI sharing switches, for 3, 4, 5 different inputs. But for displayport, there's like one option with two inputs on all of Amazon that doesn't get pricey. Using an adapter to convert from DVI to HDMI is not an option in this case, because most of them will not support resolutions beyond 1920x1200. Or they do, but the maximum refresh rate is 30 hz, providing an unacceptable viewing experience. If the company plans on selling this in the future, they would be well advised to make this model compatible with daisy chaining, or change the displayport to HDMI instead.

As many customers and professional reviewers have pointed out, this model of monitor is incapable of showing a gamut of contrast and color that is anywhere near HDR quality. In fact, its contrast and color gamut are worse than many high-range gaming monitors that predate any HDR standard. You have to set this monitor to permanent HDR mode by manipulating the buttons on it and turning off Brightness+ and other ambient brightness altering settings, and only then is its brightness and vibrancy just equal to a normal 80$ display.

Basically if you want a good image and good gaming, get a 1440p screen with great brightness and contrast. Ignore this HDR sham since there's obviously zero guarantee from BenQ or any other company that they will give you a real HDR monitor for the money. It's just a sticker and an advertising campaign that means absolutely nothing in this case. A complete fraud.

I've owned it for nearly a year now and while I noticed the lack of HDR capability right away, other annoyances have taken time to show themselves. About half the time when my PC goes on standby, the monitor doesn't come back from standby automatically, so all my active windows and taskbar end up shunted off to whatever screen Windows 10 now thinks is the primary instead of where I need them and where I left them. It's an annoying pointless task that I've had to repeat many times now.

For awhile the monitor speakers kept trying to assert themselves as the primary audio device despite me never using them deliberately ever, so while I was listening on headphones audio would start blaring through the terrible monitor speakers and filling my house. Imagine how awkward THAT can be, and the paranoia that your PC is going to blare your media files at maximum volume instead of through your headphones for no apparent reason. Once I disabled the monitor speakers as devices for the 2nd or 3rd time it finally stuck, though.

I had to shut off most of the comfort features, such as 'Brightness+' or whatever they call it, because they degraded the image quality very hard.

I do have permanent desks at home and office, so is easy to connect my laptops to one or more external displays. When I'm on the go, I cannot just simply pack a large monitor. On those occasions the SVANTTO M26 16-Inch 2K IPS 120Hz Portable Gaming Monitor comes very handy.
The monitor has a thin and light design, so it can be easily tucked away in a laptop bag or a backpack.
It also draws power directly from a laptop’s USB-C port, so there is not need to carry another power adapter in your bag. Of course, when connecting devices via HDMI you will need auxiliary power.

The SVANTTO M26 monitor is very simple to operate, if you have use any OSD controls. It has 2 buttons on one side, one for power and settings selection and a rocking button for navigating the OSD options. Below the buttons there is an audio jack. On the opposite side there is a Mini-HDMI, and two USB Type C for video and power. The unit has has pair of speakers, one on each side and they are as good as speakers found on slim laptops.

Most portable monitors have low brightness when compared to regular monitors, but the SVANTTO M26 went above the advertised brightness reaching 490 nits at 100% brightness. This is plenty even for well lit environments.
The monitor could draw close to 12W of power at full brightness. That might not be much power, but some laptops USB-C ports might not be able to handle it, especially when running on a battery. Make sure to have an outlet nearby or an inexpensive power bank.

This SVANTTO M26 portable IPS monitor worked fine with every device I connected. I tried MacBooks, Chromebooks, and Windows laptops. It also worked with a couple of Android smartphones with a USB-C to mini-HDMI cable, and an iPad when using a Lightning to HDMI cable.
It reached 130Hz when paired to MacBook Pro, and on a gaming laptop with a RTX 3060 it hit 154 FPS running at 130Hz. It also worked just fine with a laptop with AMD Radeon, since it does support FreeSync adaptive synchronization technology.

Make sure to check your device specifications, since getting over 60Hz from an external display is not always as easy as it should be. I have found that some gaming laptops could have a 120Hz or 144Hz display, but might not support over 60Hz when using an external display.
Some other laptops, might go above 60Hz when the laptop display is disabled and only the external display is projecting.
Some other laptops might go above 60Hz when using the appropriate connection or if they are plugged to a power source. Some laptops might not support 120Hz over HDMI and some might not even support an external monitor over USB-C port.

The SVANTTO M26 portable IPS Monitor performs smoothly at 120-130Hz refresh rate and it is also gorgeous with very good color accuracy, excellent Gamut and tone response. The unit gets bright, but its luminance is not that uniform. It could also be use for photo and video editing, since it also supports 100% of the sRGB color coverage, 76% AdobeRGB, 79% of P3 and 72% of NTSC.

The SVANTTO M26 portable IPS Monitor comes with a couple of high quality USB-C to USB-C cables, a mini-HDMI to HDMI cable and a 15W power adapter. It also provides a high quality magnetic cover, which also serves as the monitor stand. The only downside of the stand is that only have two available tilt angles.

The SVANTTO M26 portable IPS monitor is a bargain considering is gaming capabilities, 2K resolution, color accuracy, brightness and build quality.
It is also compact, lightweight and it works great when paired with gaming laptops and other supported devices with either HDMI or USB-C connections.

Well...

To start off, heres what came in my box (as of July 8, 2017)
1x DVI Dual Link Cable (DON'T USE THIS GARBAGE CABLE THAT COMES WITH THE MONITOR!! More info down below)
1x ASUS VG248QE Monitor
1x ASUS VG248QE Monitor Drivers CD (I'm not exactly sure what this is, but it's in the box! lol)
1x Audio Cable
1x Power Cable
1x Manual

Actual monitor review:
I have owned this monitor for exactly a month! It's definitely the cheapest 144Hz monitor that you will find at this price range (as of this review it is fluctuating around $250). If you are coming from a 60Hz monitor then you will definitely be able to see the difference. (It's life changing! No more strain on the eyes!) The way the monitor is built is also outstanding. You can turn it in all directions possible! The vertical height of this monitor is astounding, and turning it horizontally is a breeze with the lazy susan type of thing it sits on. I really only have two issues with this monitor, and both of the issues can easily be resolved. My first issue would be that the ENTIRE monitor is a glossy finish which results in a lot of fingerprints and visible dust collection, but that isn't a issue with the monitor, more on personal preference. My other issue is that when you first get this monitor plugged in and ready to go and turn it on, your eyes will bleed. The colors that this monitor is preset to is awful. The presets that they have in the menu option are also awful. It hurt my eyes when I first turned it on and configuring it to get it to a usable color saturation was difficult as this monitor really favors yellow hues (at least in my opinion). But the color issue I fixed within the first hour of owning it so no biggie!

Some tips and tricks if you have issues with your monitor (Such as a flickering red pixel problem like me):
I suggest that when you receive your monitor to go and check for dead pixels (just type in dead pixel test in Google). I did this when I first got it and it was clear for dead pixels, yay! My reason for 4 stars though is because exactly after a month (the time you get to refund an item no questions asked on Amazon) I got flickering red pixels on my screen when I watched videos or played games!! Yikes!! That freaked me out! I thought I was out $250, lol. Google is my best friend though and I passionately looked and hoped for a fix to flickering red pixels.

Most of the solutions were to check my GPU. Everything was already updated though and at this point I was pretty sad. Then I saw a solution which was "try switching out the DVI Cable". I thought this was so stupid but at the same time so brilliant! (Or maybe I thought it was stupid because I didn't even think of doing that at first...hehe) I used the cable that came with the box originally. I just went out to my local electronics store and picked up a DVI dual link cable and BOOM! No more flickering red pixels!!! So I would definitely say DO NOT use the cable that comes with the box. It will give you a very big headache and a sense of impending doom...

I hope this review helped!

Maybe it wouldn't bother other people, I don't know. That's not the monitor's fault, but it is what it is.

The backlight also seems to have a very slightly flickering to it that I can notice if I pay attention. Maybe I just expected too much from a 32" 4k panel for $350.

Last month I bought the Philips 2k 32" curved monitor (model 328E9FJAB), which is noticeably brighter, has better color and no noticeable flickering backlight. I thought the pixels were just slightly too large with a 32" 2k, but overall that monitor is much better, so I think I will be returning the 4k and keeping the 2k.

Ordered and received this monitor. Couldn't get it to keep working. Apparently, the USB connector built in to the back of the monitor was faulty. I would get a screen then it would go away after a few seconds. (I tried it with two other cables like the one that came in the box with the same result.) I ended up returning it and buying a different brand.

Note: The USB on the monitor back was a design that I hadn't seen in a long time. It's the one that is relatively flat and is separated into two sections. The other brand that I purchased (and am happy with to date) uses a mini-USB port like found on many cameras. Its connection feels rock solid; this product felt anything but.

Possibly, I got the lemon but I thought I would share my thoughts.

I am using this monitor on a 17" Late 2010 Macbook pro. I opened the box, plugged it in, downloade the driver from the website, restarted, and it came up exactly as I'd expect. It was nice and simple, and definitely a plus that its completely USB driven... I don't even have to use the second port to drive the power for the monitor.

Using it, the performance is ok, but not great. I understand what other reviewers mean when they say they can 'almost see the pixels'. I can definitely see them - its not distracting, except that the 'squareness' of the pixels is apparant, almost like bad compression artifacts.

The speed is fine for dragging windows across monitors, but video lags slightly when compared to the main display. Noting I wasn't expecting over USB, but other buyers should be aware.

The major issue I have with it is its inability to work with Keynote. Whether I set it up as a second monitor or mirroring the main display, the presentation being run from keynote refuses to play to it - the monitor is just blank. I do a fair amount of classroom teaching and public speaking, and this is almost a deal-breaker for me. I might return it for this. If I were just using it as a second monitor for development, text processing, etc it would be a welcome addition to my travel bag.

This black ITRIVIEW Portable Monitor, 15.6 Inch FHD 1080P USB-C Laptop Monitor with Built-in Dual Stereo Speaker, HDR IPS HDMI Computer Removable Protective Case Stand for Surface, PC, PS4, Xbox, Switch is really handy to have. I have a lot of writing to do, and I'm used to having two screens. I use one screen on my PC to write and the other to read my source material. When I don't want to sit at my desk, I can use my laptop, but switching between my editor and my texts becomes tedious because I only have one screen. This issue is addressed by this portable monitor. It includes a power cable as well as three different connection cables. The instructions show all of the possible connections. It was very simple. My laptop has a USB-C port, so I could connect it that way and I didn't need to use the power cable making this screen truly portable. This screen turns on and duplicates your screen as soon as you plug it in. You can then easily change the laptop's settings to extend the screen instead. I own an XPS 15, and this portable screen is roughly the same size, which works well. I like how light this screen is, as well as the fact that it comes with a case. It is simple to transport in a backpack. It took me a minute to figure out how to set up the case, but once I did, the screen stayed up nicely. This screen is not attached to the case; it is held in place by magnets. The settings were fairly simple to manipulate, and I didn't need to read the instructions to use them. You must use a small lever to move through them, but you need to do so with caution. I am a little worried about breaking this little lever and then not having any options to change the settings anymore. This screen's colors aren't as warm as on my laptop. I wasn't able to make it match completely, but felt it was good enough for my needs. Overall, I like the convenience of this screen and believe I will make extensive use of it. However, there is room for improvement in terms of color and how to change the settings.

Twice I received the -P version of the monitor, not the -PK. I even contacted customer support before the second monitor was shipped and was assured I'd get the -PK model. Nope, got the -P model. The difference between the two is a not-inexpensive color calibration tool. Otherwise the two models are the same (though the labeling of the mini-LEDs is different). So, if you order the -PK monitor, make sure you are shipped a -PK box so you don't have to spend extra money and time to get the X-rite i1 Display Pro calibration tool.

Also, there is a similar monitor out from ASUS that is much better for gaming. The PA32UCX-PK only has 60hz, which is fine for photography and most video workflows.

For me the U2415 replaces an old Apple Cinema 23 HD display. For both I've used my Retina MacBook's HDMI port because my 2 Thunderbolt ports are in use with adapters. If you use HDMI be sure to change your sound settings to direct sound out back to your computer's headphone (speaker system) port since the computer defaults, when sensing HDMI, to sending sound out via HDMI. You'll need to buy a 6' HDMI cable separately (and I don't know if it's possible to adapt display port or mini-display port to Thunderbolt so do that research if you need to). Note: if you're a Mac person unfamiliar with a potpourri of monitor ports and planning to use the supplied Display Port to Mini-display port (like for my wife's Mini), take care to use the correct port; this display can daisy chain and has two display port ports, one each in and out.

I love the ultra-minimal bezel. Having the option to rotate 90º/vertical could be useful occasionally for this graying graphic designer making his own author "onesheets" or other layout tasks, but it requires changing system display prefs BEFORE rotating to avoid eye-hand-mouse-brain damage.

The OCD's touch buttons are no big problem that some folks say. Even in the dark. You won't use the OCD much and it's no big deal to glide or poke around a few presses and you've found a button to launch the OCD.

I too like how the silver plastic matches my laptop's aluminum. I wish the Dell's base was flat; it's actually very slightly beveled on each side, making it impractical to store much if anything on that expansive base. The stand is attractive, easy to deal with, and I adapted a second U2415 for my wife's Mac Mini to an old articulating HP stand that I fashioned my own 100mm VESA mounting plate for—so she can get her Dell's bottom edge right down to her desktop (Dell's stand leaves several inches' gap).

It's a visually solid, crisp, colorful, bright display. Color-wise, my only wish would be the ability to calibrate it to match my Retina's screen. I'm not a color guy but it seems like the Retina has a bit more red. This is no big deal but, if it were to be able to goose the tone—without screwing up the whiteness used for text files, I'd do it. Probably requires a pro-grade calibration tool.

It's not possible, and it would be sensible, to change from this displays nice tall 16:10 ratio to gaming's 16:9 via Dell's own settings. However, if on the Mac you could try using the inexpensive app Display Menu, which DOES offer 1920x1080 (16:9).

This review is for the ViewSonic XG3220 4K Ultra HD. Not sure why Amazon combines reviews from several other different Viewsonic models for added confusion (I'm aware you can filter but not everyone does) when trying to shop for the perfect monitor.

Pros
+The design of the monitor is nice to look at. Solid construction. Especially since its 32".
+ The added RGB to the stem of the monitor serves no purpose but is nice to look at as well, if you like RGB on your electronics.
+ Easy assembly. No issues attaching base/stem to the back of the monitor. There is an option for a VESA mount if you choose to.

Cons
-The viewing angels are some of the worst I've seen on any monitor. Its weird, you would think from the center point of the view would be the best but if I leaned to the right of the monitor it would be the most clear and brightest. Unfortunately, I prefer not to play my games in a fixed leaning position.
-I thought it would be a pro for having an audio jack on the monitor since I share my monitor between my consoles and PC but the audio is way too low even when cranked up to 100. I have the audio fed into my audio mixer, even with the dial turned all the way up its still too low.
-Pretty severe light bleeding in the top and bottom quadrants of the monitor. It wasn't too noticeable when there wasn't any dark areas/scenes being played but if there were it drove me crazy. Adjusting brightness and gamma didn't seem to help at all.
-Colors seemed off. No matter how much I adjusted the settings or changed modes the colors always seemed really washed out or overly bright. Text also seemed difficult to read pending on the color it was up against in the background.
-Lets talk about the OSD controls. They are not only awful but extremely infuriating. The buttons are on the back of the monitor and labeled on the front. You have the Mode selector at the top and the power button at the bottom. In between those buttons are four unidentified diamond shaped labeled buttons as you'll notice in the product pictures. Its difficult to tell exactly which button you are hitting, 9 times out of 10 I would end up powering off the monitor or entering/exiting a menu I didn't intend to.

Overall, I can't recommend this monitor. I ended up returning it and going with a tad more expensive (worth it) Dell U3219Q and have no regrets.

The thing I've found most refreshing about this monitor is its color faithfulness. Right out of the box, I did some image manipulation on it, then printed the images to one of our Canon Pro100 printers, on Kirkland Glossy Photo Paper (13x19). Amazingly, the prints' colors were spot-on with what I'd seen on the monitor, which virtually never happens. The colors were faithful enough that I've not even bothered to calibrate the monitor's color settings. YMMV, of course, but the monitor I received is very color-faithful.

I like the size, which is not so small that I can't fit enough on the screen, but also not so large that it's unnatural for my eyes to have to jump too far from one side of the monitor to the other.

Interestingly, this monitor replaced a much more expensive 4K one that's known for its color accuracy. My primary machine is a Win10 laptop that will output 4K, but doesn't have a built-in 4K screen. This caused me issues (having a 1920x1080 built-in and a 4K external), so I downgraded the external to 1920x1080 and am glad I did.

With a 4K external monitor, the screencaps I send about a million of a week were way too large in filesize, due to the extra pixel information they contained, but with this monitor, they're small enough to e-mail multiple of without getting one of those "You're about to send a 35MB e-mail. Are you sure you want to do this?" messages.

I also really like the telescoping nature of the display, which makes it super-simple to move the monitor lower and higher. Excellent engineering.

The built-in speakers sound about like you'd expect, but this is no issue to me, as I use my HP monitor alongside a professional audio setup in my studio.

All in all, I couldn't be happier with this HP monitor. For the price, it's a definite winner and one which I highly recommend.

ok first and foremost i like to say a big huge THANK GOD that my monitor came with zero dead pixels ! I am very very fortunate. Now with that out of the way let me tell you this monitor is simply amazing. The detail, color vibrancy, and anything and everything to do with eye candy is definitely amplified on a 1440p monitor. Let me again emphasize how rich the colors are in this monitor. Textures in games took life with the deepest hues of colors i have ever seen on a tv or monitor. Basically, my mouth dropped after playing a couple games ! The euphoria didn't end there bro. It went up a few notches when i saw how fluid the fps were with 144 hrz. Words can not describe how awesome 144hrz gaming is. It honestly is an "experience". Personally, my 2 cents is if your a hardcore visualphile and demand nothing but the very best in picture quality/presentation then an upgrade from 1080p is a definite yes. Some may point out 4k monitors as being the true king. But that is presently untrue. First, a lot of games are trying to catch up to hardware tech. Rendering quality must improve, newer game engines need to be developed as well as new real-time graphics techniques. Throwing more pixels at your setup is not going to make things look miraculously better. Gaming engines need to be OPTIMIZED for 4k. And today games are optimized for 1440p. Not 4k. Also there is a price factor to consider. A 4k monitor that has IPS, G-Sync, and 144 hz is gonna cost you north of $1,400. So basically you are paying twice the cost of a 1440p monitor for something that games are not presently optimized for. So with all this to factor in I feel an a 1440p monitor upgrade is a more rational choice for gamers that want the very best in visual presentation

Prior to this purchase I was gaming on a Sceptre 4k monitor with a 1070ti & i5 8400, playing at 4k/1440p depending on how demanding the game was. While sharpness in image quality is great with the resolution bump It doesn't compare to the smoothness of high refresh rate paired with g sync comparability. Gaming is a whole new experience to me now and I'm scratching my head wondering why I didn't go this route in the first place. Now starting off with this monitor, it comes with a display port so you can utilize 144hz. Before you do any gaming Calibrate the monitor settings first which i will explain below:

How to activate 144z:
1. Go to Nvidia control panel
2. Click change resolution which is under the display tab, and select 1920x 1080 under PC, then change refresh rate to 144( DO NOT SELECT 1920x1080 NATIVE)

How to Activate G Sync:
1. NVIDIA CONTROL PANEL
2. SET UP G-SYNC UNDER DISPLAY TAB
3.CHECK ENABLE G-SYNC AND ENABLE FOR FULL SCREEN MODE

Color settings I use(kudos to DemoKirby on reddit for this):
my settings. Picture:

Bright: 5

Contrast 45

Black Level: 3

Blue light: OFF

ACM: OFF

Super Sharpness: OFF

Color:

Gamma 2.2

Colour temp: User

R Gain:55

G Gain:52

B Gain:50

All bias to 49

srgb mode: OFF

6-axis hue: Default

6-axis Saturate: Adjust all to 55 except yellow saturate to 59 (Gives the colors a little extra pop to stand out, which I think tremendously helps against the washed out look of the monitor.)

I would really REALLY like to say that I am loving this product for the fantastic price I paid for it ($139 shipping included from Amazon) but it's on it's way back. If you're like me, and you check a popular magazine/website for consumer reviews, read the reviews on Amazon and elsewhere, I will give you the highlights.

1) The stand SUCKS. My desk (for better or worse) is a 6 foot folding table (hey, don't laugh, it gets the job done). But where the monitor sat on my desk was the most uneven spot. So, I started with folded up paper under one side. Then got a sturdy piece of cork flooring (just happened to have it lying around), and put the monitor on top of THAT -- still uneven. Again, finally got it level with more folded up pieces of paper under one side of the cork flooring. Even so, every time I typed, the screen wobbled back and forth, back and forth, back and forth. NO, I do not type with a sledge hammer. Just a regular ordinary every day person who types. Watching the screen wobble back and forth while you type SUCKS.

2) No height adjustment. After I put the darned thing on a piece of cork flooring, trying to even it out, the screen was no longer in the "sweet spot." Raise my head an inch or two, all of the text suddenly becomes crisp and clear. Leave my head in normal position, some of the text looked clear, some did not. Not being used to looking at jagged and rough text on a screen, it SUCKS.

3) Endless adjustments. I fiddled and fiddled and fiddled and fiddled and fiddled and fiddled and fiddled with the controls. After that, I made some adjustments. NO MATTER WHAT I DID, the colors were washed out and did not look right compared to what I had been using.

And what was THAT, you might ask?

An Apple flat panel 20 inch LCD display. I have:

a) Literally NEVER had to make ONE adjustment to it (OK, so I lied. I adjusted the brightness ONCE).
b) NO PROBLEMS with the quality of the stand. The screen does NOT rock back and forth when you type.
c) The colors are gorgeous and are not washed out.
d) The text looks crisp and sharp. NO MATTER WHERE I HOLD MY HEAD!!!!!!!!!!!

Folks, look, this shouldn't be rocket science. I thought I was getting a fantastic deal (slightly more real estate, very low energy costs, latest LED technology, a brand name that I'd actually HEARD of before, etc.), but am REALLY REALLY REALLY happy to be using my old screen again.

I’ve been using this monitor for a few days now and I can state that the quality of this monitor is very good. I’ve used this in several different configurations:

1) 3 monitor setup connected to a Nvidia GTX 1080 card. Output is at 4K 60Hz using the supplied mini HDMI cable. The monitor must be powered by the included power brick and usb-c cable.

2) Connected to my laptop as a 2nd display using the included usb-c cable via thunderbolt 3 port. Output is excellent with 4K 60Hz. In this configuration, if you plug in the monitor using the included power brick it power the monitor AND the laptop at the same time. Vice versa if you have your laptop power brick charging the laptop; the usb-c cable will drive the monitor and also power it so you only need the one cable

3) Connected to Nintendo switch. Output was 1080P using the usb-c cable and also powering the monitor with the included power brick and cable. Quality was very good, although you can tell it’s 1080P. Much better than the native Switch screen though.

Picture quality: Excellent. Very detailed, similar to high end laptops running 4K 15” IPS screens. Color accuracy seems to be OK. Not as good as color calibrated OLED laptop screens but definitely as advertised.

Brightness: Very good. I didn’t run my color calibrator on it yet, but brightness appears to be 300+ nits

Panel type: Since this is a touch screen, the surface is glossy using a glass or glass like coating on the screen. Very high quality, although I can’t state how durable it is. Definitely want to be careful moving it around. There’s definitely glare, but it doesn’t bother me and I prefer the sharpness of a gloss screen over matte (probably in the minority there)

Touch screen: Touch accuracy and response is very good, but note that the touch screen is only supported using the usb-c connection. HDMI will not work.

There is some light bleed, but it’s not bad and is similar to other IPS panels. To compare, this screen has less light bleed than my Lenovo X1 Extreme 4k IPS panel.

Overall a very high quality portable monitor on par with higher end IPS screens. It’s pricey, but worth it if you want quality with a glossy touch screen.

[[VIDEOID:df9a54dc267e1d63815325eae0264182]] I bought this second monitor because I wanted to expand my space even further than my ultrawide, but wanted something portable. I own the Asus Type C monitor that is basically the same except for one key feature - this one has 3 ports. The Asus only had one, and was convenient that it could be deliver power and the data through the Type C cable. Except now I have a desktop, and it uses display ports, which do not deliver power. This Auzai monitor lets me do both if I needed to. The added flexibility of an HDMI port and two type Cs (one for power only) means I can now use this with my desktop and load my peripheral work on to the side.

As you can see I have it setup as a vertical monitor, so that I can put any videos or chat boxes on the side, while I use the ultrawide for actual work I'm editing. Super convenient.

In terms of build quality this thing is built really well, comparable to the Asus, and it's got a nice sleek finish that feels premium. The cover is integrated in though, so you can't take it off, which is the one thing I dislike. It's also not as "smart" as Asus, but since I'm not moving around much these days, it's more than sufficient.

Display is sharp and crisp, colors are vibrant, but default contrast is a bit heavy handed, making things seem a little darker and deeper than usual. Nothing a few quick tweaks couldn't solve. 15.6" may seem small, but is a good size secondary, and if you're lugging this around in a backpack, it's the perfect size to go with a 13.3 macbook.

I also have to mention, the packaging was much nicer than I anticipated. Separate boxes (though probably not environmentally friendly) for each cable and accessory, came padded in foam, and a nice touch with the free screen protector.

I'd like to preface my review with saying: This is not a bad monitor. If you're not that picky, this isn't a bad choice.
My biggest issues with the monitor are:
1. The curve. I remember hearing how cool and useful curved monitors were for some reason, and I bought one. Realistically, I don't notice a difference. It seems like a regular monitor to me, and even when I actively look to notice the curve, it doesn't feel any more "ergonomic" than my other monitor, which is a regular straight monitor.
2. The picture quality. Comparing this to my older ACER 23in monitor, the picture quality is pretty weak. The colors aren't very sharp and look almost faded in some ways. If you're doing any color-sensitive work (pretty much anything artsy, as art made on this monitor will look slightly different if viewed on other monitors) I heavily recommend you look elsewhere.

All in all, aside from those gripes, the monitor has lasted me about a year thus far with no issues at all, and seeing as I do not do any color sensitive work, it does work well as a primary monitor (with the smaller as a secondary monitor).
You can't really go wrong for the money. This is also one of the few monitors with a headphone output in this price range.

So, for starters - I had an older smaller version of this same samsung monitor and its been a trooper. The old one has been going strong for 5 years nearly at this point. I've transitioned it into a regular TV and I wanted to get a larger newer replacement. I Chose this beast. I chose to get a refurbished model to save a few dollars and it turned out to be a massive mistake. This was Samsung factory refurbished so I was led to believe the workmanship was going to be on par with the new item. Well 3 weeks into owning it the monitor began turning itself off and on. The longer the monitor was on the more frequently this would occur until finally it would just turn off and not come back on. I would have to unplug the monitor for it to reset. And the same issue would occur. The seller "dealstop" had a 90 day warranty. I shipped it out there on my dime to have it fixed. It came back and I was quite hopeful that I would not have the same issue. another few weeks went by and I noticed the exact same problem as before. I not sure if "dealstop" even replaced my monitor or what they did but they certainly did not fix the issue. When I contacted them again I was perhaps a week out of the 90 day period. When I asked for them to service the device I got a flat no, its out of warranty. Needless to say I would not recommend buying from them. So I contacted samsung directly. They seemed to be aware of this issue happening on other models and arranged for a repair... but it was out of warranty on their end as well? so they offered to fix it for around 200 dollars. I paid 259 for the unit. So - after escalating the issue with samsung, they finally agreed to cover the cost of the repair and to and from shipping.  I just got it back and I've been on it for most of the day without incident. After researching it more for myself I discovered that the logic board inside this is known to be spotty... lots of defective boards apparently. But if you get a "good" board these reportedly tend to last quite a while. The monitor itself earns 4-4 1/2 stars from me. Sharp colors 2 HDMI inputs decent speakers for what it is I suppose and a fully functional TV tuner. I'm not really a fan of the lack of physical buttons - the touch sensitive ones aren't always that sensitive and the area you need to press isn't super clear so you end up kind of pressing around until it does what you want. Usually I just end up using the remote. It appears to support mounting brackets which is a plus for future office plans but remains untested in my office. In short, buy new make sure you register the product and don't buy anything from dealstop. Thanks samsung for standing behind your product and taking care of this. I will edit if I have the issue pop up on me again.
