Works flawlessly with my 16 inch Macbook Pro 2019
I am used to a monitor-based setup; this is a new realm for me - and must say that I am pleasantly surprised with this product.
I am very impressed with the convenient connectivity options. It comes with a lot of cables - every possible cable required for all connectivity options. The USB-C cables are thunderbolt 3 compatible. I have tried this with three different setups.
1. Thinkpad X1 Carbon 5th gen with HDMI: Connect HDMI for display and sound; Connect micro-usb to USB3 for touch screen
2. Macbook Pro 2019 with USB-C: This is where I get into convenience of the PD charging. I connect the laptop charger to the display; then I connect the remaining USB-C port of the display to my laptop; there is a single cable running into my laptop thus leaving all other ports available
3. Thinkpad X1 Carbon 5th gen with USB-C: This Thinkpad comes with Thunderbolt 3 USB-C ports; It supports the same setup as option two above.
The only catch with PD charging is that the charger that comes with the product is of lower wattage than required and the display fails to start in options 2 and 3 (USB-C options). When I replaced it with my laptop charger(an HP USB-C charger with multiple Volt-Ampere ratings upto 65W), it worked.

I am equally impressed with its top of the line display quality. Although I did not measure the display specifications using any tools, it looks so spectacular that I trust the display's specs.
I find the touchscreen to be convenient on Windows, but on Mac it does not support scrolling.

I had purchased a 32" LG monitor for my work laptop earlier this year when the CV19 made working from home a necessity and that one was the same price as this ViewSonic and the ViewSonic was almost $300.  Wait 6 months and I was able to find this 32" ViewSonic monitor for the same price as the LG.  I have always been a ViewSonic fan and this monitor does not disappoint in quality and I love the streamlined stand vs. the flimsy LG plastic stand.  The ViewSonic picture is great.  ONLY downside was the LG came with Plug & Play software for split screen options. The ViewSonic does have similar software, but I went to the corporate site to find and found the driver for installation as my Windows Pro operating system only assigned a generic driver.  I called Amazon help line as I need the side by side capability of a 32" monitor and not the space of two separate monitors.  The Amazon help line directed me to the SOFTWARE (VIEWSONIC_SPLIT.zip) download page where it is the last of about 6 offerings.  After getting that installed the software I now have the multiple screen options that I require.  I have this monitor on my personal laptop and the quality of the monitor and the price are far superior to my LG BUT the ViewSonic Split Screen software is Version 1.0 and will do what the LG split screen auto-install software does, but not nearly as responsive and harder to make fit the screens as every new window or program must be resized where the LG software allows you to preselect programs (Excel, Word, etc.) to automatically fit the screen size on does not have to be resized each time.  I still give the monitor 5 stars as the quality of the monitor and stand are far superior to other flat screen 32" monitors such as LG's available.  They just need a better screen split software package.  Also, nothing I found in the literature that came with mine that mentions this software.

This review is for the Portable Monitor for Laptop 15.6", Ultra Slim Travel Monitor with Built-in Speakers & Case Metal Frame, USB-C Second Monitor, Gaming Monitor for MacBook/Phone, Xbox, Switch PS4 PS5, 1080P HDR IPS Screen.

Arrival: The monitor came in a very attractive box and was well-protected. It included two interface cables and a power cable, a power supply, and a manual.

Dimensions:
The monitor measured a little over 15-1/2 inches. The monitor itself measured 14 x 9 x 1/2 inches, and all other dimensions and controls reflected the published information.

Resolution:
The monitor was tested on a Raspberry Pi 400, NVIDIA Jetson Nano, and a desktop computer running Windows 11 with a NVIDIA GeForce GT 610 video adapter. All three computers identified the monitor with a resolution adjusted to 1920*1080. All displays were full-screen. No adapters were available to test with iPhones to the type C port of the monitor.

Monitor Adjustments Menu:
The manual and the monitor agreed with the features available to adjust the monitor. All tests were good.

Monitor Audio:
The audio was weak for all three computers. An amplified speaker could be attached to the monitor's audio output to boost the signal or switch from the monitor's HDMI audio to the computer's audio outputs.

Monitor Performance:
The monitor's anti-glare coating performed as intended with a light source directed to it. The monitor was flicker-free with video and static images, which could be viewed from any angle. The quality of the video, such as saturation, is typical for portable monitors but below standard for a tabletop or laptop—generally, a good-performing monitor. As a work monitor, it is excellent. As an entertainment monitor, above average for a portable but below for a tabletop laptop monitor. The audio, as mentioned, is also an issue.

Monitor's Case:
The monitor's PU leather cover provides adequate protection when closed and is removable. It acts as the holdup leg to prop the monitor up when in place. The setup is a bit unstable, and the monitor would often collapse during the setup.

Monitor Power Supply:
The monitor was powered by a 12VDC to 120VAC/5VDC power inverter through the inverter's USB-5VDC and 120VAC sources and did not display any irregularities.

Approval Markings:
The monitor has CE UKCA, FCC approval markings as a monitor/noise/5VDC device. The power supply has no markings at all. It is suggested that a CSA/UL-approved power supply replace the original unit.

Conclusion:
This is an excellent portable monitor for work. It is lightweight, has a large viewing area, and is slim enough to piggyback in the laptop's backpack. For entertainment, it is acceptable; however, another method is needed to boost the sound. Using Windows 11, the audio was directed to the computer's sound system from the HDMI interface.

I compared this side by side with another LG LED monitor found here http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B00TR05K6S?psc=1&redirect=true&ref_=oh_aui_detailpage_o00_s00.

Pro's:
1) Super thin bezel. It is only a few millimeters thick. When the monitor is turned on the picture does not go all the way to the edge, there is a 1/8 - 1/4" black band on all sides. I wish it would go fully to the edge but it seems by design. I really like this bezel better than previous monitors.
2) The monitor controls are so much better than the touch screen controls LG experimented with on my last monitor. The control is a physical "joystick" in the center of the monitor @ the bottom. It even allows you to turn off the LED power on indicator in the settings. I love that setting, so the light does not stare you in the face while you use it. It will however, blink when the computer is turned off until you turn off the monitor.

Cons:
1) The thin bezel unfortunately allows the monitor to "rattle" when you pick it up & handle it. The monitor did not seem loose, but this bothered me as to the long term secureness of the screen in the case.
2) These are my first LED & IPS monitors. I did not have my old high end TFT panel to compare with (it died). I first had this monitor & my 47" LG LED tv in the clone setting. When watching a live stream, I noticed that face & hair colors were significantly different. The tv had a much better natural flesh tone. I then compared this monitor with the monitor @ the top side by side. Please note: both monitors are LED IPS screens by LG. They both have the same monitor controls (and I assume the same engine). I made sure both settings were exactly the same. I then compared high & low resolution photos of people in the clone monitor setting. There was an obvious difference. This monitor had a lackluster color representation. To me the colors on this panel is off by several shades. It was very noticeable between the two monitors. The other monitor had the richer flesh & hair tones that my TV has.
3) Power cord is way too short. It is maybe 60" long. I have to have the power strip right under the monitor on my desk so the cable will reach. This was a poor decision on LG's part. It gets worse in item 4).
4) The plug is is not the traditional straight down 3 prong that fits well with other plugs in a power strip. It is a rectangular plug. The plug is too wide to sit side by side with other plugs. The decision by LG to change the plug type is an ignorant short sighted decision. I had to shut all my equipment off & rearrange everything in my power strip. I cannot use two of my plugs now due to this.
5) Monitor stand:
a) Stand is flimsy
b) There is no forward tilt (only backward tilt).
c) There is no swivel

Due to the color issues, I returned this in favor of the other monitor.

I love this monitor, but I'm sad to have to give it such a low rating. A monitor like this that costs over $1,000 shouldn't be having well-documented issues with a defective Display Port connection in the monitor itself. In order to over-clock the refresh rate to 100 Hz, and also to utilize SLI, you must use the Display Port. These features are not available if using the HDMI connection. I like too many owners of this monitor start having issues with flickering across the screen, and then the screen going blank and seeing "DP no connection" on the screen. A few times I'd restart the PC and the image would work for a few minutes, but then happen again.

I did all the recommended trouble-shooting including using a different/new Display Port cable, updating video drivers, downgrading to video drivers that were known to work without issue, swapping video cards, trying different refresh rates, etc. Nothing solved the issue and the only way I can use the monitor is with the HDMI connection. I have a second PC that was connected to the monitor via HDMI while my main PC was connected via Display Port, and when the Display Port would malfunction, I'd switch the setting in the monitor to HDMI and the secondary PC was working fine.

One time while still connected via Display Port the screen flickered, went black, and then came back showing as in the attached photo. I would go into the settings in both the monitor and Windows and none of the settings could be adjusted.

This monitor comes with a three year warranty, and I'm now three years and eight months past, so there's nothing I can do. I have a $1,000 monitor that can't be used for the purpose it was originally purchased for - NVIDIA SLI and overclock refresh rate of 100 Hz. Researching this monitor and this issue will give you many examples of others having the same issue among others, all without solution and with less than stellar options from ACER tech/customer support. I'd love to recommend this monitor, but for a $1,000+ monitor with known defective issues, ACER should extend the warranty or at least acknowledge the issue(s) and offer to fix them for a reasonable cost.

Caveat emptor. Do your research, a lot of research, before you spend over a $1,000 on a monitor.

Minor cons for gaming:
1) The 'Fast' response rate mode which is supposed to be 5ms appears to be a gimmick. It may actually be 5ms, but I don't think the panel can handle it and it creates 'motion bleed' where as something moves (i.e. in-game), you see a shadow where it was previously. This doesn't occur when set to normal (8ms). Keep in mind, that 8ms is very good, just not great nor outstanding. I have lived with 8ms forever now. I would love a better response rate, but it's not taking away from the overall experience and value.
2) Not all games can do it! Some games just don't compute 21:9 aspect ratio. That said, there are hacks for most games to make them work.
3) Wimpy graphics card? HAAAA!!!! Time to upgrade and possibly have dual GPUs. 3440x1440 is 2.4 times the number of pixels of 1080p. Which basically means, that whatever frame-rate you are getting right now in your favorite game will be cut by more than half with this monitor at native. You will find quite often that the optimizers like "AMD Gaming Evolved" or "GeForce Experience" will suggest a lower resolution than native! Like 2560x1080. Still looks very good at that resolution. So there are compromises, but don't expect to get game crazy with a low end card.

For now, I'm loving it. Games look great.. Shoot, everything looks great! For the price. You can't beat it.

I had to knock off one star because it's not very handy when you are attaching it to two computers. I have a desktop PC hooked up to the DVI port, and a macbook pro that I hook up via thunderbolt. What I discovered is that, when the DVI port is hooked up and the desktop PC is powered on (even if 'sleeping'), the monitor will NOT auto-switch to the thunderbolt connection when I plug in the macbook.

To get the monitor to automatically switch from DVI to thunderbolt, the PC has to be completely powered off. Monitor 'power save' mode is not enough. If I don't want to power off the PC, I can still switch via the menu, but it is an annoyance to have to go through several menu clicks (via the buttons on the bottom lip of the monitor) to switch over.

When I unplug the macbook while the PC is turned on, it auto-switches to the DVI and I have to repeat the whole process again next time (or shut off the desktop PC.)

I looked through the menu, but couldn't find a setting to change this behavior. I am actually thinking about getting a mechanical KVL switch just to turn OFF the DVI so I don't have to fuss with the menu buttons or power off my PC just to use the screen with my mac laptop.

I love this display! I am a Linux user, and it just worked right out of the box, no special settings required. In fact, I kept my current monitor hooked up, and it recognized both and set up dual monitor configuration.
This panel is *perfect*. No defects. No dead pixels or other quality problems. Very effective packaging with double boxes and lots of foam. I hooked it up using HDMI, and it immediately synced at 60Hz without my having to adjust a thing.

Since this monitor is not 4K, there were no problems scaling. Lots of screen real estate, so I bumped up the font size just a bit as I'm older, and my eyes are not as sharp as they once were. I set my settings to force fonts to 120 DPI, and it worked extremely well, and made fonts much clearer and easier to read. If you do a lot of reading as I do, it's a very good monitor for that, as it has special settings for color and brightness so that it's easy on the eyes, and it have five presets for adjusting blue light down so that it doesn't interfere with sleep. The on screen display to configure the monitor is easy to use.

It comes with cables to connect HDMI, Displayport or DVI, VGA, audio and for USB passthrough.
I'm a programmer, and I play strategy games and watch videos.
I can turn the monitor sideways, change the orientation to portrait, and read a *ton* of text, whether it's a book or code.
Movies with a lot of dark or poorly lit scenes are amazing and show a lot more detail that my old monitor, a Samsung 22BW. Contrast is greater than on any monitor I've seen. It has a "Darkroom" mode that I didn't know about, that dims the screen perfectly for night use, and it's very easy to select in the OSD.

Photo editing is a dream on this thing. The large screen area means that I can load large photos and I don't have to scroll or adjust. Colors are truly amazing. I'm a photographer, and I'm thrilled with the color accuracy. Photos that have fog actually *look* like fog instead of just washed out.

The design is clean and simple and effective. The stand holds the screen firmly but moving the screen takes only the lightest touch, and it's very configurable. I like being able to lift it up so that I'm not hunched over, I can sit up straight.
The viewing angle is quite good, and my family can all watch a movie together from different angles without color shift.

I have ordered a faster graphics card to drive this unit, as I have a Geforce GT720, and while it works better than I expected, I have noticed that there is some stuttering on some games and on very detailed movies with action scenes. It's subtle, but it's there. The new Geforce GTX 970 should fix that. I will update this review and report on the differences. The new card will have Displayport, and I want to drive this monitor at 75Hz, as it requires Displayport 1.2 (the monitor comes with version 1.1 as default) and a high end graphics card. The manual has a list of supported cards.

One slight criticism is that this monitor did not come with a manual, but I did find that online on ASUS's website support link.
I didn't take any points off because manuals are pretty easy to obtain online, but this would be a problem if the display didn't function properly, and while this was not a problem for me, as the setup was a breeze, if things went wrong for some reason, it would be difficult. Not much of an issue for those upgrading from an older monitor, which is going to be the case for many. Also, once manuals are printed and shipped, inevitably something requires a change to the manual, and most manuals are now found online.

First, don't swallow the HP spec that says this display has an 8-bit front end. It's a 6-bit LG-made e-IPS panel, the same panel used in the hallowed Dell U2211 and several other competitors, including one I also own: the newer LG IPS226v. The monitor stand is a heavyweight (weighs more than the monitor) and works well, once you spend some time fiddling with it. I still haven't figured out how to unmount the stand, but neither have I gone thru all the documentation (Documentation? This owner reads documentation? What's documentation?). The box is huge for a 21.5", more like a box for a deluxe microwave, but half the space is for the stand which itself is ready-assembled; just slip the monitor over the grooves in back, and voila. It's a headache having all the rear connections facing down, but it does make for a very thin monitor. The LG panel inside is 6-bit, but its a proven performer. My gray ramps show so little dithering or banding that staring at them occasionally during hours of use still reveals no defects or artifacts. Screen uniformity is even; my desktop for video work is an RGB32 dark gray, nearly black. I don't see any edge bleed, and as I say my desktop color is really dark, which helps when color-correcting video. It's a sharp image, but was a little noisy at first with my analog graphics card: I ran the VGA adjustment utility that comes with the CD, and any complaints I might have had simply vanished 5 seconds later. Neat. Out of the box, color was OK, brightness too high. After a few calibration runs with an Xrite/Display 2, the image meets D65 and sRGB standards to a T. My colorimeter tells me the darks below IRE 20 display a slight falloff in Red, something like a piddling 2% down, and blue is about 1% high down there. The rest of the RGB grayscale is a straight line. I wanted a gamma of 1.8, and that's what I got after calibration. I also tried HP's Display Assistant; not the greatest calibration software, mainly because doing that sort of thing by eye is no match for a colorimeter. But it's better than nothing, and it's more precise  than other such features I've seen. Overall the image looks sharp and almost film-like, not as grainy as my LG IPS226v. A 20-step gray ramp displays with only a barely (and I mean barely) hint of warmth in the panels, but all of them uniform, and all of them distinct from darkest to brightest. The color out of the box was OK but too cyan, with very slightly yellowed brights (you had to work to see it). I lowered brightness to 66, and lowered Green and Blue by about 15 to 20%, not that much of an adjustment from the defaults. Adjusting RGB precisely was tricky, though, because the RGB controls are set at 255 max by default, which means you can lower the colors but you can't raise them. Odd. But lowering Blue and Green worked OK. The panel is on its first day now; I expect to recalibrate after a week of burn-in. The CCFL backlight is a blessing; the LG I compare it to has great color, not quite as film-like and with visibly less low-level detail than the HP. The LG's edgelights had poor uniformity and visible clouding in two corners, the sort of problems that plague all LED's. This HP has better clarity and no screen problems that I see yet. After editing a few old videos I can see some minor but visible color errors in the darks that were obscured on the LG. The LG could get a more accurate calibration, but I'm talking about a 1% difference that can be measured but not seen by eye. From the HP I get an sRGB CIE chart that looks as close to ideal as any I've seen in this price range. Both monitors, having the same LG-made panel, have slight haloing issues that this panel is (in)famous for, but the backlight difference makes it far less obvious on the HP, and in actual use the problem effectively doesn't exist. A 100 white (RGB255) full screen white panel shows a subtle cyan clouding at each side,, so vague it seems to disaappear in actual use with graphics and spreadsheets; brightness was very even, as I measured less than 10% variation, and thankfully there was no bright hot-spot in the middle. This HP is for my 2nd video computer. With this level of performance in a non-pro monitor, I'll be using that 2nd PC more often. I'm not a big fan of HP's displays for video work, but this one looks like a keeper. I hear HP plans to revise this model by replacing the backlight with LED. What a shame. I'm glad I picked this up before yet another manufacturer ruins a good panel with yet another useless fad. Caution: I use it to color-correct video, but not for extended movie viewing: at full-screen there's frequent motion stutter. Somewhat surprising, since other monitors using this same LG panel don't have that problem.

First off I want to say if you are here because you thought the BENQ RL series wasn't as great (picture wise) as you thought it was then this Asus Vs monitor would be a great fit for you if you are looking for the best of both worlds (response and picture quality). I rated the ASUS vs series 4 stars and for my reasons is because the blacks were not that great. Below is my journey that I'm sharing with you to chose what I believe is the best console gaming monitor.

ASUS MX279H - Best IPS monitor for console gaming. Went from gaming on a huge 50+inch tv to a 27" monitor for what I've heard would be a better and more fluid gaming experience. I have to say I'm never going back to a large screen tv again. Just stick to big screen tvs for movie and tv viewing and monitors for gaming. I use Xbox one to play on this monitor. Now, the "best" is if you want a monitor with the best of both worlds for response time or low input lag, AND picture quality. This monitor has the best and deepest blacks I've ever seen.

Console gamers - to get this out of the way, do not buy any monitor thats any more than 75 hz or a 4k. 4k "upscaling" it is a misconception. Consoles will only push 1080 p and 60 hz. This monitor and any monitors I've tested out do just that except for the 4k.

Bought a Benq RL series monitor and learned its a TN panel which gives lowest response time but low color reproduction. Which basically I didn't think was the greatest picture quality. Even after calibrating the settings. At viewing angles - especially for console gamers who sometimes want to sit at a slouch position (relaxed) can cut off the image quality. You have to sit in front and upright to get the right viewing you want. As for the speakers - it just merely provides sound. Nothing more. I don't care for this because I game with my Astro headsets. I went out and bought this monitor to begin with assuming it was the BEST gaming monitor out there. It is heavily marketed and talked about in the gaming community. The "best" is misleading. It is a great monitor to its own and recommended for MLG gamers. Theres a reason for that.

Bought a Samsung UHD 4k UE590 monitor and learned I didn't need it since 4k is still young. With outdated display connectors, punishing hardware requirements, and lack of os and app support besides netflix and prime that can stream 4k. && the picture sometimes appear grainy while the 4k is trying to push the 1080p and 60hz. Whatever you do... YOU DO NOT NEED A 4k (unless you want to drop 2000+ on a rig and proper video card). Consoles only push out 1080p 60 hz nothing more! so stick to that. Also theres already talks of 5k or 8k tvs coming out!?

Bought a Asus Vs series monitor (IPS) and compared it side by side with the Asus MX that I chose to keep. The Asus Vs is a great monitor to its own with lower response time but the color reproduction is so VIBRANT with okay blacks. This makes the picture quality appear GREAT but it also makes it appear flat. Thats only because I had the Asus MX side by side with the Asus Vs to tell the difference.

When it comes to response time you are talking 1ms (benq), 2 ms (asus vs), and 5 ms (asus mx). THE BRAIN AND EYE CANNOT... CANNOT... tell the difference between milliseconds. Thats a science fact. On paper, yes. Trust me, I went back and forth switching my HDMI to each of these monitors and it all feels the same. So if you are a competitive gamer aiming to be a MLG then maybe, just maybe the 1ms monitor would matter to you... or maybe not (input sarcasm here).

Honestly though, it is input lag you want to be worried about than response time. The Asus MX monitor actually puts out the lowest input lag out of all the other monitors I've tested out.

Overall I am VERY pleased with this monitor (Asus MX) and the choice I've made. It definitely made my moneys worth. The Asus MX monitor as I said earlier offers the deepest blacks I've ever seen. It makes the picture quality pop so wonderful and definitely doesn't make the picture appear flat like the Asus Vs monitor. The blacks offer texture to the colors, picture, and gaming experience. BE SURE YOUR XBOX ONE SETTINGS IS SET TO PC RGB!!!

Oh, front the extra cash if you can for a 27", you'll thank me later.

Here's the link for the monitor I chose: [...]

I hope my journey and review helps you find a great monitor for your gaming pleasure or even use it for PC.

The LG 49WQ95C is an excellent monitor for gaming and work. However, it’s on expensive side for similar monitors, but with more drawbacks. So if you really want a single ultrawide display for professional color-critical work and gaming then look no further as this monitor will blow you away with the sheer amount of open windows you really can put on the screen.

The stand of the monitor is robust and offers a good range of ergonomics, including up to 110mm height adjustment, -5°/20° tilt, +/- 15° swivel and 100x100mm VESA mount compatibility. It is also simple easy to put together with no tools required.

The screen has a nice curvature and a light matte anti-glare coating that prevents reflections without making the image too grainy.

Connectivity options include two HDMI 2.1 ports, DisplayPort 1.4 with DSC, a USB-C port with DP 1.4 Alt Mode and 90W Power Delivery, a dual-USB 3.0 hub (1 upstream + 2 downstream), a headphone jack and dual 10W integrated speakers with decent audio quality. All four display inputs support 5120×1440 144Hz with dithered 10-bit color depth. There’s a built-in sensor that can automatically adjust the screen brightness according to ambient lighting.

At the rear of the monitor, there’s a directional joystick for quick and easy navigation through the OSD (On-Screen Display) menu. As an alternative, you can use the On-Screen Control desktop application by LG to make display adjustments using your keyboard and mouse. Noteworthy gaming features include Black Stabilizer (improves visibility in dark scenes), various picture presets and crosshair overlays. The LG 49WQ95C also supports Picture in Picture, Picture by Picture and has an integrated KVM functionality, allowing you to connect and display two PC sources side by side and control them via one set of keyboard/mouse.

Besides the standard image adjustment tools (brightness, contrast, color temperature, aspect ratio, etc.), the monitor offers several advanced settings, including four gamma presets, sharpness, 6-axis hue/saturation and color temperature fine-tuning in 500K increments.

The IPS panel also provides you with 178° wide viewing angles, which is one of the biggest differences when compared to the more popular VA ultrawides, such as the Odyssey G9. Colors, brightness, contrast and gamma all remain consistent across the entire screen, regardless of the angle you’re looking at it.
The LG 49WQ95C monitor has a subtle 3800R screen curvature, which helps with the overall viewing experience without distorting important details of your work. The 5120×1440 screen resolution on a 49″ sized screen provides you with a pixel density of 108.54 PPI (pixels per inch). As a result, you get plenty of screen real estate with sharp details and text, and no scaling necessary.

Basically, you can think of the LG 49WQ95C as two 27″ 2560×1440 monitors side by side, just without the bezels in between them. While there are many games that support the 32:9 aspect ratio, a lot of content is limited to 16:9 or 21:9 formats, which will have black bars at the sides of the screen. So, make sure your favorite games support 32:9 ultrawide resolutions in case you plan on doing a lot of gaming on this screen!

Next, the LG 49WQ95C has a strong peak brightness of 400-nits, so it will be able to get bright enough even in well-lit rooms. The contrast ratio amounts to 1,000:1, as expected from an IPS display. So, you won’t get as deep blacks as that of VA or OLED displays, but those panels have other drawbacks and unless you have the displays side by side you won't be able to tell the difference.

But overall This is an excellent and top of the line monitor that is both high quality in color representation and professional grade as well as a gaming monitor to blow off some of that steam after a hard days work on a project.

THE MARRIAGE IPS-LE+3D PASSIVE HAS BEEN A RIGHT CHOICE.

I fully disagree with the usual sentence : « passive 3D is not Full HD because only 540 lines are displayed ». This statement could lead to wrong interpretations :
- it is true that eyes only see 540 horizontal lines, BUT THEY ARE DIFFERENT FOR EACH EYE; finally all 1080 lines are displayed, so we have a kind of Interlaced HD video.
- the active 3D video seems better because each eye sees 1080 lines BUT NOT AT THE SAME TIME and the shutter glasses are expansive, heavy and require a battery.

Finally I strongly recommend this monitor.

I purchased the 27" variant on April 26, 2017. I am not a verified purchaser because it was purchased on my old Amazon account which hasn't made a purchase in the last year so I can't post reviews.

Pros:
-Fair pricing

Cons:
-Multiple dead pixels
-Scan line appear at the bottom, taking up an inch or two of screen for about ~30 seconds upon starting the monitor if it hasn't been on for a few hours
-Moderate graphical load causes the black pixels on the screen to turn blue and the whites gain a yellow hue
-Monitor interface and buttons annoying to use

All these issues started occurring only after two years, no dead pixels on arrival. It is absolutely the monitor and not my computer that could be causing this, every part from the DVI cable, motherboard and graphics card has since been upgraded or replaced.

I ordered 2 of these monitors for my gaming rig. I went with 32 (31.5 actual) because of the price was less than many 27" 1440p monitors and the fact that I have had good luck with ASUS monitors in the past. Add to this the fact that they actually weigh less than the existing 27" 1080p monitors they were replacing (a plus for vesa mounting on the arms I have) and it was a no brainer.

My first impressions out of the box was that the plastic felt a bit cheap, then I remembered they are 32 inch monitors and only weigh 13 pounds and some change. This plastic is also not the crazy shiny plastic I am used to on the Asus monitors. This ended up being a plus because I did not have to spend all day combating my OCD to get fingerprints off of them. So, I mounted them up on their mounting arms and noticed a nice surprise while doing so. The bezels are just 1/4 of an inch around the monitor.

All was good until just after plugging them in and firing up my rig. At that point I thought I had made a huge mistake. The colors were all over the place, the monitors lacked focus visibly and they ghosted horribly when moving things around on the desktop. After I calmed down from my initial reaction, I spent a little time researching some things and came to the conclusion that I needed to reload my video drivers. So, I grabbed the latest drivers for my Nvidia 980 card and loaded them from scratch. Wow, what a world of difference. Other than the white being washed out a bit, I saw all the performance I have been used to with Asus monitors. This and I have still yet to spend the time to tune them to get the best possible picture.

I took one star off because it appears that these either need a special driver or they need an extension to the standard plug-and-play video driver to enable the extra features they boast. I also expect this driver update will sharpen out the whites as I had a similar experience with my first Asus monitors many many years ago. So, until that driver arrives to enable the special features and the whites become clearer, I will hold onto that one star.

That said, I am perfectly happy with these monitors and they perform just fine for my needs whether browsing the web, shooting bad guys in games, or watching a Blu-ray movie.

The UPerfect display comes with a few accessories to get you started and I'll be listing everything that came in the box.
1.) The Stand
2.) Power Cable
3.) Power Adapter
4.) Mini-HDMI to Full HDMI cable
5.) User Manual

Before you buy something like this, I would suggest you check out a KVM (keyboard, video, mouse) switch as that may be more applicable. Amazon has quite a few, some as inexpensive at this video only switch.

I have 4 systems and 3 monitors (somebody up there hates me). The other 2 monitors use a DVI or HDMI switch and I have a USB switch and audio switch. There are a couple reason I went with individual switches. The main one I haven't found a KVM + audio switch that handles multiple monitors and the other is an occasional need to mix and match. For example start a long job on one computer and leave it on a secondary monitor while I do something on another computer. Or play music from one computer while I hop around the others.

If you have a choice between VGA (analog) and DVI or HDMI (digital) go digital the image is much much cleaner.

One more argument for individual switches may be out of date but the last few KVM switches I owned (over 10 years ago) had an annoying ringing in the VGA signal. The image quality through a mechanical switch was much better than through those things. It was long enough ago I won't mention the brand.

So if I haven't talked you out of this yet, the only other thing to be aware of is that the cables to the switch box are different than standard monitor cable. The computer input cables are male-male and the monitor cable is male-female.

If you have decided on a mechanical switch I can recommend this one. Image quality through the switch is indistinguishable from direct to the monitor.

I bought this monitor for my home workstation which I share on occasion with my wife. We both have a 13" 2015 mac book pro and I wanted a large quality high res display that we both could use when needed to work on projects (mostly obscenely large spreadsheets or design work) that require a larger screen at eye level. My goal was to have a work station that we could both use seamlessly (the least cables going to our laptops the better). As a note, I live in a small apartment and hate clutter. Since the work station sits in my living room, design was as much a factor for me as ease of use, construction and image quality.

Overall: Good value if you are looking for a no-frills 27" display with good image quality and nothing else. For superior image quality and features look for higher-priced options.

I own both PCs and Macs. So I'm not necessarily an apple fanatic. However, if you are buying this display to use with a mac book pro as I did, you may want to stick to the Thunderbolt (unless you work on any particular task that requires a 4k resolution). IMO the seamless integration of the Thunderbolt (combining power, video, audio and peripherals with a single cable) and built in speakers make up for the Thunderbolt's lower resolution (which works just fine for most tasks). I ended up having to also buy a Dell 4k docking station ($160) and external speakers ($90), which takes away from the value, which is the main benefit of this display.

Pros:
-Nice 4k resolution.
-Accurately color calibrated.
-Easy set up with included Display Port or HDMI cable. Plug and Play with my MBP. Both options rendered crisp 4k image quality.
-I enjoy the ability to rotate adjust the height, tilt and rotation of the monitor.
-Solid construction.

On the fence:
-USB and audio port in the back are nice to have for easily connecting peripherals. However, using these USB ports requires you to to connect an additional cable to your laptop in addition to the DP cable (which I'm trying to minimize). Also, I noticed an annoying lag on my wireless mouse (which connects via a small USB thingy) when connected to the monitor ports. So I ended up plugging the mouse back directly to the laptop USB ports.
-The design isn't bad, but it isn't good either. For the frame and base they used a "brushed aluminum" plastic veneer that looks good from afar but looks and feels plasticky once you are closer to the desk. I wish they would have gone with the real thing. Also, the back of the display is just a big black light-sucking plastic rectangle. So it will not look nice if you are going to have the back of the display "displayed" (as opposed to placed against a wall).

Cons
-The display doesn't stay leveled on a horizontal position. Instead it tends to tilt slightly to the side as if it was a loose and heavier on right side. This may not be a big deal for some people, but it drives me crazy looking at slightly tilted screen (and therefore slightly tilted spreadsheets and web browsers) for hours. It may be only a few degrees, but the large size of the display accentuates this problem. I couldn't find a way to tighten the rotating parts so I solved it by placing a piece of folded paper underneath the right side of the base. This is too bad as the base/pedestal was very sturdy and stable. It is not so stable after I placed the paper (but at least it is now leveled)
-Black levels could be darker (I know it is an issue with the technology rather than the specific monitor, but still...)
-No speakers. I really don't need loud, premium speakers. But a small set of speakers for me to listen to computer sounds or the occasional Youtube video would have been nice. I have to keep an external set of speakers cluttering my desktop.
While it is a fraction of the price of other monitors it is actually a great quality one.

... coming from a 19" DVI monitor from my last build around 2013. I decided that since I usually go in for more visual games (Mirror's Edge Catalyst, Skyrim, Dear Esther, etc) and not so many competitive or FPS games, I'd go all the way in for a 27 inch. I have to say, when I came home to the box, I was a bit shocked. Opening the box, I was more than a bit shocked. It took me a day or two to get used to the resolution and just the size, but I don't think I'll be able to go back. I've not yet had a chance to test the 144hz as most of my current build is still on the way, but I'm ready to be similarly amazed.

Build Quality: Heavy, sturdy, feels like it will hold together well

Size: More than enough. Make sure you have adequate front-back desk space. The legs of the stand are about 10.5 inches (26.5 cm) front to back, and the monitor itself sits about 4 inches back from the front. I'd recommend having at least a 26 inch depth desk. Mine is only 19.5 and I'd love another 6 inches of space; everything fits on the desk fine, but the monitor is a bit too close for my liking.

Setup: Three screws. Two on the backplate, one on the base of the stand. Cables as needed (comes with one display port, one HDMI, and 1 USB for setup). I will say that the power cable supplied is quite short. I don't want to pull it to measure, but it's somewhere around 30 inches or less. Just means you have to be careful about surge protector placement.

Ease of Use: Programmable directional input has been fantastic. Easy adjust color mode, brightness, profiles, etc. Headset hanger feels loose and flimsy, but it isn't holding much weight so as long as you keep it closed when not in use and don't smash it, you should be fine.

Display: As expected. Very high quality, good color representation as far as I've seen. Personally, I value my eyes more than completely mind-numbing picture, so I keep the brightness as low as I can stand and the colors slightly skewed away from a full blue experience. Still looks fantastic.

Overall: Expensive but high quality. I probably should have bought the 24 inch. 27 is absolutely amazing, but I think I'd have been happy with 24 as long as I didn't try the 27 first. But it was a decent sale, and I'm not sad. Excited to get the rest of my rig to test the full strength.

I bought this monitor for video gaming, and it is performing very well for that. The screen is bright and the colors are vivid and well-balanced. I had no dead pixels and no problem with light bleed-through. On-screen adjustments are simple and it is easy to match the brightness and contrast of the screen to a changing environment. There is no visible ghosting and the fine detail makes video games more immersive.

On the down side, the vertical viewing angle is too small, so the monitor is either too light at the bottom or too dark at the top, no matter how I adjust it. Also, the monitor only supports the native resolution on my computer. At any other size, text is blurry. Even after adjustments, I find that text and icons are sometimes too small and internet picures and video are also very small. On the other hand, I can easily fit two documents or applications side-by-side on the wide screen.

I would recommend this monitor for students, gamers, and general home/office use. For graphic artists and other professionals, you will want to invest in something better, like an Eiso or a better Samsung with a PVA screen. Unfortunately, a better monitor will cost double or triple the price of this one, so this is a good value for a budget-minded purchaser.

I'm a web developer and have this monitor hooked up to a Macbook Pro 2018 15in running High Sierra 10.13.6. It has issues when my MBP goes into sleep mode (more below). Overall the image quality is too good to return for a problem like this though and I would recommend the monitor.

Plugged into the Monitor:
- External Keyboard
- Mouse (Bluetooth but drawing power)
- Speakers (drawing power)
- Webcam

The monitor then is connected to the MBP via one USB-C (thunderbolt) connector included with the monitor. The MBP draws power over this connection too so it's the only cable going into my MBP.

The picture quality is amazing, it's a close call but I'd say it's actually better looking than the retina display on the MBP. I can comfortably fit 3 files side by side with 120 character line length and still have room to display a 1050px wide webpage on the side.

Now for the downside. The reason I docked one star from this is because it's really poorly compatible with OSX. When the MBP goes to sleep, the monitor looks for signal for a little while, then goes into power saving mode as expected. But then in about 30 seconds to a minute the monitor will wake up, complain that there's no signal which prompts the MBP to wake up and say it's battery is fully charged before waking up fully.

This is a common problem with both OSX and the Dell monitor in particular and it seems to be related to how OSX handles the one cable solution. Dell support forums blame OSX and say they will not be supporting it. I'm hoping that OSX releases a patch that solves the issue because the monitor is really too nice to return for something like this. I personally have worked around this issue by making my MBP's time to go to sleep super short, so even if it wakes up it just immediately goes back to sleep.

So Buyer Beware, the monitor does not sleep well when used as a hub for the one cable solution. Otherwise, it's an amazing monitor.

This is a great television! Those of you who have been on the fence about this tv and have already done your research/homework should probably just move forward with your purchase as you will have some difficulty buying a new television this year at a better value.

I purchased this television from Amazon after returning a similarly priced/sized Samsung television to a "big-box" store several weeks prior. To be fair, the Samsung was an entirely different technology (edge-lit LED). However, the Samsung had significant issues with uniformity of brightness and contrast that I found to be bothersome in everyday viewing. This is less likely to be the case with plasma televisions as the result of the differences in technology. At the time of my Panasonic purchase, a number of initial reviews were starting to become public on third party sites such as CNET. In fact CNET gave this one of the best ratings ever given (it's first 5-star rating). In addition a number of reviews by those who had purchased the tv were available on Amazon and AVS Forum. Since that time, Home Theater magazine has rated this television a "top pick."

I would encourage those who wish to learn more about this tv to spend a bit of time reviewing these resources if they still have uncertainty regarding this television. Due to the broad reviewer set and now ownership base of this set, you should have excellent resources to explore the pros/cons of this television in particular. To be sure, this year's lineup of Panasonic televisions has been proven to be the best ever, especially across the high end, yet this is still the best value in a beautiful picture.

Those who really wish to refine their picture once they purchase this tv are encouraged to enlist some of the setting recommendations on AVS Forum (I used the well described settings by user "d-nice") or CNET (David Katzmaier as reviewer).

Pros:
1) Outstanding contrast and black levels
2) Uniform picture presentation and colors
3) Liquid picture quality
4) Great 2D performance
5) No evidence of burn-in (a concern with older generation plasma televisions) in my use
6) Good connectivity
7) Outstanding off-axis viewing with minimal glare (we use this in a very bright room with multiple adjacent windows)
8) Reasonable selection of integrated apps (Netflix, etc).

Cons:
1) This has not faired as well as other sets in 3D performance in most reviews
2) Gaming has been described to be a bit of an issue with a concern for "input lag" by some reviewers
3) Plasma televisions are generally heavier than their LED counterparts

Comments:
1) No "buzzing sound" which has been a concern with some prior plasmas
2) This is sometimes a difficult television to purchase based on availability/popularity
3) Amazon shipping was included for me as a "Prime Member," but based on your location delivery may take some time to occur
4) I've not used the optional "pen" or "camera" as we are primarily using this television for 2D viewing of High-Def content from various sources

My family does not use this at all for 3D viewing nor gaming, just for viewing of 2D high-def content. For this purpose, this television has produced one of the most beautiful pictures I've ever seen. It has been made much more enjoyable by the application of custom settings - which do take a bit of time to apply, but are worth the effort and are well described in the references above.

In addition to our lack of need for lots of 3D viewing and/or fancy gaming feedback, I also wish to point out that a number of future technologies may eventually surpass the picture currently produced by the best plasmas (including this television). These include a potential for OLED displays as well as the onset of 2K and 4K panels. While these future implementations of viewing are exciting to consider, my goal in this purchase was to buy a reasonably-priced television for my family that would produce outstanding picture quality with today's typical inputs (1080i DirecTV, 1080p Blue-Ray, and streaming technologies such as Apple TV and Netflix). With these factors in mind we have been more than satisfied.

The Dell S2716DG is the greatest TN panel you will find anywhere on the market. It matches IPS monitors without the typical problems that come with them. I nearly returned the monitor initially when I tested it with my MacBook. I couldn't believe how terrible and washed out the colors looked. It was an issue that wasn't truly rectifiable with the monitor's settings. I was one button away from returning the monitor which should be voted the best monitor of the generation, by the way.

I chose to stick it out until my rig arrived and I could manipulate the settings with the nVidia control panel. The result of my patience? An IPS-quality monitor that if sat next to an IPS monitor you'll not notice much of a difference if at all. And what makes it even greater? 144hz and GSYNC.

The settings you need to revise and achieve an IPS-quality picture without the pitfalls:

The Dell monitor's settings:
Brightness - 58
Contrast - 65
Color: R97, G95, B96

NVidia CP, all channels:
Brightness - 45
Contrast - 80
Gamma. 84
Color/Digital Vibrance - 40

I uploaded a picture showing my early 2015 MacBook Retina (one of the best IPS panels) next to the Dell S2716DG monitor. You'll see that they are virtually the same. The Macbook is only slightly brighter and a little more deeper in the colors but hardly noticeable to the untrained eye. I'd take that over an IPS panel which is always a gamble. Enjoy!

Hue - 0

Edit: I want to add that if by chance that the screen is too bright or contrasted, you're able to adjust the settings without losing picture quality. Tune the monitor brightness setting to 40%, and the nVidia CP brightness to 45% and contrast. The configuration I wrote above will get you to the IPS-quality picture, but from there you can adjust the brightness and contrast slightly to your liking.

Actually, I am now recommending setting nVidia brightness to 45 and contrast to 80 against the old recommendation of 60 and 100. The greys look washed out when using the old setting. 45 and 80 are best.

You can only adjust color in "Custom" mode. Gamut is un-clamped.

The VA panel did show image brightness variance as you moved your head around, something you don't get with IPS.

Setting the display to HDR via Windows 10 and attempting to adjust color shows the options grayed out. White point is wrong and you cannot fix it.

"Black Stabilizer" default of "50" appears to be the closest to the sRGB gamma curve. People suggesting setting it to "0" to get better blacks are getting crushed blacks.

After calibration for SDR while the charts are OK the image on screen looks "off," with banding accentuated more than a comparable calibrated SDR IPS panel.

Contrast ratio is good and my unit had decent black uniformity and little back-light bleed.

I guess you get what you pay for. If LG unlocked calibration for all modes this would be a great multi-purpose display for light content creation and "filthy causal" gaming.

It came boxed very well, with a really nice base and all the required hardware including a HDMI cable they threw in which I always appreciate from a seller or store.

This monitor is a solid monitor. I am going to give it four stars because it has really thin bezels which looks great, it has a really slim form factor for a good build that's made well. It is a monitor that will last and the slim small bezel design is really modern design. It's really a decent well built monitor right down the middle. I believe they also through in a HDMI cable! Great business to throw in accessories that are needed. I now use this on my vex mount as a second monitor for things like OBS if you're a streamer, gamer etc; or if I just need to focus via organization for maximum productivity. This monitor I really like that the monitor weighs next to nothing but also feels really sturdy in the hand.

A couple things I'd like to address or see change. However, first for anyone that is using vex mounts to attach this or maybe hanging it the monitor here are the measurements. SO, obviously it's going to be 22' diagonally then, it's just under 18' in across (length), and about 10.5' vertical and if using vex the plates are perfect (75mm-100mm) Next, to be fair this is a very basic unit and not much more and that's not always a bad thing; but being there is only one HDMI and one VGA port for outs, I would really like to see some basics like at least a second HDMI port for the price and especially a 3.5mm aux out/in. These two options( In my opinion) at the very least should be added for the price which I believe was close to $99 retail. It's an argument when for $25-50 more dollars you can get a 27" curved or flat with speakers , aux in?out and 2 HDMI ports and a VGA.

Keep in mind, if you don't use monitors a lot, most do not come with sound and this SkyWorthis no different, no sound. This is why aux cable would be very useful. in my case, what I did was get a VGA to HDMI adapter, for more options and a few other adapters depending how I will handle my visuals and audio. In anywise this SkyWorth 22" monitor is a really good buy.

(The things I listed are simply in my opinion what I would like to see change in 2023 for these monitors and in no way takes anything away from the quality of this monitor. its a great base monitor, especially for a novice user.)

I am giving a final score of four because of the design , thin bezels, and it will be a solid monitor that lasts a good while. It also has a good length power cable, at times they are just too short, especially when doing cable management. A few more things , gaming , I gave it a three stars... it's not bad for gaming at all the screen refreshes at 75hz @1080 which looks good on a smaller 22' monitor. I have no issue gaming on here thus far. The only other thing to be aware of is the picture quality. Well it's pretty darn good, I was not sure I was going to be able to say that. The picture as it comes is almost preset perfectly. However, a small nitpick, the monitor could use a better menu to adjust menu options. Unfortunately , that's very basic as well.

At the end of the day, this monitor is a well built design, I am loving the thin bezels! Those thin bezels make the monitor look bigger than what it is. It has a really nice modern design and is lightweight. The input/outputs are enough to get you started for sure and can and would recommend this SkyWorth monitor. The back of the monitor has a nice design to it as well. This is a great middle of the road $99 monitor, I just wish it had a few things, but nothing that would stop me from getting it as long as the price is right, which would for me would be on sale around $60-70. I have another 2015 HP monitor, the [[ASIN:B007JLEEXC HP 23" LV2311 Led Backlit Monitor]], but in all honesty I prefer this HP 22xw for a couple of reasons. I am using these products solely as IP camera monitors. They get more than 12 hours of use a day viewing video from high definition IP cameras. I found that this HP 22xw is a little clearer and a little brighter than the LV2311 and I appreciate the almost invisible bezel compared to most monitors. The tiny bezel is especially good at this price point. Although my LV2311 is a tad larger diagonally, I prefer the image on the HP 22xw.

The biggest drawback, and as long as you are aware of it and it's of no concern, is the fact that the 22xw does not have any VESA mount capability. You are limited to the stand that comes with the product. I read that someone commented that they thought the included stand was flimsy. However, I wouldn't say the stand is flimsy in any way. HP attempted to produce a stand that is unique and a little different in design, but I wouldn't call it flimsy. Yes, it is not very adjustable since you can't really move it up and down, but it is strong enough to hold the monitor. The white stand and bezel are very Apple-esque.

As long as you are fine with the stand and only using the stand, this is a monitor with a beautiful image as a very good price point. It is suitable to everyday computing tasks, but probably not the right choice for gamers, artists or those seeking a very high-resolution display.

The Monitor came with a variety of cables. HDMI, DVI, and VGA. The Power cable was somewhat perplexing at first. It appears that the monitor has a small external power supply and a connection type I have never seen on a monitor. The external power supply is fine, however I find I do not like when manufacturers build a power supply into a cable to move it away from the root device. It would be nice if they had figured out a way to incorporate it into the monitor itself but that's not a deal breaker.

If you need a new monitor or just want something better for a budget price, this is a solid choice.

When compared to the VP2770's as part of the common professional-grade display line from Viewsonic, this model lacks HDMI inputs and Display Port inputs limiting its color pallet capabilities i.e. no studio color models but full RGB, sRGB. Visually the IPS display quality is very good as should be expected from a display commanding higher price points than standard TN LED displays. The included stand is fully adjustable which also includes positioning for Portrait mode along with hydraulically assisted height and finally fully adjustable tilt. Additionally the monitor driver though available with enough digging is not a Windows 8 signed version, again some simple workarounds solved this issue.

Disappointments -

Arcane Buttons for accessing panel functions.
One of two units has an intermittent power LED which does not affect function or display quality but is annoying. A workaround is available via the controls to disable the LED, however this only turns the function off after post.

For a 23' LED display in this price range, many do include an HDMI and Display Port input such as the VP2770 model which includes everything and possibly can notify NORAD of an impending air raid.

In both cases the USB ports are located in the input recess panel and effectively serve best as places to connect USB keyboards, Mice or speakers as they are not readily accessible.

If you are in the market for a professional-grade panel, you may want to evaluate displays offering more input flexibility and modern control surfaces.

I have been looking for a second monitor for my MacBook Pro. I have been using my iPad as a second monitor through the Sidecar function, but it is glitchy, and the screen is a bit small, so I wanted something larger, simpler to use, and equally portable. This seemed like an excellent option all around.

I'll start with what I like. It is unbelievably light and portable. It comes with a cover that both protects the monitor and acts as a stand. I expected to not use it as a stand, but it actually works really well and gives multiple options for the angle that it sits at. It is also a really nice size, and it is extremely simple to use with the MacBook. It is truly plug-and-play with almost no delay from plugging it in to using it.

However, the cons kind of outweigh the pros. The screen is very dark, even with the brightness bumped up all the way. I keep my MacBook brightness down to about 80%, and it is at least twice as bright as this monitor at 100%. Also, side-by-side with a MacBook, the monitor is clearly lacking in not only brightness, but definition, so it is a stark difference between the two. Also, the menu is somewhat finicky to navigate, but you get used to it eventually, and fortunately, once you've dialed everything in, you don't have to use the menu buttons very much.

But, the biggest issue for me, is that the monitor is very difficult to see at any angle besides directly straight-on, which is a major problem if you are planning to use it as a second monitor for a laptop. I'm finding that I am keeping my email on the second monitor, and then dragging it over to the main screen if I want to read it. This really defeats the purpose of the second monitor, and unfortunately, it means that I am going to switch back to using my iPad as a second monitor. It may work really well if, for example, you wanted to use it to mirror your screen so others can look at it. They can easily hold it or move it around as needed, so it could really be useful in that regard. But, for a second monitor, as it is advertised to do, it is pretty lacking.

I wish I could say something better for it because I really like the concept, but unfortunately, this just doesn't work great for me.

I bought this monitor from Acer Recertified. I believe that being recertified means that someone else bought it, found something wrong with it, and probably got a refund. It came from the Acer Recertified facility in Texas. I ran into two issues. One was a slight flicker I noticed while viewing Corsair's iCUE software with Gsync enabled. I also noticed some flicker while a few of my games were loading. The other issue is that there are two or three light spots near the top on the screen, which are only visible when that part of the screen is black. I sent the monitor in for repair, but the technicians could not reproduced my issues. I was offered a refund or I could give more details to help the technicians figure out what is wrong with the monitor. I declined the refund and gave them a more detailed report of the issues. I wanted this fixed or replaced. I didn’t want someone else to buy it and have to go through the same issues. It was sent back to me as they couldn’t find anything wrong with it. Unfortunately, issues are still there. I have no idea how they could have overlooked this. I am very disappointed. So, be careful, it’s possible you’ll buy a product that is defective and technicians won’t fix it.

*Edit 9/12/19* Maybe this monitor deserves more stars, but I can't get over the fact that the Acer Recertified techs could not see the issues I was having. I may have paid less than $300 for this, but I still can't live with spending so much on a defective unit. I'm getting a refund and buying a different monitor (not from Acer). I've include a picture of two spots that I took with my phone. I edited them in Paint and circled them in red. These spots are visible as soon as the monitor is turned on, you don't even have to plug in a display cable to see them. I tried different power cables and outlets in different areas of my room with the same result. The techs said it was probably some kind of interference. I thought this could be true, as my speakers are behind and beside my monitor, but the Dell monitor that came with my Alienware PC years ago doesn't have these issues.

OK, first of all, I'm using this for both gaming and regular PC use. I have very light-sensitive eyes, so I'm really happy with the Dell Display Manager software which can be set to automatically detect what you're doing and adjust the monitor's brightness, saturation, gamma, and contrast for you, so you aren't fiddling with monitor controls all the time. This means when I'm surfing the web, it's darker (because I can't handle the bright white web pages), and then when I switch to a video or game, it gets brighter and the contrast is adjusted automatically to give the best quality image. This is a fantastic feature.

Let's talk about the built-in monitor controls for a moment, though. Dell was smart enough to make the monitor buttons create an on-screen display showing you which buttons to press to get the desire result. No more squinting at tiny letters or symbols, or fidgeting with buttons trying to figure out which one does what. Adjusting the Dell monitor is a breeze!

This doesn't have a DisplayPort on it, which I consider a non-issue. I do have DisplayPort support on my video card, but honestly I think HDMI is just fine for a 1080p image , which is all this monitor can handle. There's no 4K resolution support for this model, but that's fine. Gaming in 4K is nice but setting to 1080p means a higher frame rate and less work for my video card, so I'm fine with HD. Besides, Windows does weird things with fonts and window sizing in 4K, and this is just easier to deal with for everyday Windows computing.

Of course, if you're still rocking the old school VGA connection you're still covered here. Dell hasn't abandoned it yet. However, you should be aware that I tried an HDMI 2.0 cable on this and it balked at detecting my PC. I had to switch to an older HDMI cable to get any results.

This monitor does not have built in speakers, so you'll need to consider that when setting up. However, it is a really nice IPS panel that has a super-thin bezel. It's all screen, with the tiniest black border, and that's nice. It maximizes desktop real estate while still giving you a very large true 27" diagonal picture.

Colors, contrast, sharpness, and white levels are all excellent. No complaints whatsoever regarding image quality. There's no ghosting or lag, either, so gaming works quite well.

Bottom line: This is an excellent 1080p IPS monitor with an intuitive software companion app and full support for HDMI and VGA. Five stars.

All the good things everyone says about this monitor are true; it's a beautiful display. Unfortunately, this model still appears to have some *serious* quality control issues.

I received one from Amazon, but it had horrible backlight bleed and discolouring. Alright, that's fine, I understand that not every display will be perfect; sometimes you get unlucky. So, I sent it back and got a replacement. Trouble is, the replacement has discoloring and bleed just as bad, and also has a faint but noticeable crosshatch pattern covering the entire screen (a defect in the anti-glare coating, apparently). At this point, Amazon offered me a refund, but flatly refused to send me another replacement. I'm disappointed with Dell's quality control. Amazon was good about accepting the returns, and not charging me anything, but they weren't willing to keep working with me to try to find a unit that wasn't defective. It's really too bad; it would be a beautiful display if it wasn't marred by these defects.

Supposedly, Dell is aware of these issues, but is still shipping the old units which are known to have a high defect rate (this one was manufactured in October 2012). If you order one of these, I wish you the best of luck; it appears you'll need it. It may still be a good buy, but be prepared to go through several replacements if you have to.

[UPDATE:] After the two monitors I went through with Amazon, I tried ordering this monitor directly from Dell (since they had a total satisfaction guarantee, you could send back a monitor for any reason). The first one I received from Dell had the same problems as the second one I got from Amazon. I then called Dell customer care on the phone, and told them about my experience; I asked them to hand-pick me a monitor, and have someone inspect it before sending it to me during the RMA process. I think they actually did have someone look at it, as it took them nearly a week longer than normal to ship it to me, and when I got it it had much better uniformity (less clouding and bleed, though there was still a little). Despite being hand-picked and inspected, however, this monitor (the fourth!) still had the pronounced crosshatch pattern. I sent it back, got a refund, and after some more research settled on a Viewsonic VP2770-LED instead. The first VP2770 I got had several dead pixels, so it too was RMA'd, but the second one I got has been fine. In terms of visual quality (color, contrast, etc.), it is nearly identical to the Dell product, but both VP2770s had much better uniformity than the Dells. I actually prefer the OSD and the stand that come on the U2713HM; the VP2770 stand doesn't have quite as large a range of motion, but it's VESA-mount compatible, so I wound up getting a mounting-arm. The OSD on the VP2770 is adequate, but is just not quite as convenient as the one on the U2713HM. Price-wise, they're pretty similar (it depends on which one is on sale where).

All in all, I really can't recommend the Dell U2713HM to anyone; until they make a major change to their manufacturing process, this is a product to avoid if you have sensitive eyes. If four monitors in a row, including the hand-picked and inspected one, had these problems, it's not just quality issue. Wait until the revision number on these gets a bump, then check the reviews carefully.

I rely heavily on Amazon reviews to make purchasing decisions. I gave up my Consumer Reports subscription because they only review a smattering of products, and never ones that seem to be actually for sale, so you have to infer and hope and put a lot of faith into the brand. On Amazon, people review the thing you can then add to your cart. Except people seem to have lost track of the stars between one and five. There is a tendency from reviewers to give one star if the product in anyway fails to live up to expectations. Or five in what sometimes seems to be an effort to stave off buyers remorse. I always gravitate towards the negative reviews as they will find the pitfalls, and I can decide weather or not I can live with the shortcomings. This monitor is not perfect, but the price is right, and it works well for my purposes.

Many of the things mentioned in the one star reviews are indeed valid. Out of the box, the brightness is cranked up to 100%. The "color magic" features are about useless. It's taken me a couple hours to dial in an acceptable color profile, and it's still too saturated. The shiny black bezel is a problem. I'm using the monitor with a MacBook Pro. On a Mac, the menus are always at the very top of the screen, of course, and the menu titles get reflected in the shiny plastic! The shiny plastic picks up reflections from the window behind me. When my desk wiggles, the reflection in the monitor wiggles and the motion in my periphery has made me think it is one of the icons jumping in the Mac's dock. Note to Samsung: shiny black bezel = bad idea.

My monitor was made in Mexico. Some of the reviews seem to imply this is a bad thing, but I haven't had any of the problems described. Perhaps by 2008 any quality control issues coming out of the Mexico factory have been resolved?

The viewing angle is indeed very narrow, but that seems exacerbated in zero ambient light situations (i.e. a darkened room). In ordinary office lighting the color shift from side to side seems to recede to practically unnoticeable. The color shift has also diminished over time, so maybe burn-in is a factor, or I've just gotten used to it in a week and a half.

The manual, if you can call it that, that came with this is absolutely the worst I have ever seen. It's in about 10 different languages with one line in each section in given to a particular language. The images are shared and labeled with numbers. It gave me a headache trying to parse out just the English from the jumble. There is no documentation on the magic features, so my bad luck with them may be because I wasn't using them properly.

That said, I love this monitor. I love having 1920 x 1200 bright, sharp pixels to lay out multiple windows side by side by side in. It hooked up to my MacBook's DVI port without fuss and it was recognized immediately. It also works fine with the MacBook under Boot Camp running Windows XP. I bought this monitor because I needed a lot of affordable, decent quality screen real estate. It fits the bill.

The monitor doesn't have a traditional power plug so can't use an old monitor's 120v power cable. It requires a power supply brick. This is the first LED monitor for me and unfortunately it is far from my old Dell 2407WFP in terms of eye comfort (to be fair, I should say Dell was 4 times more expensive when purchased 3 years ago). I bought ViewSonic VX2250WM-LED for my son as a second monitor to use with a laptop in college dorm.

Pros:
1. Lightweight and slim
2. Bright (almost too bright for anything, but watching movies)
3. Sharp
4. Price is hard to beat
5. Looks very nice on your desk

Cons:
1. Hard to adjust using monitor controls. Windows 7 Display Color Calibration wizard helped a bit: I had to increase gamma (move Win 7 gamma calibration slider almost all the way to the bottom) and make some color adjustments. Still, ViewSonic LED was not as comfortable and natural as my 24 inch Dell (I have three of them and never had do adjust anything, except for lowering brightness to 25%). Again, to be fair, I did not install the included software, assuming that any ViewSonic-specific adjustments could break compatibility with my other monitors
2. Colors and contrast change significantly with the viewing angle, so if you need a monitor for any serious art work look elsewhere
3. Too sharp. For many people this is a pro (see above) but I prefer a slightly "fuzzier" look produced by Dell
4. Dynamic contrast is not very useful because the overall brightness jumps up and down as the picture changes

I am not sure if the monitor was defective or I was used more to LCD (vs LED) panel. I wanted to return the monitor and spend 20-30% more money on a different brand but my son said it was OK for him to use
